sessions.title: Recharge Room
sessions.description: Need a spot to decompress, take a load off, get your bearings or recharge your batteries? This is the room for you!

sessions.title: Registration Hours
sessions.description: Pick up your badge or register on-site: 7:30 a.m. - 6:30 p.m.

sessions.title: Adjacent Event: Journalists Recovery Network
sessions.description: The Journalists Recovery Network will meet and offer a sober space for journalists experiencing troubles with alcohol or substance use. This is an informal meeting for individuals who may struggle with substance use to meet our members and chat more about our organization. Feel free to reach out to us at journalistsrecoverynetwork@gmail.com.

sessions.title: Welcome to IRE26 + first-timers networking
sessions.description: Welcome to the conference! Hear about tips and tactics to navigate our conference like a pro. Then, stick around for a speed round of networking with fellow attendees and IRE staff and board.

sessions.title: Exhibitors
sessions.description: Stop by and visit with the exhibitors and recruiters.

sessions.title: Front-line leadership: How newsrooms manage risk, safety, and law enforcement coverage
sessions.description:
Newsroom leaders from cities experiencing heightened law-enforcement activity will discuss how they are assessing risk and fulfilling their responsibility to protect journalists in the field.
Drawing on real newsroom decision-making, panelists will explore how they prepare reporters for volatile assignments, establish safety protocols, respond to emergencies, and balance editorial imperatives with physical and legal risk. They’ll also discuss internal communication, legal coordination, community relationships, and how leadership decisions before and during an assignment directly affect reporter safety. This moderated discussion, led by the International Women’s Media Foundation (IWMF), will offer practical guidance for editors and managers navigating unpredictable reporting conditions — with candid reflections on what has worked, what hasn’t, and what newsrooms must do differently going forward.

sessions.title: Let’s get personal: Turning congressional financial disclosures into exclusive  stories
sessions.description: Congressional lawmakers and candidates are required to file annual financial disclosures and report their stock transactions. These disclosures are a wealth of stories, as the speakers on this panel have proven. Attendees can expect to learn how to access financial disclosures, how to read them and how to use them to craft compelling articles that break news. You’ll also learn how to identify lawmakers trading stocks in industries where they might have inside information, violating the STOCK Act and padding the vast wealth gap between themselves and their constituents. Such stories are especially timely as momentum builds on Capitol Hill to ban members of Congress and their families from trading and owning stocks.

sessions.title: 60 years of frustration and fun: Happy birthday, FOIA!
sessions.description: This July marks the 60th anniversary of the signing of the U.S. Freedom of Information Act, and while not perfect, it has yielded amazing stories. This panel will run through the “best of” stories birthed because of FOIA, providing inspiration and story ideas for further requests.

sessions.title: Following the money in college sports’ new era
sessions.description: College athletics has changed faster in the past year than the previous five decades, with a whole raft of new rules and players, from private equity to President Trump. We’ll help you navigate the many​ reform-minded proposals, as well as discuss the unintended consequences such as eliminating sports, the potential impact on the U.S. Olympic team and what today’s college athlete is now facing. Panelists will also explore current and emerging financial trends in the industry, highlighting issues that journalists across the country should be monitoring, including the $1B in severance paid to get coaches NOT to coach. We'll cover rising student fees, challenges to equity and new athlete revenue-sharing payments. We’ll point you to resources, public records and key areas to track at your school.

sessions.title: Show & Tell: Students, MMJs, Early-Career Journalists 1
sessions.description: Sign up to “Show & Tell” us about your best work and get constructive feedback from the moderator and audience. Sign-ups are required for presenters. Each presenter will have 15 minutes to share their work and receive feedback. All are welcome to attend!

sessions.title: Investigating civil rights rollbacks
How to organize your reporting (and your life)
sessions.description: It doesn’t matter how many killer quotes you have if you can’t find them in your notebook. Organization is an under-appreciated skill in investigations and beat reporting. In this panel, four veteran reporters share their secrets, including organizing with everything from sticky notes to AI.

sessions.title: From Boring to blockbuster: Tricks and tools to up your visual game
sessions.description: When doing a story with hard facts and heavy data, brining an investigative story to life can sometimes be a challenge. A challenge yes...but not impossible! In this session, we'll share how you can make a non-visual story come to life. From framing interviews to color grading, graphics, and editing. Photojournalists, including NPPA leaders, will share how you can level up your visual game in an interesting, different, and creative way.

sessions.title: Writing the investigative narrative
sessions.description:
Learn how to make your investigative writing compelling and powerful. We cover everything from defining an investigative narrative to how to report, organize, write and self-edit your own!

Topics include:
Understanding the investigative narrative
How to pitch
Turning sources into characters
Writing a scene
Self-editing tips and techniques
Organizing and writing a deeply reported investigative narrative

Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.

sessions.title: SOLD OUT - Investigative skills: A master class
sessions.description:
In this hands-on, tactical workshop using real case studies, we'll discuss investigative strategies to gather the information and sources needed to deliver a revelatory investigative piece -- in short, just about everything. How do you get started? How do you manage your progress to ensure your investigation is as targeted as possible? What tactics increase the likelihood of success?
	
We'll talk about how to obtain the best publicly available databases to supercharge your reporting. We'll spend time on FOIA tactics, such as "threading" and "piggybacking," and how to stay organized when the records start rolling in. And we'll discuss the importance of building a chronology and other organizational tactics, including the use of AI tools such as Google Notebook and Gemini, that allow you to see into your own files and what other folks have already discovered.

Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.

sessions.title: Data visualization for non-designers
sessions.description:
There are many tools developed in recent years that allow anyone with a basic grasp of spreadsheets and a browser to make beautiful and publication-ready data visualizations. This session will show attendees how to create storytelling infographics and visualizations for their stories using off-the-shelf tools like Datawrapper, Flourish Studio and others. 

Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: Intro to data mapping: Getting started with QGIS
sessions.description:
Learn the basics of mapping analysis with examples from award-winning journalist and educator Andy Lehren. Leave with ideas to hit the ground running in your own newsroom. Data track; bring your own laptop. Seated is limited; laptop prep is required. Please fill out this form to get the necessary information.

Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: Leading past crisis: change, culture, and community
sessions.description:
"Crisis” is a word we use constantly, probably too much, to describe the work we do. For many newsroom leaders, it has become an unfortunate and permanent state of being.
Over the past year, colleagues have shared reflections like these: trying to fix what feels broken while enduring what’s out of their control; leading teams with fewer resources, more pressure, and less autonomy; working days that begin before sunrise and end long after dinner; uncertainty about whether the industry they know will still be there at the end of their careers; and a growing tension between loving the mission and purpose of journalism while questioning whether the work still loves them back. Too many wonder if the time has come to do something easier that pays more.

Together, we’ll begin to tackle the visible challenges of leadership— how we can guide our team through change, managing our time and workload, delegating what we can and planning what we can’t; developing our team and holding them accountable for the work, and building a sustainable culture of excellence—while also making space for the less visible strains — anxiety, self-doubt, isolation, the emotional weight of responsibility and, laughably, having a life outside of work.

And… this will not be hours of “woe is me.” If you’re still practicing the craft, serving your community, then you still believe in the strength and power of journalism. This deep dive will focus on optimism, honesty, and practical, tangible leadership skills—offering coaching, peer learning, and clear priorities for what to do next, how to care for yourself, and how to continue serving your community with purpose.

Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.

sessions.title: Audio Coaching 1:1
sessions.description: Sign up for 30 minutes with an audio pro to use however you like: Bring tape for a critique, a pitch for workshopping or your questions about surviving and thriving in the audio journalism space.

sessions.title: Barry Nash and Company One-on-One Coaching
sessions.description:
Barry Nash and his team return to IRE to provide personalized coaching sessions. If you have questions about any aspect of your delivery — your appearance, how you use your voice, how you can perform most effectively in the field or in the studio — this is an opportunity you won’t want to miss. Barry has been working with talent at all levels of experience and ability for over 30 years. He currently authors the only blog in the world focused exclusively on television news and sports performance. You can find it on Substack
Please note: Participants should bring recent footage on a flash drive, webpage/online link, or storage drive (Google or Dropbox).                                                                                                                            Sign up for a time slot here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C0445A9AD28A5FEC34-64383836-irecoaching#/

sessions.title: 15 min break
Do try this at home: Tools and tips to investigate Washington from anywhere
sessions.description: What happens in Washington doesn't stay in Washington. We'll provide a treasure trove of OSINT, digital tools, reporting strategies and IRE resources to help you (quickly and cheaply) follow the investigative trail from Washington to your community and back.

sessions.title: After the cut: Surviving and rebuilding investigative careers after buyouts and layoffs
sessions.description:
Day One: Don’t panic. Day Two: Sign out of your socials. Day Three: Make a To Do List.
Buyouts and layoffs have become a recurring reality in investigative journalism, often disrupting long-term projects, professional identity, and financial stability. This session focuses on how investigative reporters can strategically manage the aftermath—professionally, practically, and psychologically—while continuing to do accountability work.
Panelists will share concrete strategies for protecting investigations in progress, preserving sources and data, and navigating legal and ethical obligations during transitions. The session will explore career pathways beyond traditional newsrooms, including nonprofit investigative outlets, collaborations, fellowships, freelancing, teaching, and entrepreneurial models.
Attendees will also share their experiences negotiating exits, understanding severance and noncompete clauses, rebuilding income streams, and maintaining investigative rigor outside legacy structures. Grounded in real experiences, this session aims to provide investigative journalists with tools not just to endure industry upheaval, but to adapt and continue producing impactful work.

sessions.title: Following the money: Crypto tracing for business and crime reporting
sessions.description:
Criminals, sanctions-evaders and corrupt actors are increasingly turning to the alternate financial system of cryptocurrency to discreetly move money. A contradiction exists at the heart of crypto: The technology offers users deep anonymity, but it also records key details of most transactions in a public ledger known as the blockchain. With the right tools, analyzing these public records can lead to big reporting breaks. 

This session will walk participants through the specific methodologies behind ICIJ's year-long Coin Laundry investigation, which showed how the world's largest crypto firms have profited of off scams, hacks and other crimes. 

The session will be targeted toward a wide audience. Previous experience in crypto investigations will not be necessary. The session can be broken into three general sections.

First: Crypto 101. We'll first walk participants through some 101 basics. What is cryptocurrency? What is a crypto currency address? What are blockchains and what are the meaningful differences for reporters between the blockchains? 

Second: How to gather basic crypto intel. Here, we'll show the crowd how ICIJ identified cryptocurrency addresses tied to individuals, crime groups or companies we were interested in covering. We will show participants where to look for illicit cryptocurrency addresses and how to verify the accuracy of crypto address ownership. Here, we will introduce participants to the excellent tool Arkham Intelligence. We’ll show participants how to use this tool and also discuss its limitations. 

Third, How to start tracing crypto transactions. Here we'll show participants how, once they have identified a cryptocurrency address of interest, to begin simple tracing using open-source tools. A key part of this is using primary source material to fact-check the tracing. As part of this process, it's important to walk participants through basic points on how mainstream crypto exchanges work. 

This will include a brief discussion on how to leverage APIs of open source blockchain explorers like Arkham Intelligence and Tronscan to get solid analysis. At this point in the presentation, I can invite participants to open their laptops and do a simple cryptocurrency trace using Arkham Intelligence, and then perform a simple fact-check of Arkham's tracing results. Participants can also bring cryptocurrency addresses they are interested in examining to the panel to look at in real-time. (I am pitching this as a panel but it could likely be done in workshop format as well.) 

Finally, we'll have a few closing comments on best practices we learned around having experts help validate and expand on our crypto tracing.

sessions.title: Fields of evidence: Data-driven investigations into pesticide exposure
sessions.description:
Pesticides are sprayed across millions of acres every year—but the impacts on nearby communities are often hidden in fragmented data, scattered regulations, and hard-to-reach sources.

Reporters will share how they tracked pesticide use, mapped potential cancer and Parkinson's clusters, and connected environmental exposure to public health risks in farming communities. Panelists will discuss the datasets that make these investigations possible, strategies for finding agricultural sources in tight-lipped farming communities, and ways to turn complex environmental data into compelling visual and narrative storytelling.

sessions.title: OPEN MIC: News director forum, what keeps us up at night
sessions.description:
A lively discussion (and support group) for Newsroom Managers to share your reality check on the evolution of television news. 

From navigating budget cuts and layoffs, to journalist safety, pivoting to new platforms, challenges finding qualified talent, what you're looking for in your next hire, and everything in between. Come join us to share your thoughts!

This session will not be recorded.

sessions.title: Getting hostile sources to talk
sessions.description: How do you get someone to talk when they definitely don't want to talk to you? Perhaps they're the target of your investigation. Or perhaps they're afraid they'll lose their job, their credibility or maybe even their life. ESPN investigative reporter Tisha Thompson will moderate this special session with retired IRS Special Agent Chris Seymour on how to get hostile sources to talk. He will share his tips and techniques on how to secure the interview, what to do when you know they're lying, and other challenging situations he's successfully navigated with all types of sources -- including celebrities, athletes, con artists, witnesses and victims -- to secure dozens of criminal convictions over the course of his three-decade career.

sessions.title: Best practices in AI-assisted data journalism
sessions.description: From vibe coding to error message review and methodology design, we'll discuss some ideas and real-life examples on how data journalists can use AI tools effectively and ethically. Data journalists have navigated a series of technological innovations since the first NICAR conference in the early 1990s. How will we deal with the AI revolution? What’s working now and what’s likely to help us or bite us in the future? What are some guardrails and best practices? You'll learn how to use AI models to ingest, annotate and summarize large volumes of unstructured data, how to use AI to debug and generate code, and how to bring that work in line with ethical and accuracy standards. And we'll talk about some of the things that are best left to natural intelligence (humans).

sessions.title: Behind the PR wall: An off-the-record chat
sessions.description:
A candid conversation with leading current and former Public Information Officers from the federal government & private sector. They'll give the inside scoop on forging relationships, getting behind the PR wall and securing the responses & interviews you need for your stories 

**To allow our panelists to be candid, this session will be OFF THE RECORD - NO RECORDING/SOCIAL MEDIA POSTING**

Submit your questions for our experts here: https://forms.gle/BuzK7oPfGszWAeTi7

sessions.title: Finding stories in IRS Filings
sessions.description: In this training, journalists will learn how to find and read various IRS filings from 990s to 1023s, and how to identify potential stories within them. Attendees will be given concrete examples of stories that came through the analysis of IRS filings, and an opportunity to explore future story ideas. Bring questions! Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: Demo: The Gun Violence Data Hub
sessions.description:
Learn about the Gun Violence Data Hub, an initiative from The Trace, and how to use the data to report deeper and better stories in your own community. 

The Data Hub provides resources for newsrooms to bolster their U.S. gun violence knowledge, as well as an open data library of contextualized and well-documented datasets.

This session is good for those with some experience using spreadsheets. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: 15 min break
Don't outsource your instincts: Traditional research skills matter more in the AI era
sessions.description: The latest tips to upgrade your traditional search skills to responsibly and ethically incorporate AI tools into your investigative research and news-gathering process. Deepen your search skills, don't outsource and lose them!

sessions.title: Information session: Today's News for Tomorrow
sessions.description: In this session, speakers will answer questions about Today's News for Tomorrow, a collaboration between the Internet Archive, Investigative Reporters and Editors and the Poynter Institute, with support from Press Forward. The program offers newsrooms free training on digital preservation and sustained support to ensure the long-term preservation and access of their digital assets.

sessions.title: How to find immigration stories in your community
sessions.description: Immigration reporting goes so far beyond coverage of the border and immigration court, but too often tips focus on national scope. Many of the biggest immigration stories during the last year started locally, and local reporters who know the basics of how immigration systems work in the communities they cover are poised to tell the best version of these stories. This panel will cover the basics that local reporters who want to tell immigration stories in their community should know – establishing agency contacts, understanding the detention and deportation pathway in your area, leveraging hyperlocal networks tracking immigration arrests, how to comb for location-specific data from detentions to courts and how to build sources to tell immigration stories that touch arts, science, health, sports and more.

sessions.title: Investigating the impacts of the oil and gas industry
sessions.description: Learn from reporters covering the impacts of the oil and gas industry in local communities around the country. Fracking technology has allowed oil and gas production in the United States to reach record highs. But as new environmental problems emerge, the oil and gas industry is also contending with the side effects of aging, dilapidated infrastructure. In this session we will share reporting tips from reporters working in Oklahoma, Texas, Ohio and Pennsylvania to humanize the oil and gas story. They will share how to substantiate claims of environmental and health harms, where to find data sets chock-full of stories, and how to track oil and gas waste streams. We will discuss the impacts of deregulation under the Trump administration and how oil and gas accountability reporting can reach audiences across the political divide. We will also discuss how the Iran war is impacting the U.S. oil and gas industry.

sessions.title: Protecting yourself and sources in your raw tape
sessions.description: When you're collecting hours and hours of tape with sources who need varied levels of protection, having a good organizational system matters more than ever. We'll talk about why you might not want to keep your tape forever, how to think about using transcription and other services on the cloud, and other issues that come up with sound and video recording.

sessions.title: Show and Tell - Anything Goes - No specific topic
sessions.description: The time will be Thursday, June 18th from 11:30am to 12:30 pm

sessions.title: AMA for high performers
sessions.description: Feeling stuck, undervalued, or out of alignment in your career? You’re not alone...and you don’t have to figure it out on your own either. Join us for an open, no-BS conversation about what it takes to navigate change with clarity, courage, and self-respect. Whether you’re negotiating a raise, plotting an exit, or redefining what success means to you, this session is a space to ask questions, get grounded, and walk away with real tools for reclaiming your career on your terms.

sessions.title: Networking: Students
sessions.description: Mix and mingle with peers from throughout the world to find solidarity, make new friends, and build your professional community in this fun and informal networking session. This session is for students.

sessions.title: The art of the on-camera interview: viral accountability, "non"-ambushes & getting the big get
sessions.description:
One of us went viral, one of us was attacked, and one was doxed and tailed… and that's just the beginning. It's the art of the interview panel you won't forget.

Anyone can ask questions. But doing it on camera — and not going viral in a bad way — is another skill entirely.

Whether it's a viral political interview, an unexpected confrontation, a hard-won accountability interview, or a deeply emotional conversation, television interviews require preparation, instincts, and the ability to react in real time.

Join investigative journalists Julie Watts, David Hammer, and Allya Davidson for a real-world, play-by-play breakdown of the interviews you've likely seen — or should see.

Because in television, the interview isn't just about the answers. It's about what happens when the camera is rolling.

sessions.title: Investigate like the AP: Using Everlaw legal tools in news
sessions.description:
Aaron Kessler, data and investigative journalist at the Associated Press, talks about how his team uses Everlaw in document-heavy investigations.

And see how Everlaw's document review and discovery platform can help reporters dig deeper into large document sets, understand data, surface insights, and streamline their investigations to chart a straighter path to the truth. Learn more at: https://www.everlaw.com/forgood/

sessions.title: Basic campaign finance for the 2026 midterms
sessions.description:
With the midterms coming up this year, there are more opportunities than ever to investigate local, state and federal campaign finances. 

In this session, we'll practice navigating FEC filings, finding key figures and turning them into compelling stories. We'll also review common pitfalls in FEC data, such as how to avoid double-counting pass-through PAC contributions and how to classify in-kind contributions (and what those are!).

Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: AT CAPACITY - Digging into archives
sessions.description:
Archives can be goldmines for investigations. We'll head to DC to visit the National Archives and Records Administration's Research Center to learn about its collections, which include military records, land titles, photos, maps and more.  

Meet in the hotel lobby by 12:20 p.m. if you'd like to join the group. Transportation will not be provided.

This field trip will take about 2 hours, including transportation.

sessions.title: Lunch
Lunch Bunch
sessions.description: Make some new friends over lunch! Show up and we'll connect you with other folks who want to chat and network over the lunch break -- a great way to make a big conference feel smaller.

sessions.title: Adjacent Event: Snack Break - Explore AI Accountability Investigations with the Pulitzer Center
sessions.description: What are the ways AI may intersect with your reporting, whether it be algorithmic influence on government decisions, environmental impact, or simply lots of hype around time and cost savings? How might the Pulitzer Center’s training and funding opportunities support your dream reporting project connected to the accountability of AI systems and companies?                                                           Join Pulitzer Center’s Executive Editor Marina Walker Guevara and Steve Sapienza, Senior Editor, U.S. News Partnerships, and 2022 AI Accountability fellow Lam Thuy Vo for networking and a casual discussion exploring these questions. Coffee and light snacks will be provided!

sessions.title: IRE Board Meeting
sessions.description: Open to all IRE members.

sessions.title: Deep dive: Two podcasters talk about telling the unforgettable story
sessions.description: Julie Snyder and Dan Taberski are two of the country’s best-known podcasters. Snyder co-founded Serial, and Serial invented a form, launching the narrative podcast boom. Serial hit the country like a thunderclap. Taberski has made so many poignant , mind-bending podcasts (“Missing Richard Simmons,” “The Line,” “9/12,” “Hysterical”) that The New Yorker has turned his name into an adjective: “Taberskian.” They’ll talk about how to tell a story that no one will forget.

sessions.title: Everything you want to know about tracking political influence and money in politics
sessions.description: From dark money networks and corporate influence campaigns to billionaire mega-donors and grassroots fundraising, money in politics touches almost every beat. Panelists with decades of combined experience tracking campaign finances, nonprofit spending and political influence walk through how they actually find the information that drives these stories. Journalists will learn how to piece together opaque spending networks and uncover crucial details about operations through tax returns, digital ad data, corporate records, lobbying disclosures and other sources.

sessions.title: Backgrounding people in an hour
sessions.description: Whether covering a mass shooting or a political scandal, reporters need to know how to thoroughly and quickly background people and companies involved in breaking news events and long-term investigations. In this panel, journalists will share concrete tips on how to mine social media accounts, maximize Nexis searches, unearth public records, find friends and relatives and turn hunches into distinctive stories.

sessions.title: Data deluge: The education beat awaits your investigation
sessions.description:
Education is more than test scores, spelling bees and principal appointments. One of the largest line items in state budgets, the education beat has a trove of data at the local, state and federal level — from suspension and seclusion rates, demographic data and funding disparities, to civil rights challenges and special education settlements with NDAs. There is a story in your school district — you just have to find it.
Education stories are easily accessible if you know the tools.

sessions.title: Concentrated power: Covering the Growing Corporate Influence
sessions.description: Companies are getting bigger, more consolidated, and increasingly powerful, helping drive pricing and policies impacting every aspect of our lives. During this panel, learn how to probe more deeply into corporate actions and relevant relationships (including ownership structures and private equity), how companies take advantage of generous economic development incentives, reporting on dark money-backed plans at the state level for 2026 and beyond, and walk away with new tools to better investigate company behavior, even on deadline.

sessions.title: Video show & tell: Anything goes with Laura Geller and Matt Goldberg
sessions.description:
Sign up to “Show & Tell” us about your best work and get constructive feedback from the moderator and audience.

Sign-ups are required for presenters. Each presenter will have 15 minutes to share their work and receive feedback. All are welcome to attend!

Sign up here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C084CA8AC29A4FBCE9-64417636-video

*These sessions will not be recorded.

sessions.title: The latest intel on big tech and AI threats, and tools to expose them
sessions.description: GIJN recently produced best-practices stories, videos, a report, and a webinar for its Tech Focus Project based on the knowledge and exchange of ideas of 100 experts in the field. This session will distill the highlights: tools for detecting AI-generated content; experimenting with AI tools; cutting-edge tips to protect from surveillance; tools to investigate government use of AI, and techniques for radical collaboration to counter Big Tech’s assault on the info ecosystem. Andrea Arzaba (GIJN), Craig Silverman (Indicator), Lam Thuy Vo (Documented/CUNY/Pulitzer Center), and Marina Walker Guevara (Pulitzer Center) will share tips and experiences around investigating Tech and using Tech for investigations.

sessions.title: FOIA, AI and the future of access to public records
sessions.description: Artificial intelligence stands ready to impact the public records process for both government agencies and requesters. This session will examine the lifecycle of a public records request and its intersection with AI, including how agencies are deploying AI in their operations, how custom RAG systems can help journalists make more precise requests, and turning unstructured PDFs into structured data. Join this interactive session to explore what omnipresent AI might mean for the future of public records.

sessions.title: Crafting impactful digital video investigations
sessions.description: In this session, reporters and editors will share how they’re incorporating video and visual evidence into investigations for newspapers (not broadcast). Learn how they use bystander video, satellite imagery and other open-source material in a variety of digital formats to drive accountability reporting — and how they guide these productions across a large cross-functional newsroom.

sessions.title: Outlining and structure: The writer's missing manual
sessions.description:
Outlining might be the single best thing you can do to make you a better, faster writer. This isn't the roman numeral outlining your middle school teacher taught you. We'll take a story from the ground up, showing how early considerations about structure and framing develop into a draft.

Preregistration is required and seating is limited.

sessions.title: Navigating risk: Practical safety for journalists reporting in the U.S.
sessions.description: Reporting in the field increasingly exposes journalists to complex safety challenges—from covering protests to navigating surveillance technologies and law enforcement encounters in cities like Los Angeles, Chicago, Minneapolis, and beyond. Recent conversations among journalists have underscored urgent concerns, including how to safely navigate interactions with ICE and how to distinguish between different law enforcement agencies on the ground. Join the International Women’s Media Foundation (IWMF) and the Journalist Assistance Network for a practical session focused on assessing and managing these risks. Safety experts will share strategies for mitigating on-the-ground threats, along with practical guidance for covering protests and high-risk situations. Attendees will leave with concrete, actionable tools to help them report safely in volatile environments—so they can document critical stories without compromising their personal security.

sessions.title: Get that TPS report (with cover sheet): Easy, no-code AI document finders
sessions.description:
This hands-on training session shows how easy it is to use AI to find specific documents relevant to your investigation without needing programming skills, money or sign-on accounts. You will find and download a public records request log (or bring your own as Excel/PDF files - requested ahead of time from your local agency) and run it through NotebookLM, comparing results to a Google search. We will also demonstrate other useful document finder tools, including the fed’s FOIA Wizard, MuckRock’s FOIA Explorer, FOIAengine (free to IRE members), and new state-level tools being developed by MuckRock.

Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: Everything you never wanted to know about crypto: A hands-on workshop for investigations
sessions.description:
Hundreds of billions of dollars move across blockchains every year — and unlike in the traditional financial system, almost all of it is public. You just need to know where to look. 

This hands-on session will show you (yes, you!) how to follow the money on blockchains — and use what you find to break investigative stories.

We'll start with a quick crypto 101: how you can use on-chain data to surface leads, key terms and concepts, and a tour of free tools for tracing funds on-chain.

Then, we'll walk through the strategies The Wall Street Journal uses to trace illicit funds and de-anonymize crypto wallets — and we'll put those methods to work in hands-on practice with real examples from our reporting.

The methods we'll teach in this session are the same ones Wall Street Journal reporters have used to break front-page stories: identifying insider trading at prediction markets, exposing North Korean employment scams, and revealing secret deals between Trump companies and foreign royals.

The session is designed for all experience levels. No prior knowledge of crypto, data, or coding is necessary. You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate.

sessions.title: Google Sheets 1: Getting started with spreadsheets
sessions.description:
In this introduction to spreadsheets, you'll begin analyzing data with Google Sheets, a simple but powerful tool. You'll learn how to enter data, navigate spreadsheets and conduct simple calculations like sum, average and median. 

This session is good for: Data beginners.

You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class, and you will need a free Google account to participate.

sessions.title: 15 min break
How IRL events fuel audience growth and accountability journalism
sessions.description: In-person live events drive audience growth for accountability journalism by building trust and providing an opportunity for the audience, followers and consumers to get closer to the solutions journalism they consume. Newsrooms that produce IRL gatherings make a commitment to create tangible community connections and to reach loyal subscribers. By hosting intimate town halls and live events, reporters may reveal their investigative process, discuss sourcing and engage audience questions. This effort positions newsrooms to be seen as a community utility rather than a distant news institution.

sessions.title: Unsung documents
sessions.description: Veteran investigative reporters reprise a classic panel and list obscure but powerful documents that anyone can use to turbocharge a project or bulk-up daily beat coverage. They also provide practical guidelines for unearthing, scrutinizing, managing and cross-referencing routine documents to unleash their full investigative power.

sessions.title: Investigating Trump
sessions.description: With the curtailment of oversight by the Justice Department,  federal inspectors general, the Office of Government Ethics, Office of Special Counsel and Republicans in Congress, the news media has become one of the only remaining entities capable to examining actions by President Trump, his family, donors and his cabinet officials to at least bring some transparency to wrongdoing. Reporters from The NYT, WSJ, Bloomberg, Reuters, ProPublica, The WashPost, NPR, among others, have been doing this work. But it is something that reporters from around the U.S. and the world can help with. We talk about how to take up such an effort, examining some examples from the last 18 months. But we also spend time considering how various news entities in spots across the nation and the world can take up this challenge. Accountability still matters.

sessions.title: Funding your dream investigation project
sessions.description: With budget cuts and layoffs, more staff reporters and independent journalists must now fund their own investigative reporting projects. Fortunately, organizations such as the Fund for Investigative Journalism, the Pulitzer Center, USC Annenberg Center for Health Journalism, McGraw Center for Business Journalism, and the Data-Driven Reporting Project offer financial and other means of support. This workshop will help IRE members connect with funders, learn about resources for freelancers, and get guidance on finding funding, securing publisher commitments, and drafting strong grant pitches. We’ll cover key elements of a successful pitch, budgeting basics, and strategies to catch the attention of editors and grant evaluators.

sessions.title: Driveway moments: Crafting stories that stop listeners in their tracks
sessions.description: Investigative journalism meets powerful storytelling in this panel designed for audio creators aiming to create those unforgettable "driveway moments"—when listeners can’t turn off the story even after arriving at their destination. Panelists will explore how to build suspense, evoke emotion, and connect with audiences on a deeply personal level while staying true to the investigative rigor. From scene-setting to pacing, attendees will gain practical strategies for turning complex investigations into gripping, memorable audio experiences.

sessions.title: Covering AI’s environmental impact: How to make a complex story accessible
sessions.description:
Artificial intelligence is rapidly reshaping the world—but its environmental impact is still poorly understood by many audiences. Data centers consume enormous amounts of energy and water, and AI expansion is raising new questions about climate and resource use. Yet much of the reporting on this topic is highly technical and difficult for audiences to engage with.

This panel will explore how journalists can better cover AI’s environmental footprint in ways that are clear, compelling, and accessible. Research from Trusting News shows that many people care deeply about the environmental impact of emerging technologies—but often struggle to understand coverage or see how it connects to their lives.

Panelists will share examples of reporting approaches that help audiences get “on-ramps” into this complex issue. These include social media explainers, visual storytelling, social cards, and narrative framing that makes the stakes understandable without oversimplifying the science or policy.

The discussion will focus on practical strategies reporters and editors can use to translate complicated environmental and technology reporting into stories that invite audiences in—and motivate deeper engagement.

sessions.title: What do YOU do when diversity, equity & inclusion becomes taboo?
sessions.description: How do you produce inclusive and compelling storytelling on the important stories in your community if your newsroom priorities have changed? We’ll lead an honest conversation about how the shift away from diversity, equity and inclusion impacts our newsrooms, us as journalists and our journalism. The discussion will explore strategies on how to navigate corporate buzzwords and move towards editorial impact, reader trust, and community engagement.

sessions.title: Breaking news! What now?
sessions.description:
A strong I-Team or investigative reporter should be on the air when major news breaks ... so don't wait to be invited!
We will show why you should get in the mix & exactly how to add value in minutes. You’ll leave with a practical checklist for what to do now, what to do the moment news breaks, what to do before the first day ends, how to follow up the next day, and how to turn a chaotic breaking story into a sharper investigative piece the next week or month.
Bring your breaking-news confusion; leave with a playbook.

sessions.title: Networking: Educators
sessions.description: Mix and mingle with peers from throughout the world to find solidarity, make new friends, and build your professional community in this fun and informal networking session. This session is for those who teach journalism.

sessions.title: No code, open data: Using immigration data without coding
sessions.description:
Don’t know coding? No fear, this session will teach attendees how they can use data from the Deportation Data Project without any coding. Using Google Sheets, attendees will learn how they can perform analysis on publicly available immigration data. The session will demystify key fields in the data, provide practical definitions and identify pitfalls to avoid. Reporters will gain the foundation to find stories related to their communities in this trove of data. 

Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: Finding the story: Court records
sessions.description:
In this interactive hands-on session, bring your laptop and practice with us as we show you new tools to help you analyze complex court records - especially on deadline. This hands-on session focuses primarily on a powerful analysis tool: Google Pinpoint.

 We’ll also briefly discuss PACER and RECAP, bread-and-butter tools to help you locate court records across the country.  We’ll keep this session lively with fun challenges, so come hang out with us!  

This session is good for: everyone! No previous data experience is required. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: Google Sheets 2: Formulas & sorting
sessions.description:
Much of Google Sheets' power comes in the form of formulas. In this class, you'll learn how to use them to analyze data with the eye of a journalist. Yes, math will be involved, but it's totally worth it! This class will show you how calculations like change, percent change, rates and ratios can beef up your reporting.

This session is good for: Anyone who has taken Google Sheets 1 or has been introduced to spreadsheets.

You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class, and you will need a free Google account to participate.

sessions.title: 15 min break
How they attack us: A field guide to understanding and responding to anti-investigative propaganda
sessions.description: Investigative reporting creates enemies—that is a certainty. While physical threats and legal harassment are serious dangers, the most frequent global threat is sophisticated disinformation aimed at discrediting the facts or the journalists. Drawing on years of experience observing real-time campaigns launched by state media, government officials, and anti-establishment influencers following major exposés, this session will provide a practical, action-oriented playbook for newsrooms under attack. Revealing the highly predictable narratives used to undermine accountability journalism, attendees will learn how to effectively counter disinformation and develop proactive strategies to pre-bunk and inoculate your audience against predictable attacks.

sessions.title: Deep dive: Turning investigative reporting into the stuff of literature
sessions.description:
Pulitzer Prize winners Caitlin Dickerson and Stephanie McCrummen both write for The Atlantic, and when you see their bylines, you know to read. Dickerson writes epic pieces steeped in horror, from “Seventy Miles in Hell,” on the Darién Gap, to the secret history of the U.S. government’s family-separation policy. McCrummen goes where few reporters venture and writes opening sentences like this: “On the day she heard God tell her to buy a mountain, Tami Barthen already sensed that her life was on a spiritual upswing.” Their talk will take you to the intersection of investigative journalism and narrative.

The presenters request that there be no video recording, photos or posting to social media during their conversation.

sessions.title: In conversation with Yamiche Alcindor, White House Correspondent for NBC News
sessions.description:
Join Yamiche Alcindor, the award-winning NBC News White House correspondent, for a special conversation with Ron Nixon on Thursday, June 18.

From her groundbreaking reporting on the Trump administration to her coverage of race, culture and national policy, Alcindor has helped shape the national conversation and informed millions of Americans during some of the most consequential moments in recent history.

This is your opportunity to hear directly from one of journalism's most respected voices as she discusses reporting at the highest levels, navigating today's political landscape and the future of accountability journalism. 
This session is presented by NBC News.

sessions.title: Networking: Freelancers
sessions.description: Mix and mingle, meet friends old and new, and build your professional community in this fun and informal networking session. This session is for people who work as freelancers. This session is co-hosted by the Institute for Independent Journalists.

sessions.title: Full disclosure: Using state ethics disclosures to power accountability reporting
sessions.description:
Public officials and candidates across the country file personal financial and economic interest disclosures, and those records can be a goldmine for accountability reporting, useful for surfacing conflicts of interest, tracking business entanglements, backgrounding candidates, and connecting the dots between officials' financial lives and their public decisions. But the data is scattered across fifty different systems with different rules, formats, and levels of accessibility. In this session, we’ll dig into the why, how states compare, what’s available, and how you can use disclosure data for your own work. 

This session will walk through how to find, access, and use disclosure data to support reporting on elections, real estate, inter-and-intra-state business connections, environmental policy, and more. We'll highlight examples of disclosure-driven reporting from across the country, and we'll demo a growing collaborative database of disclosures collected from states nationwide as part of an ongoing open project that attendees are invited to join. We'll also share a comprehensive tipsheet on what data is available in each state and run through strategies for building a continuous workflow around these using these filings on your beat. 

Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: What crime stats can (and can't) tell you
sessions.description:
Crime statistics generate a lot of media coverage, but they can be hard to interpret and often obscure broader public safety trends. This workshop will offer practical recommendations for analyzing traditional crime data using locational and historical context, rather than chasing year-over-year fluctuations. It will also include tips for accessing data on other public safety issues, like housing, workplace, and environmental safety. Through real-world examples, hands-on exercises, and guided practice, journalists will leave with concrete tools to cover public safety data with more depth and nuance. 

Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: Google Sheets 3: Filtering & pivot tables
sessions.description:
A look at the awesome power of pivot — and how to use it to analyze your dataset in minutes rather than hours. We'll work up to using a pivot table by first sorting and filtering a dataset, learning how to find story ideas along the way.

This session is good for: Anyone familiar with formulas, sorting and filtering in a spreadsheet program.

You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class, and you will need a free Google account to participate.

sessions.title: Opening Reception Sponsored by NBC News and NBCUniversal Local
sessions.description: The reception sponsor, NBC News, will offer a few words before IRE's former executive director, Diana Fuentes, is recognized. Light bites will be available along with two drink ticket for each person. Conference badges are required for admittance.

sessions.title: Game Night
sessions.description: Have some fun with friends old and new while playing board games, cards, etc. Games and cards are provided, and feel free to bring your own. All conference attendees are welcome.

sessions.title: Recharge Room
sessions.description: Need a spot to decompress, take a load off, get your bearings or recharge your batteries? This is the room for you!

sessions.title: Registration Hours
sessions.description: Pick up your badge or register on-site: 7:30 a.m. - 5:30 p.m.

sessions.title: Mentor Breakfast (invite only)
sessions.description: If you signed up for the conference mentor program, come meet your match at this invitation-only breakfast.

sessions.title: Adjacent Event: Breakfast Chat About ProPublica's Local Reporting Network
sessions.description: Are you a local reporter or editor with an ambitious investigative project in mind? Please join us to learn more about the Local Reporting Network, a collaborative program through which ProPublica works with local newsrooms on a year-long accountability project. This opportunity is open to both staff and freelance reporters working with a local/regional newsroom on the project. Please join us for an informal breakfast chat about the program and how it works. You'll meet both ProPublica editors and reporters who can talk about their participation in the program.

sessions.title: Exhibitors
sessions.description: Stop by and visit with the exhibitors and recruiters.

sessions.title: Working it out: How editors & reporters work together, struggle together & produce great stories
sessions.description: Relationships between reporters and editors can be fraught. They can be uplifting. They can be a bit of both. This session looks at the pitfalls, puzzlements and triumphs of working together and, sometimes, working at odds with each other. What do reporters need from editors? What do editors need from reporters? Panelists include journalists who have worked on both sides of the equation, including multiple Pulitzer Prize and IRE award winners.

sessions.title: Investigative journalism amid a decline in democracy
sessions.description: With more investigative journalists working in countries with authoritarian regimes or a decline in democracy, independent reporters have developed methods and techniques to continue holding power to account, while reducing risks connected to the context in which the reporting is taking place. While freedom of the press continues to deteriorate, strategies applied by journalists as well as collaborations, have been key to continue uncovering stories of public interest.

sessions.title: You just got hit by a natural disaster. Here's what to investigate.
sessions.description: Natural disasters happen in every market. Regardless if you're facing tornadoes, wildfires, floods, global warming or ice storms, your viewers and readers will rely on you to investigate the systems in place that respond to the crises. Three journalists who have investigated system failures following natural disasters will show you what to immediately investigate and make your coverage stand out from your competition.

sessions.title: Covering extreme right influences on your beat
sessions.description: The Far Right is not just a collection of loud personalities in the Trump administration. It is a complex network of disparate groups that often work together. Ultraconservative funders, Christian Right advocacy organizations, white supremacists, social media influencers, and local legislators who have extreme goals often work together to wield enormous power in the communities you cover. This panel will offer a useful guide for reporters who want to take local stories (about extremism) and use them to shed light on how this larger national movement works to push public policy and specific agendas. We’ll share tools for finding hidden funding streams, tracking dark-money organizations, exposing behind-the-scenes political influencers, and reporting on astro-turf pipelines. We’ll talk about how to pitch these stories to editors, how to cover them in ways that help local audiences better understand the national forces at work, and how to stay safe while reporting on them.

sessions.title: Mining the mountain: Turning massive document dumps into breakthrough stories
sessions.description:
From the Robb Elementary investigative records to the Epstein files, journalists are increasingly faced with sprawling, unstructured document dumps that can overwhelm even experienced reporters.
This training session breaks down how to turn thousands of pages into focused, high-impact stories for broadcast, digital, and ongoing investigative coverage.
Participants will learn how to prepare for these document dumps, strategies for getting through thousands of pages, organizational systems, finding the stories in the stack, and tracking follow-ups.

sessions.title: Video show & tell: Photogs and editors: Show your best visuals
sessions.description:
Sign up to “Show & Tell” us about your best work and get constructive feedback from the moderator and audience. 

Sign-ups are required for presenters. Each presenter will have 15 minutes to share their work and receive feedback. All are welcome to attend!

Sign up here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C084CA8AC29A4FBCE9-64417265-video

*These sessions will not be recorded.

sessions.title: Legal rights and resources for reporters covering midterm elections
sessions.description: With consequential midterm elections at all levels on the ballot in 2026, journalists covering campaigns and elections need to know their rights before challenges arise. This session will offer a practical primer on issues journalists are most likely to encounter on the campaign trail and Election Day, including newsgathering rights at polling places, access to campaign events, and using public records laws to obtain voter registration records, ballots, and other election-related information.

sessions.title: Consumer confidential
sessions.description: Great consumer investigations start with stories people actually care about — and reporters who know how to make those stories stand out. In this session, journalists and consumer reporters will share real-world tips on finding compelling content, building reliable sources, and spotting the everyday issues that impact viewers most. The panel will also dive into how reporters can separate themselves in a crowded media landscape by developing a strong brand, connecting with audiences, and telling stories in ways that get noticed across platforms. Whether you’re new to consumer reporting or looking to sharpen your investigative edge, this conversation will offer practical ideas you can take back to your newsroom right away.

sessions.title: Reporting and writing for scene
sessions.description:
When it comes to crafting scenes, we can learn a lot from people who write fiction and screenplays. But our challenges begin long before the writing. Our scenes have to be true not just emotionally, but factually. This class will look at the importance of dialogue (dialogue >>> quotes); ways to hook and pull (and weave); and how to write a scene that readers will remember (all, without sacrificing the element of accountability — because, that’s what we do). We’ll dissect song lyrics; chat about creating dread; read a scene on the page, then watch it dramatized on screen. Plus, the incomparable Lizzie Presser (her first IRE!) will drop in and answer questions about some of her most unforgettable work.

Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.

sessions.title: The Business of Freelancing Workshop
sessions.description:
Presented by the Institute for Independent Journalists. 

Join other freelance journalists for a full-day workshop that will teach you the path to financially and emotionally sustainable freelancing! You'll receive 6 live, interactive hours of class time and a package of worksheets, freelance tools, and resources to rely on for years to come. Taught personally by IIJ leaders Katherine Reynolds Lewis, Shernay Williams, Emma Carew Grovum and Kelly Glass. 

9:00 - 10:00 am Session 1: Principles for Sustainable Freelancing

10:15 am - 11:15 am Session 2: Your Brand and Your Network

11:30 - 12:30 pm Session 3: Inside a Freelance Business

2:30 - 3:45 pm Session 4: Editors Panel

4:00 - 5:00 pm Session 5: Time, Client and Self Management

5:15 - 6:15 pm Session 6: Freelance Systems for Success

Hear from editors who assign to freelance journalists, including what they’re looking for in a pitch, how to become a go-to contributor, strategies for deepening your relationship with a client, managing conflict, and more. Speakers include editors for National Geographic and other publications.

Speakers:

Starlight Williams, National Geographic

Maryam Saleh, Type Investigations

Mitch Ryals, The Baltimore Banner

Moderator: Kirstin Garriss, award-winning journalist, public relations and communications director at URL Collective

More information here: https://theiij.com/2026-dc-workshop 

Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.

sessions.title: How explaining your sources builds trust
sessions.description:
Investigative journalism often asks audiences to trust reporting that took months or years to produce and that may include serious allegations against powerful people. In an era of intense scrutiny and accusations of bias, simply publishing the findings isn’t always enough. Increasingly, audiences want to understand how journalists reached their conclusions.

This session explores how investigative reporters can build trust by being more transparent about their sourcing and reporting decisions. Drawing on research from Trusting News and examples from investigative outlets, the session will share practical ways journalists can explain their sourcing without compromising safety or confidentiality.

We’ll discuss strategies such as explaining why certain sources were chosen, how sources were vetted, who was contacted but declined to comment, and what steps reporters took to verify claims. We’ll also look at how journalists can proactively address common audience questions about bias by showing their reporting process.

Attendees will leave with concrete language, examples, and habits they can incorporate into investigative stories, helping audiences better understand the rigor behind their work and strengthening trust in high-stakes reporting.

sessions.title: Finding forever chemicals in your community: A hands-on guide to PFAS data
sessions.description:
PFAS "forever chemicals" have contaminated drinking water systems across the United States, and the data to start investigating is already public. This hands-on session will show reporters how to find contamination and potential industrial sources in the communities they cover using freely available federal datasets. We'll walk through EPA's Unregulated Contaminant Monitoring Rule (UCMR) data to identify which public water systems have detected PFAS and at what levels, then use EPA's ECHO database to pinpoint nearby facilities that manufacture or discharge these chemicals. Attendees will leave with a step-by-step workflow they can apply to their own coverage area as soon as they get home. No prior data experience required.

Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: Claude Code for journalists: A (responsible) hands-on introduction to AI-powered data exploration
sessions.description:
Journalists are increasingly expected to work with data and documents, but many lack programming backgrounds. Claude Code — an AI coding tool that works on the desktop, command line or IDE — can help bridge that gap, letting reporters do some too-complicated-for-Excel analytical work without writing code from scratch. This three-hour, pre-registered hands-on class will give attendees a practical, start-to-finish introduction to Claude Code in a journalism context. Attendees will learn how to use Claude Code to do initial exploration of unfamiliar datasets, spot data quality issues that could undermine later findings, build simple web apps for exploring and visualizing data, develop a data analysis plan for a reporting project, and work through document sets efficiently. Just as importantly, attendees will learn what Claude Code can't do well, where it's likely to lead you astray, and how to verify its output — because responsible use matters as much as knowing the tool. No prior programming experience required. Attendees will leave with a repeatable workflow they can bring back to their newsrooms immediately.

Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: Audio Coaching 1:1
sessions.description: Sign up for 30 minutes with an audio pro to use however you like: Bring tape for a critique, a pitch for workshopping or your questions about surviving and thriving in the audio journalism space. Sign up at http://tiny.cc/audio-1-1

sessions.title: Barry Nash and Company One-on-One Coaching
sessions.description:
"Barry Nash and his team return to IRE to provide personalized coaching sessions. If you have questions about any aspect of your delivery — your appearance, how you use your voice, how you can perform most effectively in the field or in the studio — this is an opportunity you won’t want to miss. Barry has been working with talent at all levels of experience and ability for over 30 years. He currently authors the only blog in the world focused exclusively on television news and sports performance. You can find it on Substack
Please note: Participants should bring recent footage on a flash drive, webpage/online link, or storage drive (Google or Dropbox).                                                                                                                            Sign up for a time slot here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C0445A9AD28A5FEC34-64383836-irecoaching#/"

sessions.title: 15 min break
Life + Work Coaching
sessions.description: Join certified coach and meditation facilitator Nicholas Whitaker for exclusive 1:1 coaching sessions just for IRE guests. In this laser-focused session, you will have the space to share what's weighing on your mind and heart, and receive compassionate coaching to help guide you to your next steps. If you've never received coaching before, this is a great, low-risk way to get some experience with it and discover what even a single coaching session can do to help move you forward. Sign up for a time slot at: http://tiny.cc/work-and-life-ire26

sessions.title: From classroom to newsroom: Finding your footing after school
sessions.description: Transitioning from internships and student journalism to your first real newsroom can be an exciting, and daunting, leap. In this session, four early-career journalists share the steps they took to jump-start their careers after graduation—and how you can do the same. From landing your first job to establishing yourself in a new city or beat, this session is great for recent journalism grads, current students, and those looking to pivot into journalism from other careers.

sessions.title: Media law in the second Trump Administration
sessions.description: The second presidency of Donald Trump has upended norms and legal understandings regarding the ability of journalists to do their jobs without retaliation from federal and state governments. We’ll discuss how reporters and news organizations are navigating – or failing to navigate – these threats and challenges while exercising their First Amendment rights.

sessions.title: Your investigation reveals a problem. Adding a solutions story shows it doesn't have to be this way.
sessions.description: Would a solutions angle help my story?  How do I find one?  This panel will feature two investigo-solutions reporters, and how and why they chose to cover solutions. Attendees will also learn a simple three-step process for finding the right solutions angle for their investigation.

sessions.title: Art of the business investigation
sessions.description: Hear from top business journalists in print, digital and broadcast dissect the unique challenges in navigating corporate America from how to find a variety of company records to getting reluctant subjects to talk.  We'll show examples of how to turn a "no" into a "yes" from PR and crisis management firms hired to protect a company's image. We'll also detail how to get current and former employees to open up, and how to obtain and analyze obscure documents.

sessions.title: Open mic: Should I even keep doing this?
sessions.description: In the face of never-ending change, we're forced to ask the question - is it all worth it?  We talk about home life, marriage and kids, moving from city to city, constant upheaval in the business and mistrust of journalists. It's stressful!  How do you successfully navigate the business and a personal life and if it's all changing, do you keep doing it all? Two veteran journalists, moms and friends start the conversation - you finish it!

sessions.title: AI that actually knows what's happening: Live web research for working journalists
sessions.description:
AI to help you research a story, it draws on a snapshot of the internet from a single point in time. It doesn't know what was published today, and it will answer confidently anyway.

This session shows you the fix. We'll walk through prompt engineering for real journalism research and how to structure requests that produce sourced, credible, organized information rather than confident-sounding summaries of old data. Then we'll connect it to the live web and run the same prompt again. The difference is immediate and visible.

No coding required. Free tools provided. You'll walk out with something you can use on your next story. 

*Must have an account (can be free tier) with GPT.

sessions.title: Protecting Your Sources: Video edition
sessions.description: Understanding what a true source is and building relationships with them. These seasoned journalists will also discuss what to do when sources are reluctant to go on camera and the pressure when asked to reveal who their sources are. They will also share wisdom on being transparent with a source about what you can and cannot do.

sessions.title: Finding the story: Trade data
sessions.description:
As trade and tariffs become an increasingly relevant issue, this session will demo a free (but very unintuitive) tool from the U.S. Census: USA Trade! USA Trade has comprehensive, district-level data (with lots of hidden gems) that can be useful for data journalists across beats, especially those relating to global economics, trade, tech, and foreign policy. This session will demonstrate how to use USA Trade and discuss some ways to understand and use the data in your reporting. This session is good for everyone interested in learning more about trade statistics.

No data experience is necessary! Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: 15 min break
Deep dive: Cultivating sources on the inside
sessions.description:
Hamed Aleaziz and Jason Leopold have worked their way deep inside seemingly impenetrable institutions, from ICE to the Treasury Department. Their sourcing has underpinned work that’s been honored as Pulitzer finalists, including Aleaziz’s beat reporting on immigration and Leopold’s reporting on a trove of secret government records that showed dirty money coursing through major banks. In this conversation, they’ll chat about their different approaches to getting people to talk.


**To allow the panelists to be candid, this session will be OFF THE RECORD - NO RECORDING/SOCIAL MEDIA POSTING**

sessions.title: Media law for journalists: Libel & invasion of privacy
sessions.description: In this session, you’ll learn about the fundamentals of libel and privacy laws applicable to journalistic content. We’ll discuss what the plaintiff has to prove to make out a libel case, your common law and constitutional defenses, the differences in defending cases brought by public and private figures, and trouble spots such as libelous implications, use of confidential sources and the value of retractions. Lawsuits for publishing truthful but embarrassing private facts will also be considered.

sessions.title: Using court records to break stories
sessions.description: PACER, the federal court records system, is antiquated and hard to navigate. Using skills developed over years, Seamus Hughes has helped the media break national stories about topics ranging from terrorism and public corruption to corporate espionage. Seamus Hughes is back to give us tips and suggestions on how to use PACER to tell a story.

sessions.title: I-team 2.0: How to build labs pairing vets and newcomers to fill the void left by newsroom cuts
sessions.description: Investigative labs increasingly fill gaps left by massive newsroom cuts. Many successfully pair experienced muckrakers with students or early-career investigators. But each lab takes a slightly different approach. Hear top editors compare and contrast styles, and explain how they assign roles, manage egos and win Pulitzers.

sessions.title: Why you should care about sports betting, even if you don't cover sports
sessions.description: This session will cover the basics of proposition betting and will include a demo of our effort to capture the scope and nature of those bets during March Madness. We’ll go over what data is available and how even non-sports reporters can use it. You’ll leave this session with a working knowledge of how prop betting works and ideas about how to incorporate it into your beat.

sessions.title: Video show & tell: Anything goes with Gilbert Corsey
sessions.description:
Sign up to “Show & Tell” us about your best work and get constructive feedback from the moderator and audience.

Sign-ups are required for presenters. Each presenter will have 15 minutes to share their work and receive feedback. All are welcome to attend!

Sign up here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C084CA8AC29A4FBCE9-64417958-video

*These sessions will not be recorded.

sessions.title: Evolving narratives in audio storytelling about crime
sessions.description: Journalists hate the term "true crime." But true crime podcasts have captivated audiences, and the genre is evolving beyond its traditional confines. This panel explores how investigative journalists and storytellers are redefining true crime by focusing on systemic issues, deeper narratives and ethical storytelling. Panelists will share their experiences crafting compelling audio that goes beyond "whodunit," balancing audience engagement with sensitivity, accuracy and accountability. Attendees will gain insights into innovative reporting techniques, narrative structure and the ethical dilemmas of audio storytelling in this increasingly popular medium.

sessions.title: Anatomy of an Investigation
sessions.description: This year's IRE Award winners discuss how they got from idea to air.  Featured are winners from across the platform spectrum: The 60 Minutes team who produced "The Prisoners," winning National Outstanding Video and a local reporter from Shreveport, LA who won honors in Small Market Outstanding Video for "A Mother's Cry."

sessions.title: International networking
sessions.description:
Let’s talk about international journalism networks. This session is an informal chat for international journalists, first-time attendees, and anyone interested in global journalism opportunities to meet, exchange ideas, and share practical tips.

We will discuss how journalists can build connections across countries, host regional meetups, and find fellowship opportunities around the world. I will also share lessons learned from organizing the first Seoul meetup and ideas for Islamabad meetup discussion. Also, you can offer your idea or plan on international meetup. 

First-time attendees are especially welcome.

sessions.title: Immigration by the numbers - the data sets defining Trump’s deportation agenda
sessions.description:
The Trump administration is setting records when it comes to immigration enforcement. Record numbers of people in detention, deportations to third countries, arrests and no shows in court, money invested in deportations and clashes with U.S. citizens. Immigration reporters have been combing the numbers to create their own datasets, and resourcing other tracking organizations, to decipher the full picture of what a mass deportation plan looks like, costs, and reveals about the immigration enforcement system.

Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: IRE Awards Luncheon
sessions.description:
At the IRE Awards luncheon, the 2025 IRE Awards will be presented. A salute to some of the best investigative work of the past year. In addition, the Ring of Honor inductees will be recofnized along with the Don Bolles Medal and the Golden Padlock award. 
Conference name badges are required for entry.
A ticket to the luncheon must be purchased beforehand. Prices vary depending on your membership status. Purchase your ticket through your membership profile.

sessions.title: Adjacent Event: Journalists Recovery Network
sessions.description: The Journalists Recovery Network will meet and offer a sober space for journalists experiencing troubles with alcohol or substance use. This is an informal meeting for individuals who may struggle with substance use to meet our members and chat more about our organization. Feel free to reach out to us at journalistsrecoverynetwork@gmail.com.

sessions.title: Reporting from exile: How to cover authoritarian regimes, protect sources and build trust
sessions.description:
Journalists covering authoritarian regimes often continue their work from exile—but repression does not stop at the border. This panel explores how reporters investigate power across borders while navigating surveillance, threats to family members, and fear within diaspora communities.
Drawing on experiences from Iran, Cuba, Venezuela, and Tibet, panelists will share practical strategies to: protect sources and collaborators across borders; build trust with communities living under fear and trauma; navigate digital and physical security risks in exile and sustain investigative work from outside the country.

sessions.title: Media law for journalists: Newsgathering
sessions.description: In this session, you’ll learn from an experienced media attorney about the laws governing newsgathering activity, including going undercover, entering private property, using technology, gaining access to government proceedings and records, dealing with confidential sources, and more. We’ll also talk specifically about coverage of immigration enforcement and protests.

sessions.title: The new tactics and technologies of war
sessions.description:
In the past year, the U.S. military has undergone a dramatic transformation, accelerating its use of new technologies like AI and drones as it tries to rapidly implement lessons learned from the conflicts in Ukraine, Iran, and elsewhere.

Yet even as the U.S. engages in significant military operations across the world, it has been severely limiting reporters’ access to senior leaders in the Pentagon.

Three journalists who have reported on the military for over a decade will talk through reporting strategies that enabled them to get behind the scenes and how they’re grappling with the current challenges to press access. They’ll share tips that helped them uncover the definitive account of how the U.S. military adopted artificial intelligence, miscalculations in the war in Ukraine, and casualties linked to U.S. operations in Iran. To report these stories, they relied on a wide array of tools, from sourcing to embedding with troops to visual forensics.
