Any journalist trying to make sense of military operations today will leave with concrete ideas for how to pursue their own stories in this area.

sessions.title: Inside the housing crisis: What every reporter needs to know
sessions.description: The housing crisis touches nearly every community — from record housing costs to rising homelessness to millions of evictions each year — yet reporting on it can feel daunting. Where are the real stories? How do you navigate the technical data? And how can newsroom work lead to accountability or change? In this session, three experts from data, academia, and storytelling break down the biggest trends, the overlooked angles, and the most useful tools for reporters on any beat. Walk away with story ideas, data sources, and a clearer roadmap for covering one of the most urgent issues in the country. Moderated by Princeton’s Eviction Lab.

sessions.title: Filing better FOIAs (getting to documents via people, and vice versa)
sessions.description:
This session will explore how to increase your chances of receiving records that reveal significant new information to advance your story. 
Who and what can help you identify what records you should ask for in the first place? And how can you make itmore likely that you’ll actually receive a useful response? 
We will look at strategies for formulating and pursuing public records requests — treating them as an distinct reporting task, often requiring cultivating relationships, conducting interviews and other digging to help pinpoint what records could be most helpful to ask for, and ease the path to actually getting them. We will also look at how you can make the most of records to help identify potential sources for further reporting.

sessions.title: Video show & tell: Breaking news investigations on deadline with Jatara McGee
sessions.description:
Sign up to “Show & Tell” us about your best work and get constructive feedback from the moderator and audience.

Sign-ups are required for presenters. Each presenter will have 15 minutes to share their work and receive feedback. All are welcome to attend!

Sign up here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C084CA8AC29A4FBCE9-64417344-video

*These sessions will not be recorded.

sessions.title: Networking: LGBTQ
sessions.description: What better way to savor Pride month than to be among your community. Join us to mix, mingle and build community. This session is for people who identify as part of the LGBTQ+ community.

sessions.title: The 10 things I've learned
sessions.description: One of IRE’s most popular sessions each year, this panel brings together veteran journalists to share the lessons that shaped their careers, changed their reporting, and helped them survive the chaos of this business. From investigations and storytelling to mistakes, burnout, leadership, and life outside the newsroom, this session is packed with practical advice, honest moments, and a little humor. Expect real talk, memorable stories, and takeaways you can actually use, no matter where you are in your career.

sessions.title: So you wanna make a podcast?
sessions.description:
This workshop is designed for the audio-curious who feel like they don't know where to start. What staffing do you need? How much might it cost? How long might it take? How can you make something that people will actually hear? How do you approach storyboarding, getting comfortable with the gear, scripting, voicing/tracking, storytelling? We'll cover enough of the basics that attendees will feel equipped to pitch and begin developing their ideas.

Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.

sessions.title: Searching in the age of AI
sessions.description: Search engines prioritize AI summaries over results. Social platform search keeps getting worse. And SEO manipulation is scaling up thanks to generative AI tools. Journalists need to adapt how they search for information, people, and other resources and targets. This workshop shows you what's changed, what still works, and provides you with practical strategies and tools for getting past the noise and finding what you’re looking for. Bring your laptop.

sessions.title: Coaching AI tools for investigative reporting
sessions.description: AI tools like ChatGPT and Claude are most useful when you coach them, giving clear direction, pushing back on bad answers, and iterating until you get what you need. In this hands-on session, we'll use AI to draft targeted FOIA requests and iterate through them; make sense of an unfamiliar dataset by generating a data dictionary and identifying story questions; get help with Excel and spreadsheet tasks without writing code; and debug scripts when something breaks. You'll leave with reusable prompt patterns and a clear sense of where AI helps your reporting and where it will confidently make things up. No coding experience required. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training and have a free ChatGPT or Claude account.

sessions.title: Google Sheets: Importing and data prep
sessions.description:
Don't give up if your data isn't presented in a neat spreadsheet. This session will teach you how to get data into a spreadsheet and prepare it for analysis. We will look at how to import text files, deal with data in a PDF, and get a table on a web page into a spreadsheet.

This session is good for: Anyone comfortable working in Google Sheets.

You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class, and you will need a free Google account to participate.

sessions.title: 15 min break
The health care beat: following the money, power, and patients
sessions.description: Reporting on health care can feel confusing and overwhelming. The good news is that there are reams of public records and data to get you started at both the state and federal level. This session will show you where to begin when launching on a health care investigation, from official accounts of deficient care to detailed financial records that can shed light on business decisions that impact staff and patients. We'll also explore the challenges and careful considerations that must be made when filming inside hospitals and detailing patient stories.

sessions.title: Records access and the right to record: Newsgathering for covering law enforcement
sessions.description: Video of law enforcement in action and related public records are a powerful tool in accountability journalism — from covering police use-of-force incidents and protest responses, to immigration raids and other enforcement operations that happen out of public view. But the legal landscape around capturing and accessing footage of law enforcement and obtaining public records around the actions of individual officers and departments, is complex and changing. This session brings together journalists and legal experts to explore shifts in the right to record officers in public, including ongoing legal challenges to police “buffer zone” laws nationwide; using local law enforcement footage to scrutinize federal immigration enforcement; the use of drones for newsgathering amid efforts to establish no-fly zones around law enforcement operations; use of police in schools; and the varied sources for public records and footage that can be mined to investigate problem offers and departments.

sessions.title: Teaching the truth: innovations and imperatives in investigative journalism education for 2026
sessions.description:
The landscape of misinformation, media disruption, and AI is evolving quickly, and so must we educators adjust to prepare the next generation of investigative journalists. This session presents contemporary approaches, pedagogical frameworks, and practical tools for teaching investigative reporting to undergraduates and graduate students in 2026.
Participants will discuss how to integrate data literacy, digital verification, accountability reporting, collaborative methodologies, and ethical decision-making into our lessons, balanced with traditional skills like sourcing, narrative construction, and public records mastery. We’ll examine case studies of successful course designs, experiential learning models and strategies to help students navigate live investigations responsibly and safely.
The session also addresses emerging challenges such as AI’s impact on reporting workflows and media credibility; teaching students to critically assess generated content; and preparing them for cross-border, multimedia, and solutions-oriented investigative work. Attendees will leave with actionable ideas for syllabi, assignments, and assessment practices that reflect the demands and possibilities of investigative journalism in 2026.

sessions.title: Save Our Signs: Reporting on the federal push to rewrite history
sessions.description: In March 2025, the Trump administration issued Executive Order 14253, entitled “Restoring Truth and Sanity to American History,” which ordered a massive review of public monuments, signs and exhibits at National Park Service (NPS) sites to ensure that they do not “inappropriately disparage Americans past or living.” At least 57 educational signs have already been removed or altered at 10 NPS sites, and, according to a leaked internal database, government officials have flagged educational materials for review at more than 150 NPS sites. In response, a group of librarians and public historians formed Save Our Signs, a crowdsourced research project to create the Save Our Signs Archive: a centralized, open access archive dedicated to photos of National Parks signs. The SOS Archive has already been used by many journalists. In this panel, Save Our Signs team members will outline the real scope and impacts of EO 14253 and debunk common misconceptions. Attendees will learn about the opportunities and challenges facing journalists working with government sources, ranging from data sets, documents, media, and even interpretative materials at National Parks sites. In addition, we will discuss the opportunities and challenges of working with crowdsourced collections like the SOS Archive.

sessions.title: Bringing collaborative investigations to life across print and audio
sessions.description:
Collaborations between print and audio journalists have the power to bring stories to life, take listeners inside the reporting process and produce the kind of deeply engaging work that defines modern journalism. 

In this panel, you'll hear from journalists who successfully navigated print-audio collaborations that have resonated nationally. They'll also explore how to adapt an investigation for different platforms and how to juggle managing podcast production, reporting and editing needs for print stories. They’ll also discuss the pros and cons of working in two mediums simultaneously.

sessions.title: Video show & tell: Anything goes with Kara Kenney
sessions.description:
Sign up to “Show & Tell” us about your best work and get constructive feedback from the moderator and audience.

Sign-ups are required for presenters. Each presenter will have 15 minutes to share their work and receive feedback. All are welcome to attend!

Sign up here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C084CA8AC29A4FBCE9-64418022-video

*These sessions will not be recorded.

sessions.title: Gossip, group chats and encrypted drives: How to root out non-public records and keep sources safe
sessions.description:
When journalists talk about obtaining records, often it's in the context of using open records laws to pry documents from reticent government officials. But what about when the documents you need aren't held by the government — they're tucked away in a hospital's record system, or on a scammy salesperson's iPhone? What if you don't even know what records you're looking for?

During this panel, reporters Susie Neilson, Sara DiNatale and Elizabeth Koh will talk about creative techniques they've used for sourcing sensitive, non-public records, and how they used these records to produce impactful and award-winning investigations. Finally, John Skinner of SecureDrop will offer tips on how to keep sensitive sources safe from retribution by vindictive bosses or companies.

sessions.title: Scroll-stopping investigations: What works in vertical video
sessions.description: Vertical video can help investigative journalism reach audiences who may never watch a newscast or read a long-form story — but not every story works the same way on TikTok, Instagram Reels, YouTube Shorts or other social platforms. In this session, journalists who are successfully producing vertical video will break down what works, what doesn’t, and how to make strong editorial choices without sacrificing depth, accuracy or impact. From format and scripting to audience strategy and monetization, this panel will offer practical takeaways for reporters and newsrooms at every stage.

sessions.title: Find public records and leaks: The new OCCRP Aleph Pro
sessions.description:
You’ve got a leak, a name, or a suspicious company — what’s your next move? In a world of complex financial secrecy, uncovering the truth demands data power. Join this hands-on workshop to master the all-new OCCRP Aleph Pro, holding over four billion records of leaks, sanctions, corporate registries, and beyond. For years, Aleph has been the backbone of hundreds of investigations, and we have now evolved it to meet the challenges of modern reporting. Bring your laptop and learn to cross-reference targets, generate structured entities from raw text, and map hidden connections into visual networks. You will also learn how to secure access to protected leaks curated for journalists, activists, and researchers. It's time to power your next investigation using Aleph Pro.

REGISTER FOR AN ALEPH PRO ACCOUNT HERE: https://forms.gle/RXbPghTcLenPWuJm6

Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: Catching the quiet changes: Automating website monitoring with Visualping
sessions.description:
In this session, you'll learn how to use Visualping, an AI website change detection tool, to automatically monitor and get instant alerts for important changes to any websites you need to track for your beat, whether it's local court dockets, government agency websites, a politician's social media pages, or something else. We'll walk through setting up a Visualping alert, using different features and settings, and tips for using Visualping's AI criteria and summary features.

Attendees are welcome to sign up for a free Visualping account at visualping.io to follow along. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: 15 min break
"How Did That Make You Feel?" and Other Questions Not to Ask: Trauma-Informed Reporting
sessions.description: Trauma-informed reporting is a current industry buzzword and is often discussed as an ethical ideal. This panel will focus on what it means in practice. We’ll cover how trauma can affect memory and storytelling, how to ask questions that elicit accurate information without causing unnecessary harm, and what reporters owe sources from the first interview through post-publication. The discussion will also take on harder cases: whether trauma-informed frameworks apply when interviewing perpetrators, bystanders or officials responsible for harm, and how journalists can care for themselves while covering traumatic events.

sessions.title: Diseases and deadlines: Covering health issues on deadline and beyond
sessions.description:
Measles, mumps, rubella, oh my! Health care is driving major political and public interest debates, from measles outbreaks to broader policy fights about affordability and cost.

This panel will help reporters identify meaningful health stories and turn breaking health news into deeper enterprise and investigative reporting. Using several timely health topics, reporters from digital, magazine, and local radio outlets will discuss how they would approach the same issues on three timelines: 2 days, 2 weeks, and 2 months.

Attendees will learn how to identify breaking health stories with have real investigative potential and move from fast-turn news coverage to deeper enterprise reporting.

sessions.title: Anonymity in the Trump Era
sessions.description: Anonymous sources have always been key to what we do - but in the current administration, not only is the risk greater for them to come forward, but we become targets by utilizing them.  How do we work with those who can't be identified for fear of retribution or punishment?

sessions.title: Reporting on the U.S. military: Beyond the headlines
sessions.description: Military reporting becomes front-page news when there's a war going on. But during peace time, it's often a neglected beat -- despite US taxpayers paying $850 billion to fund the military and another $325 billion on the VA health system. The military touches all of our lives, but remains opaque to the average American. This panel session led by reporters who cover the military as their primary beat will discuss how to find behind-the-scenes stories about the US military that can influence real change, both on a political and personal level.

sessions.title: Building the team: Where everyone wants to work and no one wants to leave
sessions.description: Leading and managing a broadcast newsroom has never been harder or more important. This session digs into the core skills today’s news leaders need, offering a pragmatic, honest, and shared conversation about what it takes to build a positive, inclusive culture where people want to do their best work and stay. We’ll focus on practical systems for onboarding, developing teams, re-energizing veteran staff, setting clear expectations for behavior, embedding feedback into daily work, and understanding what’s really happening inside your newsroom — all while centering community-based journalism as a driver of healthy internal culture. You’ll leave inspired, better equipped to handle tough challenges, and prepared to intentionally shape the culture that supports both your people and your strategy.

sessions.title: OPEN MIC: An OTR Conversation about Corporate Ownership
sessions.description: Are we defined by the companies that owns our newsrooms? What does corporate ownership mean for our journalism in 2026?  Mergers and acquisitions, political affiliations - has it changed the way we do our jobs.? Off the record, an honest conversation among peers.

sessions.title: Networking: Journalists of color, sponsored by ProPublica
sessions.description:
Mix and mingle, meet friends old and new, and build your professional community in this fun and informal networking session. This session is for journalists of color. 

This session is sponsored by ProPublica. IRE retains control of content, including the topic and speaker selection, for all conference sessions.

sessions.title: Is it AI? Verifying video and other materials during breaking news
sessions.description: Description: When news breaks, one of the first things we do is head to social media to see if anyone nearby captured the moment. But how do you know if what you're seeing is real and not AI-generated? This panel will offer tips, tools and verification protocols for social media and other videos circulating on the internet.

sessions.title: Turn document dumps into promising leads with Claude
sessions.description:
Have you ever had to sort through hundreds—or even thousands—of public records to find the stories buried inside? In this hands-on session, you'll learn how to use the Claude API to sort through huge troves of documents and pull out the information you're looking for. We'll identify leads hidden in Foreign Agent Registration Act filings as a case study, and you'll walk away with a plug-and-play template that's ready to use for your own projects. 

Some coding experience is helpful for this session, but not required. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: Introducing the new IRE Resource Center
sessions.description: The new IRE Resource Center is here! Members can now access an enhanced, user-friendly search interface with more opportunities to easily surface thousands of tipsheets, contest entries and other training materials. In this session, the people behind the app will demonstrate its features, talk about how it was made and solicit live feedback from the audience.

sessions.title: Game Night
sessions.description: Have some fun with friends old and new while playing board games, cards, etc. Games and cards are provided, and feel free to bring your own. All conference attendees are welcome.

sessions.title: Recharge Room
sessions.description: Need a spot to decompress, take a load off, get your bearings or recharge your batteries? This is the room for you!

sessions.title: Registration Hours
sessions.description: Pick up your badge or register on-site: 8:00 a.m. - 2:30 p.m.

sessions.title: Adjacent Event: Journalists Recovery Network
sessions.description: The Journalists Recovery Network will meet and offer a sober space for journalists experiencing troubles with alcohol or substance use. This is an informal meeting for individuals who may struggle with substance use to meet our members and chat more about our organization. Feel free to reach out to us at journalistsrecoverynetwork@gmail.com.

sessions.title: Exhibitors
sessions.description: Stop by and visit with the exhibitors and recruiters.

sessions.title: Digging into public companies with SEC documents
sessions.description: Documents from the SEC (U.S Securities and Exchange Commission) contains a wealth of information to power your business investigations. In this panel, you'll learn about using those SEC and AI tools to find DEI commitments, and others, that public companies have quietly rolled back.

sessions.title: Investigating global health crises that hit home
sessions.description: There’s no shortage of health stories to investigate. The country is dealing with fallout from measles outbreaks, deep cuts to health care access, and misinformation about vaccine safety and other established health facts. Beyond the U.S., the abrupt “America First” demolition of USAID and withdrawal from the World Health Organization threaten gains against HIV and other diseases that cross borders. This panel will explore investigating health crises that address misinformation and link local communities to the rest of the world.

sessions.title: Covering state courts: unlocking the legal system
sessions.description: Over 98% of the U.S. court cases are found in state courthouses, where much of the country’s justice —and injustice— occurs. They're chronically under-covered compared to federal courts. But esoteric terms, archaic filing systems and unfriendly court staff can hamper access to a treasure trove of unmined stories. In this session, you’ll learn tips and tricks to navigate your local courts and a media law expert will show you how to fight for your First Amendment right to access documents and courtroom proceedings if anyone tries to (unlawfully) stop you.

sessions.title: What your editors won't tell you about reporting for print and audio
sessions.description:
Partnerships between print and audio journalists can take investigative stories to a new level. But the process can be tricky to navigate for a million logistical reasons: two time-consuming processes instead of one, two different sets of editors, and two separate mediums with diverging demands. 

In this conversational panel, you'll hear from two pairs of print and audio reporters who have successfully navigated these crosswinds together to create award-winning collaborations. They'll answer questions like: How do you know if a story is right for this kind of partnership? And once you’ve decided, how does it change the reporting process? (Spoiler alert: it changes everything!)

sessions.title: Using Google Trends data
sessions.description: An introduction to how journalists can use Google Trends for their reporting.

sessions.title: Open mic - Generation Gap, or not?
sessions.description:
Seasoned and early-career journalists lead a discussion about what we can learn from each other. 

Let's have an open and honest discussion about being new, mid-career and seasoned in the industry.

This open mic will discuss the challenges we face and how we can all learn from each other to make the industry better.

sessions.title: Beating burnout at its foundation
sessions.description: Burnout isn’t just about overworking — it’s about broken systems that force you to do everything yourself. Learn how to build burnout-proof businesses with custom systems, automation, and smarter workflows so your business runs smoothly — without running you into the ground.

sessions.title: How to make yourself a better writer
sessions.description:
What separates a solid draft from a story that truly sings?

In this interactive session, Ariel Kaminer, who has edited everything from narrative features to deep-dive investigations to M. Gessen's Pulitzer Prize winning opinion writing, will take you behind the scenes on her process.

She'll share her techniques for sharpening structure, tightening language, bringing characters to life, elevating scenes, and finding the strongest version of a story.

Kaminer is one of those editors who seems to possess a magical scalpel.  After she’s done with a reporter’s draft, she has made it sharper, clearer, more propulsive, and more readable but somehow left enough of the original style on the page that it's actually possible for a reporter to think "YES, I wrote that!" (Kendall has been subjected to the Ariel treatment and can vouch for it).

She'll also lead a live editing session using small chunks of participants' writing and talk through how to self-edit your work. You'll be left with concrete tools to help make your writing shine.

For those who would like to submit work for editing, please send 1-5 sentences max from an unpublished or published story to Kendall (kendall.taggart.journalist@gmail.com). There will also be an opportunity to submit sentences for editing during the session.

sessions.title: Making the most of OTT: How to make your investigative content stand out on streaming services
sessions.description: Investigative pieces by video journalists are no longer limited to a TRT of just a few minutes. Thanks to streaming services, reporters and producers are able to use their newsroom’s OTT platforms to tell longer and more in-depth stories, or to take audiences behind the scenes of how their investigations are made. But with so much OTT content available, how do successful journalists attract people to their work? In this session, reporters and producers share unique insights about how they’ve leveraged OTT platforms to effectively broaden their reach.

sessions.title: Managing investigators: Leading those born to challenge authority (10th Edition)
sessions.description:
Being a news manager is already tough, but what if you supervise investigative journalists? They come with an extra layer of challenges because their very job (and likely their personality) makes them hyper-alert to authority figures. This course is designed to give you some tools and tactics to lead individuals and entire teams of investigators more effectively. Learn from three investigative managers from different media at different stages of their leadership careers. How did they launch into their roles, and what experience have they gained along the way? This course is for current investigative managers and anyone aspiring to step into such a position in the future.

NEW AT IRE26: For our 10th "Managing Investigators" master class (and our 50th IRE Conference), we're bringing back a BIG group of our past instructors for even more guidance, viewpoints and support! Think of it as Managing Investigators 2.0! More individual time and leaders for coaching!

Topics will include: managing compassionately, hiring challenges, transitioning to management, forging partnerships, building relationships, handling resource cuts, organization/structure, tough decisions/conversations, in-house training/growth, delivering feedback, creating inclusive opportunities, and juggling responsibilities/projects/work.

Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.

sessions.title: Art of the investigative interview
sessions.description:
You need one final confirmation to run your story. It's the key piece of a months-long project, only one person can provide it, and you've got one shot to get it.

The entire story comes down to The Interview. Will you be ready?

We can help. Sign up for this half-day Master Class in interviewing and get a playbook for getting the information you need.

We'll cover all the crucial steps, from preparation to the conversation, confrontational interviews, talking with survivors, getting the most from witnesses, convincing those who don't want to talk with you and much more.

So the next time you dial that number, knock on that door or corner an official who’s been avoiding you, you’ll be ready.

Preregistration is required and seating is limited.

sessions.title: Finding the story: Housing data
sessions.description:
This hands-on session will teach you how to find your next housing story in data. You'll learn a practical method for predicting metro-area home price trends using Census building permit and population data. Additionally, you'll take a look at towing data and learn how it affects housing. 

Whether you're covering displacement, gentrification, or the affordable housing crisis, this session will equip you with datasets and techniques to investigate how cities are quietly reshaping who belongs in their communities.

This session is good for beginners. You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate.

sessions.title: Making news archives more useful with LLMs
sessions.description:
Some of the most valuable information news organizations have sits in their archives, mostly unused. New reporters or those just new to a beat need to know the collected wisdom from past coverage, and Large Language Models can help make that process easier. This session covers the concept of employing artificial intelligence to harness the potential of news archives and shows off some experiments with local news organizations.

Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: The paper trail: Finding local stories in national ICE arrest, detainer and detention data
sessions.description:
Millions of records. One enforcement surge. And somewhere in it all, your story. The Deportation Data Project‘s FOIA-obtained releases on ICE arrests, detentions, and detainers are among the most detailed public records ever produced on American immigration enforcement — and in this workshop, you’ll learn how to work them in R. We’ll clean, join,  and interrogate all three datasets to trace the enforcement pipeline from detainer request to detention facility, surfacing the patterns and outliers that make national data locally meaningful. You‘ll leave with working code, a reproducible workflow, and story angles you can start reporting when you get back to your newsrooms. Basic R familiarity required. 

Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.

sessions.title: Audio Coaching 1:1
sessions.description: Sign up for 30 minutes with an audio pro to use however you like: Bring tape for a critique, a pitch for workshopping or your questions about surviving and thriving in the audio journalism space.

sessions.title: Barry Nash and Company One-on-One Coaching
sessions.description:
Barry Nash and his team return to IRE to provide personalized coaching sessions. If you have questions about any aspect of your delivery — your appearance, how you use your voice, how you can perform most effectively in the field or in the studio — this is an opportunity you won’t want to miss. Barry has been working with talent at all levels of experience and ability for over 30 years. He currently authors the only blog in the world focused exclusively on television news and sports performance. You can find it on Substack
Please note: Participants should bring recent footage on a flash drive, webpage/online link, or storage drive (Google or Dropbox).                                                                                                                            Sign up for a time slot here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C0445A9AD28A5FEC34-64383836-irecoaching#/

sessions.title: 15 min break
Follow that supply chain! Uncover how questionable products end up on store shelves
sessions.description: Our planet is increasing global. Furniture from Vietnam. Cashews from Cambodia. Oysters from Vancouver. Clothing from Indonesia. Unscrupulous companies involved in environmental destruction or labor abuses may be behind brand-name goods. This panel will help you follow the supply chain for your investigation.

sessions.title: Using AI video tools to keep local government accountable
sessions.description: Thousands of local government meetings happen every week, and most newsrooms can only cover a fraction of them. A new wave of AI-powered tools is helping journalists keep tabs on far more meetings than any one reporter could track alone — transcribing recordings, identifying key moments and flagging potentially newsworthy actions. Journalists will hear from developers at newsrooms and universities who are actively building these tools, learning how they work in practice and the editorial judgment calls involved in using AI to help surface the news. For newsrooms stretched thin, the payoff is real: broader monitoring of local government frees up reporters to spend more time on the in-depth work that holds officials accountable.

sessions.title: Building trust with sources in a time of fear and retaliation
sessions.description:
Washington Post reporter Hannah Natanson became known as the "federal government whisperer" for her extraordinary ability to cultivate trust with sources across the federal workforce. Using social media, encrypted Signal chats and tireless communications, sometimes fielding hundreds of Signal messages from sources a day, she built a network of more than 1,200 confidential sources. Her reporting on the far-reaching impact of the Trump administration's Department of Government Efficiency grew out of the conversations, work that earned the 2026 Livingston Award for national reporting.

During this session, Natanson will discuss how journalists can build credibility with sources, sustain trust over the long term, and protect vulnerable sources who may fear personal or professional retaliation.

sessions.title: Investigating rail safety with data and docs
sessions.description: Learn how to investigate rail safety in your community from reporters on two award-winning projects, including a 2025 IRE winner. They will share public data, records, sources and strategies for documenting hidden risks and deadly hazards.

sessions.title: The global use of freelancers for investigative reporting
sessions.description: What is the public-interest potential of the freelance investigative reporting sector, to what extent is it being realized? What are the variables? This panel will explore to what extent, and how, could a robust freelance investigative reporting sector offset some of the economic upheaval and hollowing out — locally, regionally, nationally, globally.

sessions.title: Video show & tell: en Español with Carolina Cruz and Liz Gonzalez
sessions.description:
Sign up to “Show & Tell” us about your best work and get constructive feedback from the moderator and audience. 

Sign-ups are required for presenters. Each presenter will have 15 minutes to share their work and receive feedback. All are welcome to attend!

sessions.title: ICE, HHS, EOIR: Covering the alphabet soup of immigration agencies
sessions.description: Immigration policy has always played out across several federal agencies: Homeland Security, Labor, Health and Human Service, Justice and more. But now the Trump administration’s whole of government approach to a mass deportation agenda means everyone plays some part as agents from the FBI, DEA, ATF, the U.S. Marshals, and even the Coast Guard or the Secret Service are increasingly making street-level arrests. Join journalists covering federal agencies for a session on how the agencies they cover have played a role and how to navigate them all on deadline

sessions.title: Audio meetup
sessions.description: Whether you work in the audio space or just want to get to know the people who do, come by for a casual meet and greet with friends and colleagues.

sessions.title: Mistakes we learned from and you can too!
sessions.description: We all have success stories in investigative journalism, but it's the mistakes we make along the way that truly impact our careers for the better. These journalists share honest experiences in how they overcome them and what those missteps can teach us all.

sessions.title: Your AI research assistant: a live demo for investigative reporters
sessions.description: AI tools like ChatGPT and Claude can do a lot more than write emails and summarize articles. In this live demo, we'll put them to work on the kinds of tasks that eat up your reporting time: pulling data off a web page without writing a scraper, turning a messy PDF into a structured spreadsheet, comparing two versions of a bill or contract to find what changed, cleaning and standardizing inconsistent names or addresses, and building a quick local app to browse and search your documents. No slides, no theory. It's just a fast-paced walkthrough of real investigative tasks, with time for "can it do this?" questions from the audience.

sessions.title: Media Lawyer Q&A
sessions.description: It's no surprise that investigative journalists can run into legal roadblocks while doing their work. But who can you call when help is needed? This session features media lawyers for a practical Q&A on the issues journalists run into every day, from defamation and access fights to source protection, public records, legal pitfalls that can trip up even experienced reporters and more. Bring your toughest questions and leave with clarity on how to protect both your reporting and your newsroom.

sessions.title: 15 min break
Rethinking the crime beat
sessions.description:
Some newsrooms are rethinking traditional crime coverage; moving beyond incident driven reporting toward more holistic public safety beats. This shift reflects both audience needs and newsroom realities: declining resources, growing public skepticism of official narratives, and recognition that issues like housing instability, substance use, behavioral health, and economic conditions all shape community safety outcomes as much, if not more, than the crimes tracked by police. 

This panel will highlight journalists and outlets that have successfully revamped their crime beats and explain how they did it, from a local ABC affiliate in South Bend rethinking their nightly broadcast to AL.com's 'Beyond the Violence' beat. Panelists will share practical strategies for identifying new story angles, developing sources beyond law enforcement, using data to contextualize incidents, and building trusting relationships with the communities they cover.

sessions.title: Investigating the privatization of K-12 education
sessions.description: Publicly funded education is increasingly being offered at private schools. In Republican states across the country, sweeping school voucher programs provide taxpayer money for private school tuition and even homeschooling expenses. And in 2027, the first federal voucher program will launch, bringing vouchers to states that never had them before. Proponents tout these programs as education freedom for families, who can choose a school that serves their children best. But these programs come with a host of questions that are ripe for journalists to explore: What rules and regulations (or lack thereof) apply to private schools that receive public voucher money? What are the implications of religious school participation? Which families are benefiting most from these dollars, especially in places where vouchers are mostly paying for children who were already attending private school? What is the impact of vouchers on state budgets and existing public schools? In other jurisdictions, charter schools funded with public money have confronted opposition and scrutiny, with a lack of accountability and basic operating standards. This panel will look at this massive shift underway in K-12 education. Attendees will come away understanding the issue and its nuances and with specific stories they can report at home.

sessions.title: Mining the Epstein files
sessions.description: 'The Epstein Files' landed early this year, making public a massive assortment of more than 3 million pages of records on the notorious predator. This session will explain how news organizations have mined the Epstein Files for stories. It will also discuss the many lessons learned from this unprecedented task and explore the array of challenges in reporting on such a huge set of records.

sessions.title: A year in local investigations: Watchdog story ideas
sessions.description: Learn tricks from the stories and reporters featured in the Local Matters newsletter, which spotlights a selection of local watchdog journalism every Sunday. The journalists behind Local Matters will run through inspiring work the past year — with a focus on tactics and tips you can bring back to your own newsroom. We will feature stories from online outlets, radio, television and newspapers.

sessions.title: Editing the investigative podcast
sessions.description: Experienced editors will share their best tips on deciding when to pursue long-form audio stories, developing story arcs that grip listeners and guiding your team through creating a revelatory and impactful podcast.

sessions.title: Video show & tell: Anything goes with Erik Sandoval
sessions.description:
Sign up to “Show & Tell” us about your best work and get constructive feedback from the moderator and audience. 

Sign-ups are required for presenters. Each presenter will have 15 minutes to share their work and receive feedback. All are welcome to attend!

Sign up here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C084CA8AC29A4FBCE9-64418011-video

*These sessions will not be recorded.

sessions.title: Tips, sources and documents: How to investigate the intersection of religion, politics & culture
sessions.description: Religion is taking center stage in the Trump administration and public life. Join veteran religion beat reporters and journalists who reveal ways to develop sources and find documents to investigate corruption and controversy in religious groups and uncover public and hidden connections to political campaigns and secular society. The panel will touch on diocese bankruptcies, Christian nationalist influence over the White House, and scandals in the Catholic Church and Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. This session is presented in partnership with the Religion News Association.

sessions.title: 30 video ideas that work anywhere
sessions.description: The demand for news sometimes requires us to turn investigations fast. In 30 Ideas that work anywhere, these journalists will share true in-depth and quick turns that you can do as soon as you return to your newsroom.

sessions.title: Strategies for investigating anonymous companies
sessions.description:
This hands-on session will show you how to use publicly available information to identify who owns and benefits from companies that are set up in jurisdictions with comprehensive corporate secrecy. In an era of increasing transnational corruption, being able to publicly expose who is behind a company and the deals in which they participate will be critical for accountability journalism. While having a leak or an inside source that exposes this information would be ideal, this is not something we can rely on and there are other approaches. This training draws from years of experience piercing the veil around such secretive companies. 

Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: Google Sheets: Using string functions to manipulate data
sessions.description:
Maybe you converted a PDF or imported a table into a spreadsheet -- or maybe an agency gave you a poorly formatted file. You can use string functions to reformat your data and get your spreadsheets working for you. 

This session is good for: Anyone comfortable with using formulas and functions in Google Sheets.

You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class, and you will need a free Google account to participate.

sessions.title: Lunch
Lunch Bunch
sessions.description: Make some new friends over lunch! Show up and we'll connect you with other folks who want to chat and network over the lunch break -- a great way to make a big conference feel smaller.

sessions.title: Adjacent Event: Journalists Recovery Network
sessions.description: The Journalists Recovery Network will meet and offer a sober space for journalists experiencing troubles with alcohol or substance use. This is an informal meeting for individuals who may struggle with substance use to meet our members and chat more about our organization. Feel free to reach out to us at journalistsrecoverynetwork@gmail.com.

sessions.title: Debt collection USA: How to cover surging debt collection lawsuits near you
sessions.description: The number of debt collection lawsuits are rising nationwide, propelled by debt-buyers, credit card companies and a wide array of industries from hospitals to mortgage companies to solar panels. Our panelists will provide on-the-ground examples and data tips on how to mine courts for all kinds of stories affecting people struggling with consumer debt.

sessions.title: Investigating the child welfare system
sessions.description:
Across the US, government agencies investigate millions of families each year through what is commonly referred to as the child welfare system. Yet much of this activity functions as a form of state surveillance and intervention that operates with limited transparency and public understanding. Coverage of the system often relies on official narratives about child safety without examining the systemic factors - including poverty, housing instability, and service gaps - that drive involvement in the system or the impact of investigations and separations on children and families. 

This panel will focus on practical strategies for investigating the functioning of the child welfare system. Panelists will share how to obtain and interpret agency data, analyze removal and reporting patterns, and identify stories that go beyond individual tragedies to examine policy and structural issues. Panelists will also discuss sourcing challenges, records barriers, and ethical considerations when reporting on families and children.

sessions.title: Deep dive: The reporter and editor relationship
sessions.description: Jane Mayer is one of the best-known investigative journalists in the country, having covered the Bush administration’s torture program, the dark money flowing through politics, and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s secret history. Yet even she needs an editor. Dan Zalewski has edited Mayer’s work for decades. He is known at the New Yorker for making reporters’ work sing and pushing them to achieve the seemingly impossible. In this conversation, they’ll talk through how to make the most of the reporter and editor relationship.

sessions.title: Investigative podcast series: ambitious storytelling and winning collaborations
sessions.description:
When investigative reporters collaborate with a narrative audio team, podcast magic gets made. But how do these collaborations even begin? How do you keep a sprawling – sometimes year-long – production process on track? And why is a podcast series the right fit for your investigation? 

The Embedded podcast, NPR’s home for documentary storytelling, thrives on a model of collaboration. You’ll hear Embedded producers, editors, and reporters explain how they turn in-depth reporting into award-winning documentaries like Embedded’s Supermajority and Tested. Plus, they’ll share tips for making immersive, fact-based podcast series that keep listeners hooked from start to finish.

sessions.title: Teaching the investigation: how students can do groundbreaking investigative stories
sessions.description: Investigations done at universities are not only teaching the next generation, they are providing valuable stories for communities. Come to this session to hear how collaborations are uncovering dangerous day cares, exposing police shootings, uncovering legislators living lavishly and other landmark stories.

sessions.title: OPEN MIC: Going Independent - What Does That Mean?
sessions.description: For those just starting or those starting over - what does it take to go independent?  There are many definitions of the "independent journalist" these days.  Is it starting a Substack that actually succeeds? Freelancing? Let's talk finances and legalities, too.

sessions.title: Resources for local investigations: Tips for a winning pitch (and what not to do)
sessions.description: Come hear from organizations that are providing front-line help to local newsrooms: Report for America's partnership with Investigative Editing Corps; New York Times's Local Investigations Fellowship and ProPublica's Local Reporting Fellowship. You will leave this session with information on what each program can mean for you and your newsroom, and how you can fine-tune a winning pitch.

sessions.title: Lightning talks
sessions.description:
The power of five sessions packed into one hour. Come listen to some broadcast pros teach you tricks of the trade in this rapid-fire lightning talk.

1. How to be a self-made promotions team when yours is only focused on weather.
2. How to pitch your investigative story to your managers.
3. Track federal lawwsuits like an award-winning pro.
4. Landing and managing the high-stakes interview.
5. Make your own luck when you need an "MOS" interview.

Eric Flack is a Murrow, Emmy and AP Award winning Chief Investigative Reporter at WUSA and he'll tell you that (even with all those accolades) it isn't easy to get his stories properly promoted.

Walter Smith Randolph is executive producer of investigations at CBS News New York and was a was a reporter and anchor for 15 years. Randolph will teach you how to pitch your investigative idea to your managers.

Fallon Gallagher is an award-winning legal affairs reporter for MSNBC and will teach you how to track federal cases like a pro.

Karen Ye is an award-winning journalist with extensive experience producing both long-form documentaries and daily news reports across the U.S. and Asia. Ye will break down how to land and manage the high-stakes interview.

David Hodges is an award-winning investigative reporter at WBTV who loathes having to get MOS interviews. Hodges will teach you how to use government records and data to make your MOS meaningful.

sessions.title: LGBTQ+ Data Reporting and Visualization
sessions.description:
At a moment when misinformation, anti-LGBTQ+ laws, and attacks on trans people are all on the rise, it is crucial for journalists to find and collect critically needed data and tell stories that cover the LGBTQ+ community fairly and accurately. 

In this session, learn about how to work with key data (and work around limitations like undercounts of gender-nonconforming people) to create visualizations, find underreported stories, and power impactful investigations.

sessions.title: From the idea to the prototype with Google AI Tools
sessions.description:
In this hands-on workshop we will do a deep dive into Google’s AI tools —including NotebookLM, and Gemini, among others—designed specifically for complex news workflows. We’ll move from prompting 101 to developing simple, functional prototypes and custom research agents you can deploy immediately. Prefer to learn by observing? You are completely welcome to just come and watch the build process!

Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.  Participants must have a Google account, as well as Gemini and NotebookLM.

sessions.title: Google Sheets: Advanced pivot tables
sessions.description:
You've done a few pivot tables and are getting curious about what more you could do with them. What happens if you aggregate by more than one column? What are those "column" and "filter" boxes for? Come unlock the full potential of pivot tables in this intermediate spreadsheet class.

This session is good for: People familiar with spreadsheets and aggregating data with pivot tables, or anyone who has taken Sheets 1-3.

You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class, and you will need a free Google account to participate.

sessions.title: Turn your questions into data with an LLM
sessions.description:
Learn how journalists at Bloomberg Industry Group used a large language model to turn questions into data as part of investigations into federal civil rights cases involving restraints, babies being born in jail and children being strip searched at school.

This session is good for people with some experience working with data. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training. You will also need to sign up for a Gemini account to participate in the hands-on exercise.

sessions.title: Master the power of mapping with QGIS
sessions.description:
Mapping is a powerful tool to find patterns in data that can only be explored on a map. This session will give you the basics for doing mapping with the open-source tool QGIS.  You'll learn how to build thematic maps, how to bring outside data into QGIS and connect it to your map and how to combine data in a map to look at geographic relationships in your data.
This class is good for those with solid spreadsheet skills ready to move into mapping. 

You will need a laptop. Please download and install the long-term release of QGIS for your operating system (3.44.10 Solothurn LTR) at qgis.org/download. Please note that the LTR is not the latest release, but the most current stable release.

Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.

sessions.title: Office Hours: 1:1 career meetings with newsroom leaders & hiring managers
sessions.description:
Looking for specific feedback on a cover letter or portfolio piece? Maybe you want to wordsmith a section of your resume or update your bio. Sign up for a 20-min one-on-one session with a manager or newsroom leader with expertise in data, editing, investigations, career pathways and more. 

Sign up now for a 20-min Office Hour session with an IRE volunteer mentor coach. https://forms.gle/NJfuoLZC4iMYDcHT6 

Matches will be made on a rolling basis!

You can get FREE expert advice on:

A resume
A cover letter
A clip or package  
A portfolio
A sticky career question
A job hunt strategy 


Please note, you MUST be available in person, during Saturday of IRE26, from 3-5 PM.

sessions.title: 15 min break
Scams and scammers: Investigating the fastest-growing crime in the world
sessions.description:
Scam attempts are no longer poorly spelled missives from a prince in west Africa. Today one of the largest crimes in the world is showing up in text messages, popping onto social media, ripping off friends and neighbors. 
This session equips investigative journalists with practical methods to identify, document, and expose modern scam ecosystems. Drawing on real reporting examples, (and there’s one in every town), we’ll walk through how to spot early warning signs, emotional manipulation methods, digital footprints, follow money flows, and try to connect individual victims to larger fraud networks through blockchain analysis.
Attendees will learn how to use open-source tools, court records, platform data, and victim interviews to build airtight investigations; how to safely engage with scammers without compromising security; and how to collaborate across newsrooms and borders when scams scale internationally. The session will also address ethical and legal considerations, including source protection, digital safety, and avoiding re-victimization.
Designed for reporters at any stage of experience, this workshop focuses on turning scattered complaints into high-impact accountability journalism that exposes who profits, who enables the fraud, and why regulators and platforms so often fail to stop it.

sessions.title: Protecting your sources, devices, and work in a new era of press scrutiny
sessions.description: The landscape for investigative reporting has fundamentally changed in the wake of the federal government’s unprecedented search warrant targeting a reporter’s home and electronic devices earlier this year, requiring journalists to reassess their source communication practices, digital security habits, and risk profile. This session will equip journalists with skills and knowledge to safely work on sensitive investigations today. Hear from media attorneys and investigative journalists about the core legal protections for journalists’ work product, as well as practical steps reporters can take to safeguard their newsgathering materials.

sessions.title: Fact-checking for reporters
sessions.description: Most newsrooms can no longer afford to spend resources on fact checking, leaving them exposed to potential corrections, public embarrassment, or even litigation. Given the current political climate and the administration's aggressive stance toward watchdog journalism, it's more important than ever to learn how to vet your own work and protect yourself. This session, led by Type Investigations Research Editor Nina Zweig, will teach reporters how to self-check their work and approach their reporting with an eye toward fact checking.

sessions.title: Building blocks of leadership
sessions.description:
Is your team clicking together like a perfectly built structure or clunking along like mismatched pieces? Strong collaboration and communication are the foundation of any high-performing team, and in this interactive session, you’ll discover how to strengthen both through the power of play.
Using building blocks as both metaphor and hands-on tool, we’ll explore what it takes to build trust, align around shared goals and adapt to change. You’ll gain fresh insights into team dynamics, leadership styles and the invisible structures that support effective teamwork. Come ready to build, reflect and leave with practical strategies for assembling a more connected, resilient team: one brick at a time.

sessions.title: Behind the story on the accessibility beat
Show and Tell for early career journalists/MMJ's
sessions.description:
Sign up to “Show & Tell” us about your best work and get constructive feedback from the moderator and audience. Sign-ups are required for presenters. Each presenter will have 15 minutes to share their work and receive feedback. All are welcome to attend!

Sign up here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C0548AAAF29AAF4C70-64533545-show

sessions.title: Investigating the aftermath of climate disasters
sessions.description:
The last few years have demonstrated the devastation of climate change-fueled disasters. While much reporting is focused on the immediate aftermath of these disasters, resources and attention fade quickly. But it's in the months and years after a disaster where investigative journalism is the most crucial -- examining whether systems set up to support recovery are truly working and what can be done to prepare for the next natural disaster. 

This panel brings together reporters who have covered wildfires in Los Angeles and Maui, hurricanes in Florida and North Carolina and flooding in Texas to discuss building trust with sources who have experienced trauma, finding accountability angles in a rapidly developing situation and tackle emerging issues around safety and recovery given the ever evolving nature of climate disasters.

sessions.title: AI tools you can use
From chaos to clarity: Organizing your reporting and building effective story structure
sessions.description: In a world of juggling multiple projects across all platforms, organization and impact matter. This session will go over routines, tools and tips to keep your reporting organized from the start -- and from there we'll help you develop best practices for building effective story structure to maximize the impact of your work on-air, online and social media.

sessions.title: Bringing data and records to life in audio
sessions.description: The data that journalists work with is often messy, and they rarely provide us with clear-cut or sweeping conclusions. How can we bring complicated numbers, especially in finance and science, to the listeners in a way that makes sense? How can we unearth public recordings that are often never heard? Panelists will share their strategies for breaking down complex concepts, finding the human stories behind the data, and crafting compelling audio narratives with public records.

sessions.title: Google Sheets 1: Getting started with spreadsheets (repeat)
sessions.description:
In this introduction to spreadsheets, you'll begin analyzing data with Google Sheets, a simple but powerful tool. You'll learn how to enter data, navigate spreadsheets and conduct simple calculations like sum, average and median. 

This session is good for: Data beginners.

You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class, and you will need a free Google account to participate.

sessions.title: Charts for small newsrooms
sessions.description:
Charts for small newsrooms

Just because you're in a small newsroom doesn't mean you can't make great, informative charts! Even a little bit of Excel can take you far. 

In this session, we'll show you how to quickly make a great graphic by yourself, using publicly available election numbers to make simple but informative graphics. We'll also go over some principles of good graphic design to make sure your charts are communicating what they need to, and how to export charts for print or web publication.

Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: 15 min break
Lessons from the best international investigative and data projects
sessions.description: Now host to both the Global Shining Light Award - recognizing investigations from perilous places – and the SIGMA global data journalism award, GIJN recently announced the 4 winners for the former, and 10 for the latter. In this session, judges, organizers and winners of those competitions will share some of the methodologies that inspired them, and takeaways from these foreign stories that could benefit US journalists.

sessions.title: Structuring successful partnerships: Professional newsrooms and university investigative programs
sessions.description: University investigative journalism program leaders and their professional newsroom collaborators will talk about how to build successful partnerships to publish Pulitzer-finalist-quality investigative projects produced by students and pros. Attendees will walk away with concrete tips on how to develop and manage collaborations between experienced professionals and early-career reporters.

sessions.title: Covering power when power pushes back: lessons from international journalists
sessions.description:
Across the world, journalists have spent years reporting under governments that intimidate reporters, surveil sources, weaponize legal systems, and attempt to discredit independent media. As political hostility toward the press intensifies in the United States, investigative reporters can learn valuable strategies from colleagues who have long navigated these pressures. 

This panel, organized by the North American Committee of the International Press Institute, a 75-year-old free press organization, explores what American investigative reporters and accountability journalists can learn from colleagues who have worked under sustained government pressure around the world, particularly Latin America.

Panelists with firsthand experience covering Venezuela, Mexico, and Nicaragua will share lessons learned from those environments—including techniques for cultivating government sources, protecting sensitive communications, anticipating legal threats, and continuing accountability reporting when officials attempt to intimidate journalists or their sources.

The panel will focus on practical reporting techniques and source-protection strategies reporters can immediately apply in their own investigative work. Attendees will leave with concrete strategies used by journalists around the world to keep reporting—even when governments push back.

sessions.title: Break into prison coverage: How to report on federal prisons, no matter your beat
sessions.description: This session will cover how to report on federal prisons. Many outlets’ coverage areas include or intersect with federal prisons, but not all outlets have reporters who cover incarceration exclusively. Federal prisons can also be intimidating to reporters of all experience-levels; they are a black box of bureaucratic tape and obstacles. The goal of this panel is to teach reporters and editors across a spectrum of beats and experience level how to incorporate federal prison coverage into their newsroom.

sessions.title: Investigative stories you can feel: Engaging audiences through voices
sessions.description: How can investigative journalism create lasting impact and resonate with audiences. This panel explores how to craft stories that reveal deep truths while keeping listeners  engaged.This panel explores the art of navigating liminal spaces, storytelling that exists as both hard-hitting exposé and character-driven narrative, and what it means to bring the panelists own histories and perspectives to an investigation. What does personal point of view unlock in terms of nuance and humanity, and where do the challenges lie?

sessions.title: Digging through the archives
sessions.description: Some of the best scoops have originated from spending time digging through archival records in collections held by universities, libraries and other institutions. This panel will discuss how reporters can use archival materials to produce high-impact investigations and also add depth to reporting on a wide range of beats. Participants will come away with a practical toolkit for identifying relevant archival materials, navigating finding aids, and understanding document scanning tools that can assist with research. Specific types of archival records  that can be used for health, environmental and city land use investigations also will be highlighted.

sessions.title: Deep dive: Empathy as an indispensable part of investigative reporting
sessions.description: Robert Samuels, a national enterprise reporter for the Washington Post, has written about poverty, refugees, systemic racism—subjects, all, that center people confronting forces that can be unrelenting and inhumane. He co-authored “His Name Is George Floyd,” winner of the Pulitzer Prize for general nonfiction. Lizzie Presser, a reporter on ProPublica’s national staff, has written about racial disparities in amputations and dispossession of Black landowners in the South. Her work on preventable deaths following state abortion bans won the Pulitzer Prize for public service. For both, a signature of their powerful, considerate work is empathy.

sessions.title: When video broke open ICE investigations
sessions.description: From citizen deaths at the hands of federal agents to fast-paced detentions and deportations carried out without due process, video has become one of the most powerful tools journalists have to investigate immigration enforcement. In this panel, reporters covering ICE in their own communities will discuss how they find and verify video to break news, use it to challenge narratives, and report responsibly when footage is the only evidence that exists—especially when it contradicts the government’s account. Panelists will also share strategies for protecting vulnerable and undocumented sources and uncovering what's happening in neighborhoods across the country as human stories, not just national headlines.

sessions.title: Networking: Women
sessions.description: Mix and mingle, meet friends old and new, and build your professional community in this fun and informal networking session. This session is for people who identify as women.

sessions.title: Google Sheets 2: Formulas & sorting (repeat)
sessions.description:
Much of Google Sheets' power comes in the form of formulas. In this class, you'll learn how to use them to analyze data with the eye of a journalist. Yes, math will be involved, but it's totally worth it! This class will show you how calculations like change, percent change, rates and ratios can beef up your reporting.

This session is good for: Anyone who has taken Google Sheets 1 or has been introduced to spreadsheets.

You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class, and you will need a free Google account to participate.

sessions.title: Finding the story: Covering public utilities
sessions.description: Public utility coverage state regulatory agencies were historically sleepy and obscure. But now, they're at the center of some of the most contentious issues journalists cover, including data centers, utility affordability and climate change. Wildfires, AI, climate change, healthcare, and affordability crisis coverage could all be improved with knowledge about how public utilities -- particularly water and power utilities operate.  This hands-on session will share potential storylines and datasets to explore to find your next scoop.

sessions.title: IRE Membership Meeting
sessions.description: Open to all IRE members.

sessions.title: Make the most of a mentor/mentee relationship Sponsored by San Antonio Assoc of Hispanic Journalists
sessions.description: Being a mentor or a mentee in journalism can be a life-changing experience and a great path to career growth. It's important to have a relationship that includes honesty, respect and intent. This panel will have two mentors and two mentees sharing advice, reflections and lessons on how to make it successful. Learn how to build impactful mentorships that can help broaden your journalism networks, learn about jobs and opportunities and encourage career growth.

sessions.title: New tools for this investigative moment
sessions.description: This panel will offer new practical tools from the US and around the world for urgent investigative needs. In this session, panelists will profile a tool that reduces the stakes for whistleblowers; the new Project Salt Box tracker to investigate migrant detention procurement; and updated OCCRP tools to investigate cross-border asset looting by authoritarians and their allies.

sessions.title: Google Sheets: Pivot tables
sessions.description: Come to this hands-on class to learn how to use pivot tables in Google Sheets and make your data analysis faster and easier. This session is good for everyone who understands the basics of data journalism and has a beginner's knowledge of Google Sheets. Bring your computer!

sessions.title: Finding the story: the youth mental health crisis
sessions.description:
School databases can provide a window into the youth mental health crisis, if reporters know how to analyze them.

This hands-on session will show you how to use school data as the foundation for accountability reporting on student safety and mental health, including how to analyze statewide incident data, rank districts by incident categories and identify patterns, spikes and outliers that warrant deeper investigation.

The session will also cover how to look beyond the numbers through public records requests, interviews with families and advocates and reporting on undercounting or misclassification of incidents.

Attendees will leave with practical techniques to analyze state school datasets, identify districts where incidents are rising or disproportionately reported, and investigate potential systemic failures. 

This session is good for beginners. You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate.

sessions.title: Introduction to R and RStudio
sessions.description:
Jump into data analysis with R, the powerful open-source programming language. In this class we’ll cover R fundamentals and learn our way around the RStudio interface for using R.

This session is good for: People with a basic understanding of data analysis who are ready to go beyond spreadsheets. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets).

sessions.title: Introduction to Python
sessions.description:
A free, three-hour crash course on how journalists are using the Python programming language to solve problems. We'll cover data analysis, web scraping and other common newsroom tasks.

Participants will need a laptop (no tablets) and a free Google account.

sessions.title: 15 min break
Climate disinformation reporting and databases
sessions.description:
Join Global Research Director of Desmog Jamie Corey and Investigative Climate Reporter Miranda Green in a talk about the history of climate disinformation and how it's morphed from Exxon Knew obfuscation to blatant misinformation in pink slime newspapers. 

The panelists will discuss how they've discovered and covered a variety of climate disinformation stories and traced the dark money funding these efforts. They'll share the databases and resources they used to help other reporters pursue their own deep dives into the organizations funding pro-oil and gas messaging, lobbying local governments to pass pro-fossil fuel measures and publishing paid partisan news that attacks clean energy.

sessions.title: Investigating the southern U.S. border
sessions.description: Learn from panelists with experience reporting from the ground on immigration and the United States' southern border. The panelists will discuss the history of the border region, shifting immigration laws under Trump, and strategies for producing investigations about one of the most important regions of the US and the international geopolitical landscape.

sessions.title: Taking it all home
sessions.description: You've just spent the past three days absorbing as much information as possible and now it's time to go home and put it all to use. But where do you start? And how do you not immediately lose all that knowledge you just gained? Come to this session to get simple tips and tricks to bring it all home and put it to good use. You'll also learn how IRE can help you ride the conference wave long after National Harbor.

sessions.title: Turning documents Into data with AI
sessions.description:
Investigative reporters drown in documents, from PDFs of court filings, inspection reports, and financial disclosures that contain structured information trapped in unstructured formats. In this session, we'll use AI tools to extract usable data from real-world documents and then build a simple, local web interface you can use to browse and search the results. No server or programming experience required — we'll walk through "vibecoding" the tool step by step using AI to write the code for us. You'll leave with a repeatable workflow for turning a stack of documents into a searchable dataset you can actually report from.

Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: Under the Influence: Digging through federal lobbying records
sessions.description:
The business of lobbying is big in Washington, and federal lobbyists are required to file periodic reports that offer reporters a window into the forces and characters working to influence policy and policymakers. Come learn how to access and use the Lobbying Disclosure Act and Foreign Agents Registration Act databases to explore the billions of dollars going toward influence peddling each year and how to build a story beyond the filings.

Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: 15 min break
Quick-hit investigations
sessions.description: Learn strategies, story ideas, resources and tips for watchdog stories on a variety of beats. You'll come away with this session with a long list of potential meaty enterprise stories to produce in days or weeks rather than months.

sessions.title: Career roundtable: How to survive and thrive in your newsroom
sessions.description: Join us for a conversation about how to navigate the journalism job hunt, build relationships with mentors and set yourself up for success in an industry that can often feel daunting. We'll chat about our paths to our current roles and be on hand to answer your questions, big and small.

sessions.title: Finding the story with federal data: transit crime and safety
sessions.description:
Finding the story with federal data: transit crime and safety

Do you cover a city with an active transit system? Do people in your community have a lot of opinions about the safety of the system? Are there ever any breaking news incidents about safety or security on your transit system? Then this session is for you. The Federal Transit Administration keeps several extremely detailed databases that you can use to compare your system to others around the nation, so you can objectively report what's a problem and what's not. Data reporter Caitlin McGlade will guide you through what's out there and how you can use it.

sessions.title: No-code automations for investigations
sessions.description:
Learn how to use Zapier, a tool that helps you incorporate automation into daily workflows and newsgathering processes, no code necessary. You might be interested in automatically updating databases daily, or using automation to send out newsletters. Learn about uses for journalism and investigations.

Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

sessions.title: Detangling federal lobbying disclosures with LobbyR
sessions.description:
Political influence is getting harder and harder to track, but the Lobbying Disclosure Act Reports helps cut through some of the noise. With LobbyR, researchers, journalists, and others can easily access, clean, and visualize a much more comprehensive version of federal lobbying disclosures to understand what companies, groups and other interests are asking Congress and the administration for.  

In this session, you’ll learn how just a few lines of R code will allow you to query, summarize, and plot real-world lobbying transparency data, enabling rapid insights and automated workflows. And there's no complex setup required.

If you want to ask questions like “who lobbied on issue X in the last five years?”, or “how many dollars did registrant Y report?”, or “are there clients/registrants filing that overlap?”, LobbyR makes this work programmatic, reproducible, and transparent.

Participants should have a basic knowledge of R. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets). Participants should pre-register for an API key through this link: https://lda.gov/api/register/
