speakers.bio: A.J. Lagoe is managing editor of investigations for KARE-TV in Minneapolis. His reporting routinely leads to criminal convictions, legislative and congressional hearings, along with new state and federal laws. A.J. is a multi-time recipient of journalism’s highest honors including Peabody, duPont Columbia and IRE awards.
Aaron Adelson helps create investigative content for KUSA-TV in Denver, CO. He has previously worked as a News Director and investigative reporter.

Aaron Kessler is an award-winning journalist specializing in investigative reporting and data science at the intersection of business and politics. He has 20 years of experience as a reporter, data journalist and newsroom innovator covering a range of subjects including politics, health care, finance, technology, transportation and housing. He currently works for the The Associated Press, based in Washington, DC.
speakers.tagLine: The Associated Press, Investigative Data Journalist

speakers.bio: Aaron Leibowitz is a local government reporter for the Miami Herald. He helped lead an investigation into the shocking rate of people being struck and killed by Brightline trains that was named a 2026 Pulitzer Prize Finalist for Local Reporting and won an IRE Award. He also contributed to the Herald’s coverage of the Surfside condo collapse in 2021 that won the Pulitzer Prize for Breaking News Reporting.

Aaron Patton helps legal teams and investigative partners leverage eDiscovery technology to navigate complex litigation and high-stakes investigations. Translation: find signal in the noise of document dumps. Drawing on over 20 years of experience across legal practice and consulting, Aaron brings a practitioner’s eye to technology adoption—focusing on streamlined processes and the practical application of AI-assisted review. Aaron is also the creator of the popular Gates Dogfish eDiscovery meme series on LinkedIn and writes the eDiscoveryPromptVault.com weekly newsletter.

Aaron is a partner in the Investigations and Criminal Litigation Group at the law firm WilmerHale, focusing on investigations and national security matters. He is a former federal prosecutor and has served in multiple roles at the US Department of Justice (DOJ) and the FBI. Aaron served as the Deputy Special Counsel; FBI Chief of Staff; Senior Counselor in DOJ’s National Security Division; and Assistant US Attorney in the National Security Unit in the Eastern District of Virginia prosecuting espionage and other crimes. Aaron started his government career as an FBI agent investigating international terrorism. Aaron is currently a Distinguished Fellow at the Karsh Center for Law and Democracy, and he has received the Attorney General's Award for Excellence in Furthering the Interests of US National Security, among others.

Aarón Torres has covered immigration at the state and federal level since 2023. He was at the border on Inauguration Day in 2024 and wrote about the CBP One mobile application being shut down. In 2025, he covered ICE's arrests at courthouses, interviewed individuals at detention centers and the agency's expansion effort.

Adam A. Marshall is the director of national litigation at the Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press. His work includes litigation in federal and state courts and training journalists on government transparency. 

Adam is the author of numerous articles and book chapters on government transparency. He is also a recipient of the President’s Volunteer Service Award and the GW Law Pro Bono Service Recognition Award. In 2017, Adam was named to the Forbes “30 Under 30: Media” list for his work promoting government transparency, including the development of the FOIA Wiki. And in 2026, he was inducted in the FOIA Hall of Fame. 

Adam is an alumnus of The George Washington University Law School, Kalamazoo College, and  the London School of Economics. 

You can find Adam on Bluesky at https://bsky.app/profile/a-marshall-plan.bsky.social
speakers.tagLine: Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press

speakers.bio: Adam Marton is a data journalist, senior lecturer at The Philip Merrill College of Journalism at The University of Maryland and director of the Capital News Service Data & Graphics bureau. He previously worked at The Baltimore Sun as the senior editor of data and graphics. His work on police accountability has twice been named as a Pulitzer Prize finalist: in 2024 for Lethal Restraint and in 2015 for the 45 Minute Mystery of Freddie Gray’s Death.
speakers.tagLine: Capital News Service Data and Graphics Bureau / University of Maryland

speakers.bio: Adam Rhodes is an award winning Latine journalist whose work primarily focuses on queer people and the criminal legal system. Their recent work has examined anti-LGBTQ+ bias in dozens of death penalty cases, HIV criminalization laws around the country, anti-trans disinformation, and more. At IRE, they manage the Total Newsroom Training Program, aimed at small-and-mid-sized newsrooms, and also oversee IRE's online publication, the IRE Journal.
speakers.tagLine: Investigative Reporters & Editors, Training Director and Managing Editor

speakers.bio: Adam Ganucheau is the executive editor and chief content officer of Deep South Today, a nonprofit network of independent newsrooms in Louisiana and Mississippi. Previously, he served as editor-in-chief of Mississippi Today. He was the lead editor of the 2023 Livingston Award and Pulitzer Prize-winning investigation "The Backchannel," which exposed high-profile players' roles in the state's welfare scandal.
speakers.tagLine: Executive editor and chief content officer, Deep South Today

speakers.bio:
Adelina Lancianese is senior producer for the Embedded podcast, NPR's home for audio documentary storytelling.

--FULL BIO TK--

Adriana Navarro is a data journalist at the Arizona Center for Investigative Reporting, where she covers the intersection of gender, politics and public policy. Previously, Adriana worked on an award-winning investigative series examining privately sponsored travel and lobbying loopholes in Congress. Her work has appeared in The Baltimore Banner, Politico and The Washington Post.
speakers.tagLine: Investigative and Data Journalist, Arizona Center for Investigative Reporting

speakers.bio: Graduate of the CUNY Craig Newmark School of Journalism. Freelance journalist. Lawyer.

Aidan Hughes is a data journalist at the University of Maryland's Howard Center for Investigative Journalism. A former fellow at the Howard Center, Hughes has helped lead a variety of investigative and data projects including an Investigative Reporters and Editors Award-winning series on privately sponsored travel for members of Congress.
speakers.tagLine: Data Journalist, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism

speakers.bio: Ainsley Martinez covers politics and Brooklyn for Patch. An Oklahoma City native, Martinez previously worked as an editor and head of data visualizations at the Long Island Herald, and reported for Oklahoma Watch, an investigative nonprofit, and The Oklahoman. She earned a bachelor’s degree in Professional Media from the University of Central Oklahoma and a professional certification in data journalism and visualization from Columbia Journalism School’s LEDE Program.

Alaina Bookman is a reporter covering Birmingham metro area news. She joined AL.com in 2023 as a Report for America corps member and violence prevention reporter covering efforts to improve safety and well being through AL.com's "Beyond the Violence" project. In 2024, Bookman covered the effects of violence on youth mental health as a Solutions Journalism Network Fellow. She also covered Alabama's child care crisis through stories that garnered national attention. In 2025, she led AL.com's Making Birmingham Better series, highlighting community development efforts. She is a Dallas native and graduated from the University of Texas at Austin with a bachelor's degree in journalism.
Investigative editor on the Business Desk at The New York Times. Formerly at Bloomberg News, BuzzFeed News and The Indianapolis Star.
speakers.tagLine: Business Investigations Editor, The New York Times

speakers.bio:
Alexandria Burris is an investigative reporter at the Indianapolis Star. She began working at the newspaper as a business reporter in 2019, covering real estate, the tech sector, labor and the economy. Since joining IndyStar's investigative team, she's written about church sexual abuse, Christian nationalism, child labor, immigration and the demise of a high-profile tech bootcamp.

Alexandria is a graduate of Louisiana State University's Manship School of Mass Communication and Emerson College in Boston.

Alexia Fernández Campbell is an investigative journalist at Bloomberg Industry Group. She previously worked as a staff writer for the Center for Public Integrity, Vox, The Atlantic, National Journal and the South Florida Sun-Sentinel. She is a Pulitzer finalist and duPont winner for her historical investigation of the 40 Acres and a Mule program. A 2020 investigation she published about the federal government's failure to respond to anti-Asian hate crimes led to passage of the COVID-19 Hate Crimes Act. Alexia’s work has also been honored by the Society of Professional Journalists, Investigative Reporters and Editors, The Association of Business Journalists, The Alliance for Women in Media and the Asian American Journalists Association. 

Alexia holds a bachelor's degree in mass communication from the University of Tennessee and a master's degree in journalism and public affairs from American University. She is Mexican, American and Brazilian and is fluent in Spanish and Portuguese.
Aline has covered immigration in Maryland through the Howard Center for Investigative Journalism. She was able to contribute data analysis and visualizations to the center's projects.

Alison is a freelance story editor with more than 25 years of experience in audio news and narrative storytelling. She currently edits the investigative podcast In the Dark (New Yorker). Previous work includes The 13th Step (NHPR), NPR's Embedded, Believed (NPR/Michigan Radio), 544 Days (Crooked Media/Spotify), The Heist (Center for Public Integrity), Code Switch (NPR), Land of the Giants (Vox), and The New Yorker Radio Hour. Previously, she spent 15 years at NPR, where she was Senior Editor of All Things Considered and NPR's audio storytelling trainer. She got her start as a producer at WBUR in Boston. Stories she edited have won the Peabody, Murrow, and duPont Columbia awards, and The 13th Step was a Pulitzer Prize finalist.

Alison Young is senior national reporter at Healthbeat, where she covers public health issues, including the work of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Young is a past president of IRE’s board of directors and has reported on health, science, consumer and environmental issues for national and regional news organizations, including USA Today, The Atlanta Journal-Constitution and the Detroit Free Press. She also has been a professor at the Missouri School of Journalism and led its Washington Program. Her reporting honors include National Press Club Awards, Scripps Howard Awards, Gerald Loeb Awards, the Hillman Prize, and an Alfred I. duPont-Columbia University Award. She is the author of Pandora’s Gamble: Lab Leaks, Pandemics, and a World at Risk, which was named to Kirkus Reviews’ Best Books of 2023.

I joined ProPublica’s South unit in 2021, after spending several years covering public education and state politics at The Texas Tribune. My reporting in Texas exposed school officials criminalizing students for vaping, highlighted the state’s role in remote learning failures and drew attention to mental health challenges among young children. My series on the legacy of school segregation in Texas was a Livingston Award finalist. I also won a prize in the 2020 National Awards for Education Reporting for beat reporting that investigated the impact of the coronavirus on Texas public schools. 

My reporting at ProPublica, in collaboration with WPLN News, about Tennessee youth being wrongly arrested and expelled for alleged threats of mass violence won a prize in the 2024 National Awards for Education Reporting. In the aftermath of the project, the legislature passed a fix to the school threats law.

Allya Davidson is a multiple Emmy, RTDNA and Canadian Screen Award-winning investigative journalist. She is the executive producer of CBC News’ flagship investigative documentary program, the fifth estate, which will enter its 52nd season in the fall. Allya has produced documentaries for VICE and CTV's W5, and internationally for Channel 4 (UK), ZDF (Germany), Four Corners (Australia) and PBS Frontline. She is passionate about mentoring BIPOC journalists and advancing equity in the field. Allya gave the inaugural Al Hamilton Lecture at Toronto Metropolitan University in the Spring of 2024; her lecture’s contents are now part of first-year journalism syllabi at TMU. She was recognized with the McGill University Alumni Association Impact Award in 2024.
speakers.tagLine: Executive producer, the fifth estate, Canadian Broadcasting Corporation

speakers.bio: Alyssa Katz is the editor-in-chief of the Forward. Katz is an award-winning journalist who worked at The City from 2019 to 2026, first as Deputy Editor and then as Executive Editor. In these roles, she managed the investigative reporting team while guiding coverage of labor, housing, politics, government and social services, and leading interactive projects and data investigations. Prior to joining The City, Katz served in editorial roles at the New York Daily News, The Village Voice and other publications.

Amanda Becker is The 19th’s national reporter. She joined the nonprofit newsroom focused on gender, politics and policy in 2020 as its founding Washington correspondent. She is now based in Ohio and West Virginia.

Becker has covered national politics and issues for nearly two decades, including stints at the White House and in Congress, oral arguments at the Supreme Court, four presidential elections and scores of House and Senate campaigns. 

Before joining The 19th, Becker spent more than seven years at Reuters, where she was embedded with Hillary Clinton’s presidential campaign, and several before that at CQ Roll Call, where she covered lobbying, congressional ethics and influence.

Becker was a 2023 fellow at the Nieman Foundation for Journalism at Harvard University. Her first book, “You Must Stand Up: The Fight for Abortion Rights in Post-Dobbs America,” was published in September 2024.

Investigative reporter Amy Brittain joined The Washington Post in 2013 and has specialized in coverage related to criminal justice and sexual assault, harassment and misconduct. Her podcast “Canary” was named by Apple Podcasts as one of the top 12 podcasts of 2020 and won the 2021 Robert F. Kennedy Journalism Award and the Edward R. Murrow Award. She was part of a team of Post reporters to win the 2016 Pulitzer Prize in National Reporting for a groundbreaking database effort to track fatal police shootings. She has also been awarded a George Polk Award for reporting on steroid abuse by law enforcement, a Mirror Award for breaking the story of sexual misconduct by TV host Charlie Rose and a James Beard Award for investigative reporting. She was twice named a finalist for the Livingston Awards. Brittain studied journalism at Louisiana State University and at the Stabile Center for Investigative Journalism at Columbia University, where she earned her master’s degree.

Anastassia “Stassi” Olmos is an award-winning investigative reporter with Atlanta News First Investigates, specializing in accountability, consumer, and wide-impact reporting. A multimedia journalist who shoots and edits most of her own work, Olmos has reported in major markets including Los Angeles, Tampa, Nashville, and Atlanta. Her work has covered high-profile issues such as Florida’s property insurance crisis, the Murdaugh boat crash, and major protests in Los Angeles.

Olmos is known for turning complex investigations into accessible journalism across broadcast, digital, and social platforms. Her recent “HOA Nightmares” series brought statewide attention to alleged homeowners association abuses in Georgia, helped drive legislative reform, and earned recognition including a regional Edward R. Murrow Award, an Atlanta Press Club award, and a Southeast Regional Emmy nomination.

A Los Angeles native, Olmos earned a bachelor’s from USC and a master’s from Columbia Journalism School.

Andrea Arzaba wears two hats at GIJN: she is the Digital Threats project director and also the organization's Spanish editor.

A bilingual journalist, she has dedicated her career to portray stories from the Latinx community. Her work has appeared in media outlets such as National Geographic, Proceso, Animal Politico and NAHJ’s Palabra, among others.

Andrea Castillo covers federal immigration policy and its human consequences from Washington, D.C. She joined the newspaper in 2017, initially covering immigration from Los Angeles. Castillo’s reporting focuses on enforcement, detention, the legal immigration system and the political battles shaping federal and local changes.

Andy Lehren is investigations editor for Mongabay, a leading global environmental news organization. He is director of investigative reporting at CUNY’s Graduate School of Journalism. On the IRE Board of Directors. Former investigative reporter for The New York Times and NBC News. Won 100+ journalism honors, including Polk, Peabody, Emmys, multiple IRE awards, contributed to a Pulitzer and part of a Pulitzer finalist team. Works extensively in data journalism, including for NICAR.

Andy Court is a veteran investigative producer for 60 Minutes. A graduate of Yale University, he worked as a reporter for The Concord Monitor and The Jerusalem Post, and as editorial director of The American Lawyer magazine before moving to television.  His stories on child labor, immigration, health care, and international corruption have won the Polk, duPont, Emmy, Peabody, Loeb, IRE, and Edward R. Murrow awards.

Andy Pierrotti is a national award-winning investigative reporter recognized with the duPont Columbia Award, George Foster Peabody, multiple Emmy and Edward R. Murrow awards. Andy’s investigations have changed state laws, put people in prison, freed a man from jail and led to historic state fines.

Angelica Mayor (she/her) works as a Program Manager at the IWMF, where she manages safety initiatives, reporting grants and emergency assistance.

Before the IWMF, Angelica worked as a crisis responder for the National Human Trafficking Hotline and a case manager for survivors of human trafficking, sexual assault, domestic violence and other violent crimes. These experiences led to her interest in joining the IWMF to support and empower women and gender-diverse journalists against gender-based violence. Additionally, Angelica sees the importance of representation in media to highlight important stories worldwide. Angelica speaks English, Tagalog and Spanish.
speakers.tagLine: Program Manager, International Women's Media Foundation

speakers.bio: Anna Massoglia is an investigative journalist and the editor of MuckRock. She leads the MuckRock News team and oversees its Sunlight Research Desk. Anna publishes Influence Brief and previously led OpenSecrets' media team, directing editorial processes and investigations. As a subject matter expert, she has worked with a wide range of newsrooms, academic institutions and nonprofits.
Annabelle Hanflig is an associate producer for 60 Minutes on CBS News.
speakers.tagLine: Associate Producer for 60 Minutes on CBS News

speakers.bio:
Annys Shin is ICIJ's deputy managing editor, based in Washington, D.C.

Previously, Annys worked for The Washington Post as a business reporter, an editor for the Washington Post Magazine, and most recently as social issues editor for the newspaper.
Pulitzer Prize-winning investigative reporter for Bloomberg News. Proud alumnus of BuzzFeed News and the Tampa Bay Times.

April Alonso is a visual journalist from Cicero, IL, and is the Digital Edidtor & Co-founder of Cicero Independiente.
At Cicero Independiente, April leads digital publishing and visual strategy, working with editors, reporters, and community members to shape stories grounded in local experiences. She collaborates with freelancers, oversees multimedia, and develops distribution strategies to extend reach online and offline. She also leads initiatives centered on care and safety.

April Quevedo is a reporter for the Milwaukee Journal Sentinel covering the Metcalfe Park neighborhood on the city’s north side. 
Previously, she spent nearly a decade working in logistics and supply chain management.
Originally from Chicago, she holds a master’s degree from the University of Maryland’s Philip Merrill College of Journalism.
speakers.tagLine: Reporter, Neighborhood Dispatch at Milwaukee Journal Sentinel

speakers.bio: Ariel Kaminer is an editor on the New York Times opinion desk, where she works with a wide range of contributors on topics from national security to monetary policy to celebrity culture and beyond. Prior to that, she was the investigations editor at BuzzFeed News, where she led a team of 14 reporters and editors with the mandate to take the biggest swings possible. She's also been the editor of the Times's Arts & Leisure section, as well as a magazine editor and a higher education reporter.
speakers.tagLine: New York Times Opinion deputy editor for Ideas and Investigations

speakers.bio: Arlene Martínez joined Good Jobs First, a nonprofit corporate and government watchdog, in 2020. Before joining GJF, (which maintains the Subsidy, Violation, and Tax Break Tracker databases), she was a reporter with the USA TODAY Network/Ventura County Star, The Morning Call, the LA Times and Hispanic Link News Service, where her work focused on the choices people in power make when it comes to who and what gets prioritized.

Ashley Cleek is a producer for Reveal. She helped develop and launch VICE News’ flagship podcast, VICE News Reports. As a reporter, she's worked with This American Life, VICE, NPR and Latino USA. Her work has won a national Edward R. Murrow Award, a Gracie Award, an International Documentary Association Award and a Third Coast award, and she was a 2020 Livingston Award finalist. She has reported stories across the American South, Turkey, Russia and India. Cleek is based in New York.

Ashley Graham is an award-winning investigative reporter and anchors KSLA News 12 at 6. 

Her passion for in-depth storytelling and getting results led her to start her career in mid-Michigan in 2018. Through her work, she has helped wrongfully convicted inmates gain freedom and helped storm survivors find safe housing. She investigated crime in Southwest Florida, was a lead COVID-19 reporter at the height of the pandemic and covered the aftermath of one of the nation's most devastating hurricanes, Ian.

Ashley is a graduate of Emory University and Northwestern University, where she received her master of science in journalism (MSJ).

Audrey Nielsen is a reporter, researcher and data journalist with a focus on public accountability, historical records and public data. Audrey develops reporting tools and resources for collaborative journalism. She is an investigative and research journalist at Muckrock on the Sunlight Research Desk.

Austin Fast is an investigative data reporter for USA TODAY who uses programming languages to scrape, clean, and analyze large data sets to report on government accountability, criminal justice and public health. His reporting has received national recognition, including a National Headliner Award for the true crime podcast “Untested,” and from the Society of Professional Journalists’ New York City chapter for uncovering fraud in the Paycheck Protection Program.

Before joining USA TODAY, Fast reported for NPR’s investigations team and worked in public radio in Ohio, Arizona, and Alaska. He has also covered breaking news at a Cincinnati television station and served as a Peace Corps Volunteer overseas. He holds a master’s degree in investigative journalism from Arizona State University and bachelor’s degrees in journalism and international studies from Miami University.
speakers.tagLine: Investigative data reporter for USA Today

speakers.bio: A junior at Roosevelt University, Avery Causley-Ingram is the Editor in Chief for The Torch, the student newspaper at Roosevelt University in Chicago, Illinois. Her passions include covering health journalism but specifically around Native healthcare, reproductive rights and bodily autonomy.
speakers.tagLine: Editor in Chief, The Torch (Roosevelt University)

speakers.bio: Gray is a research methods professor and oversees Newmark J-School’s Research Center. She is the former director of news research at The New York Times and is writing a book about research.
speakers.tagLine: Newmark J-School, Investigative Research Instructor and Associate Professor

speakers.bio: Ben Felder is an Oklahoma City-based editor for Investigate Midwest, a non-profit news organization that covers corporate agriculture and other powerful players in the U.S. food system.

Benét J. Wilson is a training director for Investigative Reporters and Editors. She was previously the lead credit cards writer for Bankrate and director of the Poynter-Koch Media and Journalism Fellowship for early-career journalists. Before that, she was a senior editor and writer for The Points Guy. She serves on the boards of Mercer University’s Center for Collaborative Journalism and the Institute for Independent Journalism. She has served on the boards of the Online News Association and the National Association of Black Journalists. She graduated from American University in Washington, D.C., with a Bachelor of Arts in broadcast journalism.

Benét J. Wilson is a training director for Investigative Reporters and Editors. She was previously the lead credit cards writer for Bankrate and director of the Poynter-Koch Media and Journalism Fellowship for early-career journalists. Before that, she was a senior editor and writer for The Points Guy. She serves on the boards of Mercer University’s Center for Collaborative Journalism and the Institute for Independent Journalism. She has served on the boards of the Online News Association and the National Association of Black Journalists. She graduated from American University in Washington, D.C., with a Bachelor of Arts in broadcast journalism.

Beth Hawkins covers education for a national nonprofit outlet, The 74 Million. She writes about inequities and innovations in school systems, with a special focus on children with disabilities and LGBTQ students. She lives in Minneapolis.
Bethany Barnes is the deputy investigative editor at the Tampa Bay Times and one of four journalists who curate the Local Matters newsletter.

Bigad Shaban is an investigative correspondent with over 20 years of experience in broadcast news.  His wide-ranging reporting has spurred government hearings, police investigations, and new laws.  From diving into the Pacific with marine biologists to flying into the eye of a hurricane with government researchers, Bigad has covered some of the nation's most consequential stories.  His reporting holds the powerful accountable and has earned him journalism's highest honors, including a Peabody Award, a duPont-Columbia Award, and 19 Emmy Awards.  

His series on the misuse of school resource officers prompted action by the Obama White House, while his investigation into wrongful evictions led to one of the largest housing reform efforts in San Francisco's history.

Bigad currently serves as Senior Investigative Reporter and fill-in anchor for NBC Bay Area.  His reporting has also been featured across NBC News platforms, including NBC Nightly News.
speakers.tagLine: Senior Investigative Reporter, NBC Bay Area

speakers.bio: Brendan Keefe is Chief Investigator for Atlanta News First Investigates and one of the nation’s most-decorated investigative reporters. His work has earned two National Emmys, the Peabody Award, the DuPont-Columbia Silver Baton, nine National Edward R. Murrow Awards, and more than 150 regional Emmys. Motivated by exposing systemic failures to make communities safer, his reporting has changed laws, led to multiple patents, helped fix the nation’s 911 system, and stopped DEA agents from searching innocent air travelers nationwide. Keefe has reported in Atlanta for over a decade and began his career 35 years ago as a news photographer, later reporting and anchoring in Houston and New York City. He was embedded with the U.S. Army during the Iraq war, taught at the Poynter Institute, and graduated from Kenyon College (English Literature). Today he works as a one-man band—shooting, producing, and editing complex investigations for Atlanta News First and Gray Media’s InvestigateTV+.

Bret Schafer is the Senior Director of Policy & Research, Information Operations, at ISD-US. Prior to joining ISD, Bret was a Senior Fellow at the German Marshall Fund’s Alliance for Securing Democracy (ASD), where he led ASD’s information manipulation team. As an expert in computational propaganda, state-backed information operations, and tech policy, Bret has spoken at conferences around the globe. His research has appeared in the New York Times, USA Today, the Wall Street Journal and the Washington Post, and he has been interviewed on NPR, NBC, CNN, Al Jazeera and the BBC. He previously spent more than 10 years in the entertainment industry, including stints at Cartoon Network and as a freelance writer for Warner Brothers.

Brian Howey is the co-founder of The Bell Project, a nonprofit newsroom that investigates torture in the American criminal justice system. His reporting has inspired new police oversight laws in two states and prompted a Justice Department investigation into a Mississippi sheriff’s department. A Pulitzer Prize finalist, he has also won a George Polk Award and a duPont-Columbia Award, and he is a two-time Goldsmith Prize finalist. He earned a master’s degree from the UC Berkeley Graduate School of Journalism.
Brian M. Rosenthal is a Pulitzer Prize-winning investigative reporter at The New York Times. He is the chair of the IRE Board of Directors.

Brianna Sacks explores how climate change is transforming the United States through violent storms, intense heat, widespread wildfires, and other forms of extreme weather. She deploys to disaster zones and does investigative and enterprise reporting on the preparations for, responses to and aftermaths of catastrophic events.

Her investigations into Florida's insurance system after Hurricane Ian influenced the passage of a new state law and earned her a finalist for the Livingston Award. Sacks also got second place for SEJ's Outstanding Beat Reporting for her investigations into the failures of utilities, insurers, and government agencies after Hurricane Ian and the Maui wildfires. 

In 2025, Sacks and her team were finalists for the Pulitzer Prize for their coverage of the aftermath of Hurricane Ian.

Bryan Metzger was a senior politics reporter at Business Insider, where he covered Congress, campaigns, and money in politics. He previously covered money in politics for New Mexico in Depth, a nonprofit newsroom in his home state.
speakers.tagLine: Former Senior Politics Reporter at Business Insider

speakers.bio: Caitlin McGlade is an investigative data reporter at the Charlotte Observer. She has about 15 years of experience holding the powerful accountable, and her journalism has made impact in North Carolina, Arizona, Florida, Kentucky and Ohio.

Dr. Carl Gershenson is Managing Director at Eviction Lab. He has published on the causes and consequences of housing instability, with a special focus on how eviction leads to further economic and residential insecurity.

Carla Minet is the executive director and editor in chief at the Centro de Periodismo Investigativo in Puerto Rico. She’s done investigative work mostly on political campaigns, corruption, environmental issues and government affairs. For the past 20 years, she worked as a reporter, editor and producer for radio, tv, print and online media in Puerto Rico. She has been a contributor to The New York Times, The Washington Post, NPR and BBC, among other international media outlets. From CPI, she has led teams that have won the IRE Philip Meyer Award for Precision Journalism, the Louis M. Lyons Award for Conscience and Integrity in Journalism at Harvard, the Aronson Award at CUNY, and the Izzy Award for Conscience Journalism at Ithaca College, among others. She is part of the Membership Committee of the International Consortium of Investigative Journalists (ICIJ) and board member at the Institute for Nonprofit News (INN).

Carolina Cruz es la reportera investigativa encargada de Telemundo Responde en la zona de Washington DC, Maryland y Virginia. Es graduada de la universidad Columbia College Chicago con un Bachillerato en Periodismo Televisivo y un Grado Asociado en Comunicaciones de Prairie State College ubicado en Illinois. Entre amigos habla con orgullo de sus raíces puertorriqueñas y mexicanas. Ha dedicado gran parte de su vida en ayudar a otros a encontrar un lugar donde puedan ejercer el periodismo.
speakers.tagLine: Investigative Reporter for TELEMUNDO RESPONDE in DC

speakers.bio: Cat is a master’s student at the University of Maryland’s Philip Merrill College of Journalism and a fellow at UMD’s Howard Center for Investigative Journalism.
speakers.tagLine: Data Reporter, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism @ UMD

speakers.bio: Catherine Sweeney has been reporting professionally since 2012. She started out in local government reporting for newspapers, but transitioned into covering health full time in 2020. She now covers health for Nashville Public Radio. Common topics include rural health access, abortion rights, lethal injection drugs and protocols, social services and nutrition.

Charles Minshew is the senior editor of data journalism for The Atlanta Journal-Constitution, where he leads a team of reporters using data, open records and code to tell stories about Atlanta, Georgia and the South. He also serves on the board of the Georgia First Amendment Foundation.
speakers.tagLine: Senior Editor of Data Journalism at The Atlanta Journal-Constitution

speakers.bio: Computational journalist Cheryl Phillips leads Big Local News, which collects, processes and shares governmental data that are hard to obtain and difficult to analyze; partners with local and national newsrooms on investigative projects across a range of topics; and makes it easy to teach best practices for finding stories within the data. Among other projects, the team of Stanford faculty, data journalists, students and researchers is doing work to inform the public about disparities in criminal justice, environmental and economic impacts of forest fires, local government accountability through audits, the integrity of elections at the state level, and root causes of out-of-reach home prices in local markets.
speakers.tagLine: Big Local News Founder/Lecturer, Stanford University

speakers.bio:
Cheryl Thompson-Morton is the Head of Advisory Programs at The Lenfest Institute for Journalism, where she leads efforts to support news organizations in developing strategies for financial sustainability and audience growth.

She is also co-led the Transforming Crime Coverage to Public Safety Journalism program. This change management course helps newsrooms rethink their approach to crime reporting and develop new policies to cover public safety in more effective and responsible ways.

Prior to her current role, Cheryl led the Black Media Initiative at the Center for Community Media at the Craig Newmark Graduate School of Journalism at CUNY, designing programs to support Black-owned newsrooms through research, training, and networking opportunities.

Thompson is an award-winning investigative correspondent for NPR. Before joining NPR in 2019, she spent 22 years covering law enforcement, political corruption and the White House for The Washington Post, and was part of teams that won the Pulitzer Prize for National Reporting in 2002 and 2016. She was the reporting coach on the NPR podcast "No Compromise," which won the Pulitzer Prize for Audio Reporting in 2021. She served as a Pulitzer Prize juror for investigative reporting in 2022 and chaired the jury in 2023. She is a 2026 SPJ-DC Hall of Fame inductee and is the recipient of more than 40 journalism awards. Her first book, Forgotten Souls: The Search for the Lost Tuskegee Airmen, was released earlier this year.

Chiara Eisner is a correspondent on NPR's investigations unit. Her reporting for NPR has led to congressional action and regulatory changes, and has been honored with multiple awards.

Eisner's exclusive reporting on the sensitive government documents found on a public hotel printer in advance of the 2025 Putin-Trump summit in Alaska was NPR's most read article in 2025.

Her coverage of deadly wildfires in Los Angeles used thousands of hours of firefighter communications to reveal how problems on power lines contributed to new fires and was awarded the 2026 Investigative Reporters and Editors (IRE) award in audio. Eisner's reporting on how cuts to the CDC's work force put public health at risk helped restore the jobs of 27 scientists and was a finalist for a 2026 NIHCM Award. 

Eisner came to NPR from The State in South Carolina, where her reporting revealed the hidden toll of execution work and exclusive details about the state's plans to put prisoners to death by firing squad.

Since 2016, Chris has led a team that has changed state and local laws, reformed corporate policies, and returned more than $8M to San Francisco Bay Area residents. Prior to joining KNTV, Chris worked for WTVT-TV (Tampa, FL), WFLA-TV (Tampa, FL), and The Associated Press in Washington, DC. He started his career at WCJB-TV (Gainesville, FL), after graduating from The University of Florida's College of Journalism and Communication.

Chris Cioffi is a senior congressional reporter at Bloomberg Industry Group covering tax and tax policy. He previously covered Congress for CQ Roll Call in Washington. Before that, Chris spent time at newspapers in Billings, Mont., Lynchburg, Va., and in the newsroom of his hometown paper—Raleigh's News & Observer. He has master's degrees in data journalism from the University of Maryland in College Park and data science from American University.

Chris Davis is a deputy investigations editor for the New York Times, where he helps lead the Local Investigation Reporting Fellowship program to support stories and journalists in local communities. Davis previously oversaw investigative work at USA TODAY and across Gannett’s local newsrooms. Davis spent 15 years as an investigative reporter and editor in Florida, including at the Tampa Bay Times. Davis is a graduate of the University of South Carolina.
speakers.tagLine: Deputy Editor, New York Times Local Investigative Reporting Fellowship

speakers.bio: Chris is the Media Partnerships Lead for Everlaw, an eDiscovery platform. Chris works at the intersection of technology and journalism. He is an expert at helping bridge the gap between cutting-edge tech and AI, and the reporters who rely on it. From Meta to CrowdTangle to Everlaw, he has built and scaled products used by newsrooms worldwide, led complex partnerships and advised journalists and researchers navigating the rapidly evolving information space.
speakers.tagLine: Media Partnerships Lead, Everlaw for Good

speakers.bio:
Chris Nakamoto’s reporting over the past two decades has changed laws and lives across southeast Louisiana.
Chris co-anchors WAFB 9NEWS at 5pm and serves as the Chief Investigative Reporter. He also works as a national investigative reporter for InvestigateTV, the investigative arm of Gray Media which owns WAFB. 
In 2023, Nakamoto received the prestigious Alfred I. dupont-Columbia silver baton for his numerous reports on the handling of the Ronald Greene case by Louisiana State Police. Nakamoto's coverage led to a state and federal investigation into Louisiana State Police and resulted in the dismantling of leadership at Louisiana's premier law enforcement agency.
His work has also been recognized with numerous Emmys, regional Edward R. Murrows along with Associated Press and Louisiana Association of Broadcasters awards.
Chris graduated from Loyola University with honors.

Chris heads up Denver's nationally recognized investigative team at KUSA-TV. He is the recipient of a Peabody, du-Pont, IRE Award, and six national Murrow Awards. His work on complex subjects has helped change federal and state laws. He currently also assists TEGNA stations develop investigations with national reach. He likes pugs and people who agree to on-camera interviews.

Chris Seymour served as a Special Agent with the Internal Revenue Service Criminal Investigation for the Los Angeles Field Office. In addition to casework, he served as an undercover shopper, and lead tech agent and radio coordinator. During his tenure at the IRS, he worked cases including MGM, The Cosmopolitan, the Wayne Nix Gambling Enterprise, Ippei Mizuhara, Scott Sibella, Mathew Bowyer, and most recently Yasiel Puig.

Cierra Putman is a nationally recognized, award-winning journalist with the 13 Investigates team at WTHR in Indianapolis. Her reporting has changed laws, uncovered injustices, and held leaders accountable, making a real difference in people's lives. Her work recently helped a toddler obtain a passport, reuniting him with his parents after their sudden deportation from the U.S. Before joining WTHR, she worked as a general assignment reporter across multiple states, delivering exclusive interviews and enterprise reports. She also serves as Vice Chair of the NABJ Investigative Task Force.

Cindy Galli is Director of Justice and Legal Reporting at MS NOW, where she oversees a team of award-winning reporters in Washington and New York who enterprise investigations with government accountability at their core.  She also serves on the network’s partnership team, growing reporting collaborations with other news organizations.  

Cindy is currently serving her fourth term on IRE’s board of directors.  

Prior to MS NOW, she was executive producer of ABC News’ investigative unit, where her team was honored with a duPont-Columbia Silver Baton, national Emmy, Murrow, Headliner and Overseas Press Club awards, as well as a Peabody nomination.  Cindy built a collaborative team comprised of local reporters and conceived and oversaw the network’s groundbreaking commitment to stay in and report on Uvalde, Texas after the 2022 school shooting.  

A longtime consumer investigative producer, Cindy started at KGO-TV in San Francisco and graduated from University of California at Berkeley.
speakers.tagLine: Director, Justice and Legal Reporting, MS NOW

speakers.bio: Clara Bates is a state government reporter at the Santa Fe New Mexican. She previously reported on poverty and social services for the Missouri Independent. There, with support from the USC Center for Health Journalism, she investigated Missouri's patchwork system of legal representation for parents whose children are taken into foster care. Her story was included in the Center for Just Journalism's guide to child welfare reporting. Another of her stories, an investigation into a potential wrongful conviction in St. Louis, formed the basis for an ongoing habeas petition. Clara grew up in Las Vegas and graduated from Harvard.

Clare Amari is an investigative reporter based in Houston, Texas. Most recently, she investigated school policing in Texas through The New York Times’ Local Investigations Fellowship. Before joining The Times, Clare covered local prosecutors and the criminal courts in Greater Houston for the nonprofit news organization Houston Landing. There, she wrote on issues ranging from the region’s overburdened justice system to deadly police pursuits to an inmate who spent decades on death row before his sentence was overturned as unconstitutional.
speakers.tagLine: Associate Reporter, The New York Times

speakers.bio: Clavel Rangel Jiménez is a journalist with more than 15 years of experience covering labor unions, migration, human rights, climate, corruption and extractive industries in Venezuela and across the Americas. She co-founded the Venezuelan Amazon Journalists Network, an initiative that promotes investigative journalism, knowledge exchange and collaboration among reporters. Rangel began her career as a reporter and editor at Correo del Caroní, an award-winning regional newspaper in southern Venezuela, where she extensively covered the region. She later served as a correspondent at El Nacional and El Pitazo; as Editor-in-Chief of the Spanish-language newsletter Soy Arepita; and as a Senior Editor at El Tiempo Latino in Washington, D.C. Her work has been featured in The Guardian, Chicago Health Magazine, and Armando.info. Originally from Venezuela, she now resides in the United States.
Cody was a newspaper reporter, data specialist and web developer before joining IRE as a training director in 2017. He became tech lead in 2022.
speakers.tagLine: Director of technology, Investigative Reporters & Editors

speakers.bio: Courtney Kan is an award-winning editor with 15+ years of experience producing ambitious investigations and immersive visual-first storytelling. Most recently, she was the Senior Editor for Visuals on The Washington Post's national desk, overseeing visually-driven breaking news stories and enterprise projects. She has experience leading multidisciplinary teams for major news events, investigative projects and breaking news and editing across a range of formats including podcasts, immersive visual projects, data-driven reporting, documentaries, and visual investigations.
speakers.tagLine: Former Senior Editor for Visuals, The Washington Post

speakers.bio: Craig Lyons joined the Arnolt Center for Investigative Journalism in November 2021 as its first managing editor. Lyons has spent nearly 20 years covering government agencies at the local, state and federal level in Michigan, Indiana, New Hampshire, Vermont and Maine.
speakers.tagLine: Indiana University/Arnolt Center for Investigative Journalism

speakers.bio:
Craig Silverman is an award-winning journalist and author and a leading expert on online disinformation and digital investigations. He is the cofounder of Indicator (https://indicator.media/), an independent publication dedicated to exposing digital deception and to equipping professionals with knowledge and skills to help them investigate it.

Prior to Indicator, he was a national reporter for ProPublica covering voting, platforms, disinformation, and online manipulation. Craig previously served as the founding editor of BuzzFeed Canada and the media editor of BuzzFeed News, where he pioneered coverage of digital disinformation and media manipulation.

Craig is also the editor of the European Journalism Centre’s Verification Handbook series. These free online books offer world-class guidance on how to verify online content and investigate disinformation and media manipulation.

WRAL investigative reporter and documentary producer Cristin Severance leads one of the only full time documentary units in local television news. She produces long form investigative documentaries focused on some of North Carolina’s most important issues, including foster care failures, sports betting, immigration, Hurricane Helene recovery, and the rise of cancer in young adults. Her reporting has prompted change at the state level, including foster care reform legislation in North Carolina. Her work has earned national honors including a Gracie Award, a Service to America Award, and the AACR June L. Biedler Prize for Cancer Journalism, along with dozens of regional Emmy and Murrow Awards. Severance frequently speaks about investigative storytelling, documentary production, and creating journalism that drives impact across television, streaming, and digital platforms.
speakers.tagLine: WRAL Investigative Reporter and Documentary Producer

speakers.bio: Curtis Gilbert is the deputy managing editor of investigations at APM Reports and Minnesota Public Radio. He leads the team that makes the podcast Sold a Story, which has set off an avalanche of legislation on how schools teach reading. Along with journalists from The Salt Lake Tribune and KUER, he won the top national prize in investigative business journalism, the Barlett & Steele Award, for the podcast Sent Away. His work has changed laws, sparked lawsuits, been cited in a vice presidential debate and forced government officials to resign. He also contributed to the Peabody Award-winning podcast In the Dark. In addition to his work in audio journalism, Curtis has also won three regional Emmy Awards for a series of viral web videos designed to demystify complex issues in the news. Curtis is a graduate of Macalester College, where he occasionally teaches a class in narrative journalism.

Cynthia Barnett is the director of Climate and Environment Reporting Initiatives at the University of Florida's College of Journalism and Communications. She leads student investigations on water, chemicals, climate change and other environmental concerns. She is an award-winning author and journalist who reports on water and climate change around the world. Her books include Rain: A Natural and Cultural History and The Sound of the Sea: Seashells and the Fate of the Oceans, both named among the best science books of the year by NPR's Science Friday. Her new book underway is The Sea Beneath Us: The Secret History and Coming Crisis of Groundwater.
speakers.tagLine: Director of Climate and Environment Reporting Initiatives, University of Florida

speakers.bio: Cynthia Dizikes is an investigative reporter at the San Francisco Chronicle. Her reporting has focused on wrongdoing, negligence and fraud in health care organizations, public agencies and the cleanup of contaminated land. Her work has led to new laws and regulations, leadership overhauls, improved practices, and millions of dollars in funding for underserved communities.

Before joining The Chronicle in 2022, Daarel Burnette II served as an assistant managing editor and reporter at Education Week and bureau chief for Chalkbeat Tennessee, a start-up news organization based in Memphis. He has worked as an education reporter at The Atlanta Journal-Constitution, The Minneapolis Star Tribune, and The Louisville Courier Journal. He also worked as a general-assignment reporter at the Chicago Tribune. He received his undergraduate degree in print journalism from Hampton University and a master’s degree in politics and journalism from Columbia University.
speakers.tagLine: Senior Editor, Chronicle of Higher Education

speakers.bio: Dan Lamothe joined The Washington Post in 2014 to cover the U.S. military. He has written about the Armed Forces since 2008, traveling extensively, embedding with five branches of service and covering combat in Afghanistan. A native of Chicopee, Mass., Lamothe began writing for professional newspapers in high school, and his enthusiasm for it grew while attending the University of Massachusetts-Amherst. As an undergraduate, he was deeply involved at The Massachusetts Daily Collegian, the student newspaper on campus, and led its newsroom as managing editor as a senior.

Dan Krauth is an award-winning investigative journalist who joined the Eyewitness News team in October 2019.  
 
His most recent reporting on “squatting” exposed a legal loophole which directly led to legislators changing New York State law and to the squatter featured in his investigations being sent to prison.  
 
He has investigated a variety of issues over the past two decades, including negative plastic surgery practices in Florida which prompted lawmakers to overhaul safety regulations in 2019.  His reporting also led to recommended changes in how a popular plastic surgery is performed worldwide. 
 
Dan received a national Edward R. Murrow Award for Investigative Reporting in 2025 along with more than a dozen Emmy Awards and other regional honors.

Dana Coester is a professor and director for West Virginia University's Media Innovation Center as well as founder of the national Murrow award-winning digital outlet 100 Days in Appalachia. Coester’s work has long focused on the intersection of technology, media and communities, with a focus on critical infrastructure for sustainable local journalism. She is passionate about the preservation of local press freedoms, and she launched the Rural Digital Resilience Project to address the unique challenges that America’s rural communities face on and offline. Coester received a JustFilms award for her film Raised by Wolves, a feature length documentary on the risks to young people online exposed to nihilistic violent extremism. Coester earned her master’s degree in journalism from the University of Missouri-Columbia.
speakers.tagLine: WVU Media Innovation Center, Rural Digital Resilience Project, 100 Days in Appalachia,

speakers.bio: Daniel Chang is a correspondent for KFF Health News, covering Florida and the South. His reporting focuses on health policy, access to care, hospitals and health insurance.

Daniel Connolly is a senior reporter for legal news service Law360 and a contributor to WKNO radio and the Institute for Public Service Reporting at the University of Memphis. He's the author of "The Book of Isaias," a nonfiction work about children of immigrants coming of age in Memphis. He was a finalist for a Gerald Loeb Award in 2024 for his coverage of a troubled debt relief company and served as an elected member of IRE's contest committee in 2025.
speakers.tagLine: Senior Reporter, Law360 and Contributor to Institute for Public Service Reporting / WKNO Radio

speakers.bio: Daniel Girma has been a producer for NPR's Embedded Podcast for five years.

Daniela Ibarra joined KSAT 12 in 2023. She returned to her hometown of San Antonio after reporting in Oklahoma and West Texas, where her award-winning accountability reporting helped drive changes in state law and local public safety improvements. A proud graduate of University of North Texas, Daniela is a 2025 Investigative Reporters & Editors Chauncey Bailey Journalist of Color Fellow.

Daniela Molina is a national bilingual investigative producer & reporter with a strong interest in trafficking and health care stories. During her time at InvestigateTV, she's uncovered nursing home abuse, the desecration of Black cemeteries, data collection and privacy concerns, and exposed secrecy in military medical malpractice. She also produces a Spanish-language financial consumer segment, “Cuidando Su Billetera,” which airs on Gray Media Group’s Telemundo stations.

Darya helps publications, broadcast programs, and authors ensure their stories are airtight. With original research and public records, she has advanced investigations into the social impacts of technology, criminal legal systems, and a variety of human rights issues. She was previously the Research Editor at Rest of World and an Associate Producer at Fault Lines on Al Jazeera English.

Dave Levinthal is one of the nation's leading journalists investigating the nexus of politics, policy and power. He's the former editor-in-chief of Raw Story and deputy editor of Business Insider and as an editor or reporter at the Center for Public Integrity, Politico, OpenSecrets and Dallas Morning News. His writing has appeared in The Atlantic, Rolling Stone, TIME, Columbia Journalism Review, Washingtonian and the Washington Post, and he's a regular guest on national television and radio news programs.
speakers.tagLine: Senior editor at NOTUS overseeing money and politics, investigations and special projects.

speakers.bio: David Armiak is research director and an investigative journalist with the Center for Media and Democracy. David joined CMD in 2015, has conducted extensive investigations on dark money, corporate corruption, and right-wing networks, and is responsible for filing and analyzing hundreds of public records requests every year. David has a strong research interest in social movements and political power, and has delivered many talks on the subject.
speakers.tagLine: Research Director and Investigative Journalist, Center for Media and Democracy

speakers.bio: David Cuillier is director of the Brechner Freedom of Information Project at the University of Florida. A former journalist, he has taught data journalism and access to public records for 25 years, serves on the federal FOIA Advisory Committee, coordinates Sunshine Week, and co-authored “The Art of Access: Strategies for Acquiring Public Records.” He has testified three times before Congress regarding FOIA and served as president of the Society of Professional Journalists and National Freedom of Information Coalition.
speakers.tagLine: Brechner FOI Project, University of Florida

speakers.bio: David Hammer leads the investigative team at WWL Louisiana, the CBS affiliate in New Orleans. He's won seven regional Emmy Awards and was a finalist for a National Investigative Emmy for exposing and chasing down an official who was elected, without opposition, to oversee sexual assault investigations after being accused of child sexual assault himself. Hammer also spent 15 years as a print reporter, winning the Scripps Howard and Society of Environmental Journalists' national awards for his investigation of what caused the 2010 BP Gulf oil disaster.

David Hodges is an award-winning investigative reporter at WBTV. His work has exposed government failures, uncovered corruption, and forced accountability from powerful institutions. He tries to accomplish two things with every story he tells: shed light on hidden issues and help people in need.

His investigation into a fatal construction fire in Charlotte exposed numerous failures from contractors and fire department leaders that led to substantive changes at the local and state levels. His special report, Fire and Failure, was awarded a regional Emmy.

His reporting on everything from HOAs to towing has sparked criminal investigations, led to arrests and indictments, and prompted reforms at the local and state levels. Hodges’ investigations have uncovered government misconduct, exposed systemic failures in the criminal justice system, and revealed how public officials have misused taxpayer money.
Deborah Nelson is a Pulitzer Prize-winning journalist, past IRE president and professor at University of Maryland, home of the Howard Center for Investigative Journalism.

Debra Kamin is an investigative reporter for The New York Times who covers wealth and power in New York City, focusing on exposing trauma and abuse inflicted by powerful individuals acting with perceived impunity. 

Kamin’s work consistently drives institutional accountability. She uncovered multiple allegations of abuse surrounding the luxury real estate brokers known as the Alexander brothers, and her reporting  helped eventually lead to their federal sex trafficking convictions. Her reporting has also included a major exposé on pervasive sexual misconduct within the National Association of Realtors (NAR), prompting the immediate resignation of its president, as well as extensive coverage of the Epstein files and interviews with his victims.
speakers.tagLine: Investigative reporter, The New York Times

speakers.bio: Derek Willis teaches and does journalism, especially data journalism, at the University of Maryland. He also loves cricket.

Desiree Hanford is a clinical professor at Northwestern University's Medill School of Journalism where she teaches undergraduate and graduate reporting courses and seminars including introductory reporting, business reporting and investigative reporting. She is a past president of SABEW.

Diara J. Townes is an award-winning environmental engagement journalist from New York. She's covered stories shaped by state and national trends through nonprofit research, freelance reporting and her newsletter, The Curious Scout. She’s NC Local's program manager, supporting their expanding programmatic work for North Carolina journalists, and the audience and engagement manager with environmental justice publication, The Margin. Diara has worked for Aspen Digital and Sunlight Research Center and has served as an adjunct at the Newmark Graduate School of Journalism at CUNY.
speakers.tagLine: Engagement Journalist, NC Local, The Margin

speakers.bio: Doug MacMillan is a corporate accountability reporter at The Washington Post. His work has uncovered harmful practices by businesses across a wide range of industries, from senior housing to immigration detention, and exposed problems with emerging technologies, such as the police use of facial recognition software. He previously covered the rise of tech giants from from the San Francisco bureaus of The Wall Street Journal and Bloomberg.

For Reuters, I focus on securities regulation, consumer finance and prudential oversight. I was previously a Congo-focused anticorruption investigator for a transparency organization and have covered war crimes trials, resource corruption and human rights. My reporting sparked a foreign bribery probe of a publicly traded mining company.

Drew Sullivan is a co-founder and the publisher of OCCRP. He co-founded the organization in 2007 with Paul Radu and served as its editor in chief until 2023.

Under his leadership, OCCRP has become one of the world’s leading investigative journalism networks and has received numerous honors, including the Maria Ressa Prize for Courage in Investigative Journalism, the Missouri Honor Medal, the Global Shining Light Award, the Tom Renner Award for Crime Reporting, the European Press Prize, and the Skoll Award for Social Entrepreneurship. I 

Drew also helped to create Reporters Shield, a membership program that helps defend investigative reporting from SLAPPs and other forms of legal harassment. In 2022, he helped build Floodlight, which connects investigative reporting to the entertainment industry for adaptation into films and television series. In 2025, Drew helped launch Revenue Axis, a program that helps OCCRP media member centers strengthen their financial sustainability.

Dylan Freedman is the A.I. Projects Editor at The New York Times. He reports and builds investigative tools to help journalists make sense of a wide range of topics. Before joining The Times, Dylan worked on a machine-learning team at Google, served as the lead developer for the journalism nonprofit DocumentCloud and architected elections platforms as a principal software engineer for The Washington Post. He has taught data journalism courses at Temple University and University of Maryland, College Park. Dylan graduated from Harvard with a bachelor’s in computer science and music and from Stanford with a master’s in journalism. He lives in Washington, D.C.

Ed Mahon is an investigative reporter at Spotlight PA, an independent, nonpartisan, nonprofit newsroom dedicated to high-quality investigative and public-service journalism about the Pennsylvania state government and urgent statewide issues. 

His public health reporting has spurred policy changes, created new legal precedent in favor of transparency, prompted academic research in peer-reviewed journals, and earned statewide and national recognition from the Association of Health Care Journalists, Investigative Reporters and Editors, and other organizations. In 2025, Mahon received the Neil and Susan Sheehan Award for Investigative Journalism from the National Press Club Journalism Institute for his work covering the opioid epidemic, tracking opioid settlement funds, and elevating the voices of those most affected by the country’s addiction crisis.

Eli Cahan MD, MSc is an award-winning investigative journalist covering the intersection of child welfare and health policy. His written work has been featured in Rolling Stone, where he is a contributing writer, as well as ProPublica and The Washington Post, among other publications. His multimedia work has appeared on TV via ABC and radio via NPR. Eli’s reporting has won awards from the National Press Club, News Leaders Association, and elsewhere; he was shortlisted for the Livingston Award in 2025. Eli is also an intensive care doctor at Stanford University, having completed his pediatrics training at Harvard Medical School.
speakers.tagLine: Journalist / Physician, Rolling Stone / Stanford University

speakers.bio: Eli Hager is an investigative reporter who writes about poverty issues and social safety net programs for ProPublica. A Pulitzer Prize finalist for his reporting on the theft of Social Security benefits from foster children, he previously reported on juvenile justice and child welfare for The Marshall Project. Hager's work on these issues has appeared on This American Life and NBC Nightly News and in the New Yorker, the New York Times, the Atlantic and elsewhere.

Elizabeth Dunbar is executive editor of MinnPost, an independent nonprofit newsroom based in Minneapolis. Before joining MinnPost in 2022, she was a member of the newsroom leadership team at Minnesota Public Radio. In both newsrooms, she has responded to complex breaking news and safety considerations for journalists during an unprecedented time in Minnesota, beginning with the pandemic and the George Floyd uprising in 2020 and most recently with ICE enforcement this winter in the Twin Cities.

Elizabeth Koh is an investigative reporter on the Boston Globe’s Spotlight Team, covering health care, social services, and government accountability. She was previously a foreign correspondent for the Wall Street Journal in Seoul and a statehouse reporter at the Miami Herald, where her reporting helped change laws and trigger investigations into what was then the nation's largest domestic violence coalition. She is a two-time Pulitzer finalist: in 2021 with Journal colleagues for stories about nursing homes in the pandemic, and in 2025 as part of the Globe team that covered the collapse of a major hospital chain.

Emerald Morrow is an investigative reporter and fill-in anchor at 10 Tampa Bay News (WTSP-TV) where she has worked for eleven years. 
 
Her work has earned 11 regional Emmy awards, a regional Murrow Award and multiple honors from the Florida Association of Broadcast Journalists, Florida Society of Professional Journalists and the National Association of Black Journalists.
 
She attended Northwestern University’s Medill School of Journalism, earning bachelor’s and master’s degrees in magazine and broadcast journalism.
 
In the free time she’s able to find, she loves to read, sew, play tennis and explore the Gulf Coast beaches.

Emilia is the executive director of the Global Investigative Journalism Network (GIJN). She was formerly data and research editor at the International Consortium of Investigative Journalists (ICIJ), where she participated in more than 20 ICIJ award-winning investigative collaborations, including the Pulitzer-winning Panama Papers. She has been a professor at the Central University of Venezuela and a contributor for the Washington Post and Armando.info, which she co-founded. She was previously the investigative reporting coordinator at IPYS Venezuela.

Emilia Ruzicka is a freelance data journalist seeking their next full-time job. They work part-time managing data for the Trans News Initiative and conducting accessibility trainings for the Organization for Accessible Journalism. Emilia was formerly a Research Assistant investigating emerging technology use in journalism, a Project Mentor for Columbia's Lede Program, and a Data Reporter at Stacker. They have published with the Urban Institute, the Center for News, Technology, and Innovation, and other outlets. Emilia enjoys reporting on health, science, technology, queer issues, and public data. They have an MA from University of Virginia and a BS from Brown University. For more, visit emiliaruzicka.com.
Litigation Reporter, ALM Media & Vice President, Courthouse Reporters of New York
speakers.tagLine: ALM Media and the New York Law Journal

speakers.bio: Emily Schwing is an award-winning investigative journalist with two decades of experience. Her 2024 investigation into the fraudulent use of federal funds in the aftermath of typhoon Merbok resulted in federal policy changes at FEMA and a civil rights investigation at the US Department of Homeland Security. She was also part of the New York Times’ Covid Tracking Team, which won a Pulitzer Prize for Public Service in 2021. In 2019, Emily published a multi-year investigation into clergy sex abuse and its repercussions in Indigenous communities in Western Alaska and the Pacific Northwest.

Emma Carew Grovum is the founder of Kimbap Media, working at the intersection of journalism, technology, and audience. She holds a Cornell University certificate in Building a Diverse Workforce, completed leadership programs at Yale and Poynter, and participated in CUNY’s 2025 AI Lab for Leaders. Emma has previously worked at The Marshall Project, The Daily Beast, The New York Times, Foreign Policy, The Chronicle of Philanthropy, and The Star Tribune.

Eric Ferrero is the Executive Director of the Fund for Investigative Journalism, which provides grants and other support directly to journalists for specific investigative projects. Over the last 20 years, he has served in senior leadership roles at nonprofit and philanthropic organizations that support and partner closely with journalists to uncover groundbreaking stories, including at the Open Society Foundations, Innocence Project and Amnesty International.
speakers.tagLine: Executive Director, Fund for Investigative Journalism

speakers.bio:
Murrow, Emmy and AP Award winning Chief Investigative Reporter Eric Flack grew up in Washington, DC, and was drawn to reporting at an early age.
He worked on the school paper in high school and college, where Eric was a staff writer for the Daily Tar Heel at the University of North Carolina Chapel-Hill. Eric's defining moment came on 9/11/2001 when he found himself on vacation in New York City during the 9/11 attack on America. He raced to the World Trade Center arriving moments before the first tower fell. Eric teamed with a CBS Network news crew and filed reports from Ground Zero for the next 4 days. Eric has almost three decades of experience in local news. His reports have changed policy, exposed government waste and held the powerful accountable in government and law enforcement.

Eric Lipton is an investigative reporter in the Washington Bureau of The New York Times and a four-time winner of the Pulitzer Prize, most recently in 2026 for a series of stories he and other Times reporters wrote about actions taken by President Trump and his aides that financially benefitted their families, particularly as it relates to the crypto industry.  He also won a Pulitzer in 2015 for investigative journalism, based on a series of stories about the boom in lobbying of state attorneys general. At The Times, he writes about a broad range of issues, from the Trump family business ventures, to spending at the Pentagon on killer AI drones and even on the explosive growth of sports betting in the United States.

Erik Sandoval is the Director of Storytelling for Graham Media Group, overseeing editorial strategy and creative journalism across six television stations nationwide. An Emmy Award-winning journalist with two decades of experience, Erik is known for investigative reporting and solutions-focused storytelling that drives real community impact. His work has earned multiple Emmy Awards, Florida Association of Broadcast Journalism Awards, and an Edward R. Murrow Award. As a fellow with the Solutions Journalism Network and a force behind Graham Media Group's "Solutionaries" initiative, Erik champions reporting that goes beyond exposing problems — delivering stories that inform, inspire, and create change. A trusted coach and newsroom leader, Erik is passionate about developing journalists and pushing the boundaries of how local television tells stories that matter.
speakers.tagLine: Graham Media Group Director of Storytelling

speakers.bio: Evan Simon is an investigative journalist and documentary filmmaker based in New York. His groundbreaking investigations have spanned the globe and held powerful corporations to account using novel approaches to environmental journalism. Examples include using tracking devices to investigate plastic recycling claims, hunting down abandoned oil and gas wells with handheld gas detectors, and flying a thermal drone to catch an AI data center defying federal law. Simon is a two-time National Emmy Award winner, a National Edward R. Murrow Award winner, a duPont-Columbia Award winner, and a Peabody Award nominee.

My focus is on tracking dark money groups distorting public policy, elections, and our judiciary. My research has helped inform stories published in outlets like the Washington Post, New York Times, ProPublica, the Guardian, NBC, CNN, and more.
speakers.tagLine: Director of State Courts and Democracy, True North Research

speakers.bio: Data and Investigative Journalist, previously with the Colorado Springs Gazette/Denver Gazette, Realtor.com, Palm Spring Desert Sun, Arizona Center for Investigative Reporting, Arizona Capitol Times, with reporting for The New York Times, The Washington Post, ProPublica, The Guardian U.S., and the Center for Public Integrity.
speakers.tagLine: Data Editor, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism at Arizona State University

speakers.bio: Ewald Scharfenberg is founder of Armando.info, an award-winning Venezuelan investigative journalism outlet where he currently serves as co-editor. He has lived in exile since 2017. He was a correspondent for Spain's El País, a member of the Global Committee of the International Freedom of Expression Exchange (IFEX), and a member of the Latin American Advisory Council of the Press and Society Institute. He received the Special Award for Excellence from the Gabriel García Márquez Foundation in 2025.

Ezana Ćeman joined OCCRP in 2021 and is the product manager for data platforms. She is responsible for managing and enhancing platforms, including OCCRP Aleph Pro. Ezana works closely with teams across the organization to create user-centered experiences, drive product innovation, and ensure progress toward achieving OCCRP’s strategic objectives.

Ezana holds a bachelor’s degree in computer information systems from Pace University in New York City and a master’s degree in information technology from International Burch University in Sarajevo, Bosnia and Herzegovina. She is a Grace Hopper Scholar and a two-time Forbes 30 Under 30 Scholar. Ezana is the head of digital education, literacy, and advocacy at FemLab, an NGO in Sarajevo dedicated to advancing gender equality, democratic values, and women’s rights.
speakers.tagLine: Product Manager for Data Platforms, Organized Crime and Corruption Reporting Project

speakers.bio:
Fallon Gallagher is a Legal Affairs Reporter for MS NOW, where she covers the Supreme Court and the rest of the federal judiciary. She reports on major civil and criminal cases, including the DOJ's indictments of President Trump’s perceived enemies, litigation over ICE tactics and policies, and the  legal challenges to reproductive rights.  

Before joining MS NOW, she covered critical legal stories for NBC News, including President Trump’s criminal trial in Manhattan as well as the fallout from the Supreme Court’s Dobbs decision. She won an Edward R. Murrow award for her coverage of President Trump’s criminal case dismissal for NBC Nightly News with Lester Holt. 

Before joining the legal beat, Fallon was Lester Holt’s Anchor Producer. She traveled the world with Holt, covering everything from the horrific school shooting in Uvalde, Texas, to the funeral of Queen Elizabeth II. 

Fallon attended Northwestern University, where she majored in Journalism and political science.

Fatemeh Jamalpour is a feminist journalist who was banned from working in Iran by the Ministry of Intelligence. Over the past two decades, she has reported on Iran, covering grassroots activism, human rights and women’s rights for outlets including The New York Times, The Sunday Times, Time, The Times, the Los Angeles Times, BBC World News and Shargh newspaper in Tehran.

She is the co-author of For the Sun After Long Nights, a book about Iran’s women-led uprising that includes her firsthand reporting from the Woman, Life, Freedom movement. The book was shortlisted for the George Orwell Political Writing Prize and longlisted for the 2025 National Book Award.

Felicia Sonmez is a reporter covering growth and development for Blue Ridge Public Radio. She is part of the team that won a national Edward R. Murrow Award, several Society of Professional Journalists Green Eyeshade Awards and other recognition for coverage of the aftermath of Hurricane Helene, the worst natural disaster in North Carolina's history. Prior to joining BPR, Felicia worked for more than a dozen years as a print journalist, including as a national political reporter for The Washington Post, a China correspondent for Agence France-Presse and an editor for The Wall Street Journal in Beijing.
speakers.tagLine: Growth and Development Reporter, Blue Ridge Public Radio

speakers.bio:
rancisco Vara-Orta brought 17 years of newsroom experience to his role as IRE's first director of diversity and inclusion before being named interim deputy executive director in March 2026.

Vara-Orta joined the IRE staff in February 2019 as a training director. While working as a trainer, he has conducted sessions on managing data and investigative reporting for journalists across the United States and internationally.

He has worked for a variety of online and print publications, including Chalkbeat, Education Week, the San Antonio Express-News, Austin Business Journal, Los Angeles Business Journal and the Los Angeles Times. He earned a master’s degree in investigative/data journalism at the University of Missouri and a bachelor’s degree from St. Mary’s University in San Antonio.

Frank is a senior reporter for Law360, covering state and federal courts in New York City since 2019. Reporting from the courtroom for major criminal and civil trials, he has spoken up for the press corps, liaising with court officials and filing letters with the court to secure public access to proceedings and filings. Frank has written about Manhattan's spotty, secretive criminal court case files and created the only comprehensive digital docket for Donald Trump's criminal case. Beyond daily court coverage, Frank's investigative work often focuses on abuses of power in the judiciary.
speakers.tagLine: Senior Reporter, Law360 & President, Courthouse Reporters of New York

speakers.bio: Frederick “Fred” Wherry is the Vice Dean for Development and Inclusion in the Office of the Dean of Faculty and the Townsend Martin, Class of 1917 Professor of Sociology at Princeton University.
