He is currently writing a book What We Owe: How Debt Came for All of Us and How We Get Free (under contract at Crown Publishing) that examines the rise of mass debt, which now rivals mass incarceration in scale, and exposes the predatory tactics of debt collectors and the court system . He founded the Debt Collection Lab (debtcollectionlab.org) and the Dignity + Debt Network (dignityanddebt.org) where he works on issues of economic justice. He is the author, editor, or co-editor of 10 books, including Viral Debt (with Jodi Gardner & Mia Gray), Credit Where It’s Due: Rethinking Financial Citizenship (with Kristin Seefeldt and Anthony Alvarez), and Money Talks (with Nina Bandelj and Viviana Zelizer). He was president of the Social Science History Association and the Eastern Sociological Society.

Gabe Rottman is the Reporters Committee's vice president of policy, leading the organization's work to advance key policy priorities like the PRESS Act and the Department of Justice news media guidelines. He's a practicing attorney with a focus on novel issues at the intersection of press freedom, newsgathering, and technology.

Rottman comes to RCFP from PEN America, where he opened the organization’s Washington, D.C., office and served as its first Washington director. He also was the deputy director for the Center for Democracy and Technology’s Freedom, Security and Technology Project. At CDT, he led efforts on cybersecurity policy, and worked extensively on electronic surveillance and the rights of security researchers.

From 2012 to 2015, Rottman served as the lead federal legislative and regulatory counsel at the American Civil Liberties Union on open government, press freedom and the First Amendment. While at the ACLU, Rottman directed federal advocacy on the Espionage Act and its implications for journalist source protection.
speakers.tagLine: Attorney and Vice President of Policy, Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press

speakers.bio:
Gabriel Pietrorazio is a senior broadcast field correspondent – covering tribal natural resources and Indigenous affairs – for NPR member station KJZZ in Phoenix, Arizona.

Originally from Connecticut, Gabriel graduated in 2020 from Hobart College in western New York, earning bachelor’s degrees in Media and Society as well as Political Science and a minor in American Studies.

In 2021, Gabriel received his master’s degree from the Philip Merrill College of Journalism. Now, he’s pursuing a certificate in American Indian Studies from Scottsdale Community College.

From the Grand Canyon to the Southern border and everywhere in between, Gabriel regularly tracks D.C. decision-making across the Southwest. He’s won more than 50 awards, including an IRE Medal and National Headliner.

You can routinely hear his reports air through the Mountain West News Bureau, “The World,” “Here & Now,” NPR’s “Weekend Edition” and Native America Calling’s “National Native News.”

Gary Harki is the investigations editor for Bloomberg Industry Group. His 2020 investigation into the strip-searching of children by Virginia prisons led to the halt of the practice and numerous changes to state law. In 2019, he won the Al Nakkula Award, honoring outstanding police reporting for his series on jailing people with mental illness. Previous stops include Spotlight PA, The Virginian-Pilot, The O’Brien Fellowship at Marquette and The Charleston Gazette.
Gavin Off is a data journalism instructor at the University of Florida. Prior to that he worked as an investigative data reporter at the Charlotte Observer for 15 years.

George Chidi covers democracy in the American South for The Guardian. A former reporter for the Atlanta Journal-Constitution, his journalism career began in the U.S. Army and has ranged from covering the tech bubble in the 2000s to service as a small town city councilman in Pine Lake. He has also written for VICE, Rolling Stone, Slate and the Intercept, and contributes from time to time to local publications Decaturish and Atlanta Magazine.  His work during the pandemic examining the causes of Atlanta's increase in violent crime won a Guggenheim fellowship for crime reporting, the national award for public affairs writing from the Society for Professional Journalists and an Ambie nomination for the podcast "King Slime," covering the Young Thug trial and and an Emmy nomination for his quarterly public affairs series "The Next Atlanta" on Fox 5. George holds a bachelor's degree in journalism from UMass Amherst and an MBA from Georgia Tech.
speakers.tagLine: Southeast Reporter, Democracy and Politics, for the Guardian

speakers.bio: George Freeman is Executive Director of the Media Law Resource Center. He was most recently Of Counsel to the law firm of Jenner & Block. He is a former Assistant General Counsel of the New York Times Company, where he was at the forefront of numerous high-profile cases for the company and its affiliated businesses. George is a well-known speaker on media and First Amendment issues. He is the William J. Brennan Visiting Professor at the Columbia Journalism School and also teaches at New York University and CUNY’s Graduate School of Journalism. He has led or participated in many media groups and is the founder and Co-chair of the American Bar Association’s Forum on Communications Law annual conference. He is a graduate of Amherst and the Harvard Law School.
speakers.tagLine: Executive Director, Media Law Resource Center

speakers.bio: George is the editor of The Trace’s Gun Violence Data Hub, planning and managing the bits, bytes and resources that make it possible. George most recently worked at the Los Angeles Times where he helped lead a data and graphics team that anchored several awards, including a Pulitzer Finalist nod for its coverage of the 2023 Lunar New Year shooting in Monterey Park, California. He previously worked with several NPR member stations in California, winning a national Edward R. Murrow award for work holding the U.S. Forest Service accountable for its failures in tracking and executing wildfire prevention measures. George wore several hats over a five year run with the politics and policy wonks at CQ Roll Call in Washington, D.C. His first byline appeared on a photo in the Badger Herald, University of Wisconsin-Madison’s independent student newspaper. He is an avid motorcyclist, handyman, and ailing Boston Red Sox fanatic.
speakers.tagLine: Editor, Gun Violence Data Hub - The Trace

speakers.bio:
Gilbert Corsey is like a newsroom Swiss Army knife, equally skilled in investigative reporting, feature storytelling, and breaking news, whether he’s in the field or behind the anchor desk. 

He currently serves as an evening anchor and special assignment reporter at WDRB-TV in Louisville, Kentucky.

Corsey has spent more than 15 years uncovering stories involving police misconduct, flaws in Kentucky’s juvenile justice system, questionable taxpayer spending, political corruption, and environmental waste. He's known for holding decision makers accountable. 

Corsey says, “Storytelling is storytelling, no matter what story you’re telling.”

That belief and versatility helped him earn him widespread recognition from his peers. He has received more than 35 Emmy and Murrow Awards and has twice been honored nationally with National Association of Black Journalists Salute to Excellence honors.

Gisselle Medina (they/them) covers immigration, religion, and culture, in California’s Central Valley for Fresnoland as a Report for America corps member. Medina recently won two California News Publishers Association awards for coverage of LGBTQ+ issues and religion. 

Their work has been published in the Los Angeles Times, The Oaklandside, The Frisc, and more. Medina received a master’s degree in journalism, with concentrations in investigative reporting and multimedia, from UC Berkeley, where they also studied English as an undergraduate. 

Originally from Los Angeles, Medina lived in Fresno for 10 years, where they cultivated a passion for helping people while residing in a room and board facility and uncovering disparities faced by the residents.

Gonzalo Magaña is the Director of Special Projects & Community Engagement at ABC10 in Sacramento, where he oversees the station’s investigative unit, special projects, and Race & Culture initiatives. With more than 20 years of experience in journalism and newsroom leadership, he specializes in investigative storytelling, multiplatform content strategy, and leading high-impact reporting teams.

Magaña has helped lead investigative and community-centered journalism that has sparked statewide conversations, prompted reforms, and helped drive legislation in areas including public health, education, and crime. Before ABC10, he spent more than 12 years in Spanish-language news, holding leadership roles at Univision in multiple markets, including San Antonio, Texas.

His work has contributed to journalism recognized with 33 Emmy Awards, multiple Edward R. Murrow Awards, Alfred I. duPont-Columbia Awards, Peabody recognition, IRE Awards, The Gracie Awards, and Walter Cronkite Awards.
speakers.tagLine: ABC10/TEGNA, Director of Special Projects and Engagment
Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press

speakers.bio: Greg is a reporter for The New York Times covering the Pentagon and the U.S. military.

Haley Parsley recently completed a fellowship at the Howard Center for Investigation Journalism. While there, she worked on a long-term reporting project that investigated the rail industry's influence over federal regulatory agencies. She holds a graduate degree in journalism from the University of Maryland, and is a summer reporting intern for The Sacramento Bee’s Capitol Bureau.

Hannah Levintova is the special projects editor and an investigative reporter at Mother Jones and the Center for Investigative Reporting. Her work focuses on uncovering financial wrongdoing in areas that impact daily life, from housing to healthcare, with a particular emphasis on Wall Street. 

Her multimedia reporting on for-profit healthcare has won an Ambie and a National Headliner Award, and was nominated for a 2026 Emmy for Outstanding Business Coverage. She holds an MBA from Columbia Business School, where she was also a Knight-Bagehot Fellow in Business Journalism. Levintova is based in Providence, RI.
speakers.tagLine: Special Projects Editor & Reporter at Mother Jones

speakers.bio: Hannah Natanson is a narrative enterprise reporter for The Washington Post. She most recently covered the Trump administration’s transformation of government, playing a key role on a team recognized with the 2026 Pulitzer Prize for Public Service. Her coverage also won the 2026 Toner Prize for national political reporting and was a finalist for the Poynter First Amendment Prize. She previously reported on education for six years and won a Peabody Award in 2024 for a podcast series on school gun violence. She also was part of a team of Post journalists awarded the 2022 Pulitzer Prize for Public Service for coverage of the Jan. 6 insurrection at the U.S. Capitol; and part of another team of Post journalists who were finalists for the 2020 Pulitzer Prize for Breaking News for coverage of mass shootings in Dayton and El Paso. Natanson graduated from Harvard, where she served as managing editor of The Harvard Crimson.
speakers.tagLine: Narrative enterprise reporter, The Washington Post

speakers.bio: Hannah Riley Fernandez is a writer, communications expert, and organizer based in Burlington, Vermont. She is the Director of Programming at the Center for Just Journalism, supporting journalists and newsrooms who do reporting on public safety issues. She has over a decade of experience at the nexus of media and the criminal legal system and is also a freelance writer.
speakers.tagLine: Director of Programming, the Center for Just Journalism

speakers.bio: Helu Wang is a reporter covering economic development, housing and education for the USA-Today Network.

Holly is data editor at KFF Health News, a national nonprofit newsroom dedicated to health policy and health care coverage. Previously, she was an investigative reporter at The Dallas Morning News. She lives in Maryland.

Howard Berkes is a mostly retired investigative reporter who worked at NPR for 38 years and garnered more than 40 journalism awards for investigative, science, business, sports, breaking news, health, rural and feature reporting. His work included a 12-year span of reporting on coal mine safety and the resurgence of black lung disease. In 1986, Berkes and NPR colleague Daniel Zwerdling reported the first detailed account of the failed 11th-hour effort to stop the fatal launch of the space shuttle Challenger. His reporting in 1998 helped expose the biggest ethics scandal in Olympic history and he's covered eight Olympic Games. Berkes collaborated with ProPublica, the Center for Public Integrity, and PBS Frontline, among others. He was awarded a Nieman Journalism Fellowship at Harvard University in 1997. Berkes helped create and now chairs the governing board of Public Health Watch, an independent non-profit investigative newsroom focused on public, environmental and occupational health.
speakers.tagLine: Retired Correspondent, NPR Investigations; Chair, Governing Board, Public Health Watch

speakers.bio: Hyuntaek is an independent journalist who worked as the assistant editor at The Chosunilbo and served as the board member and chair of the international committee of IRE.
Ijeoma Opara is a journalist and graduate student at the University of Maryland, College Park.

Isabella is an investigative journalist from Mexico. She spent five years at Latinus, covering corruption in the Mexican military and federal government. Previously, she reported for Reforma and El País México, covering political beats, including the Mexican presidency. She is currently a Fulbright scholar pursuing a master's degree at the University of Maryland's Philip Merrill College of Journalism and a fellow at the Howard Center for Investigative Journalism.

Ivy Scott is an engagement reporter for The Marshall Project, responsible for producing community-driven, investigative and public service journalism about immigration and the criminal legal system. Previously, she was a reporter at The Boston Globe, most recently on the climate solutions beat and before that, covering state courts, police accountability, and the Massachusetts Department of Correction. She is a 2024 Livingston Awards finalist and her podcast miniseries on dating and relationships in prison was Webby nominated and won a Society for Features Journalism award.
Award-winning investigative reporter focused on government malfeasance, economic development and immigration in Western New York.

Jacqueline Stenson is a health journalist and educator who currently serves as the manager of projects at the USC Annenberg Center for Health Journalism, where she collaborates with reporters nationwide on major investigative and explanatory projects. She has worked on staff as a reporter and editor with multiple media outlets, including NBC News/MSNBC, The New York Times Syndicate and Condé Nast Publications. Her articles have been published in the Los Angeles Times, Reuters, NBC News, TODAY, Health, Self, Chicago Sun-Times, Dallas Morning News, Detroit Free Press, Washingtonian and more. She taught journalism and creative nonfiction writing classes for more than two decades at the UCLA Extension Writers’ Program. She earned her master’s degree from the Northwestern University Medill School of Journalism and her bachelor’s degree from the Pennsylvania State University College of Communications.
speakers.tagLine: USC Annenberg Center for Health Journalism

speakers.bio: Experienced, versatile, award-winning investigative reporter formerly for The Wall Street Journal, The Washington Post and former editor of the National Catholic Reporter, with skills in public policy, political and government investigations. Frequent lecturer at universities and national professional journalism organizations and mentor of dozens of journalists for several outlets.
Jamie Corey is the Global Research Director for Desmog. She is based in Washington, D.C.

Jamie Grey is the director of investigations for Gray Local Media’s national investigative team, InvestigateTV. She has reported, produced and managed in Missouri, Iowa and Idaho. She was previously a professor and chief investigator at the University of Missouri School of Journalism and KOMU.

Jamitress Bowden is currently the spokeswoman and communications director for the U.S. Committee on House Administration, Democratic Staff. Jamitress has collectively spent about eight years working in the House of Representatives. Previous roles in the People’s House include serving as a spokeswoman for the House Committee on Oversight and Reform – first for the late Chairman Elijah E. Cummings and then for Chairwoman Carolyn B. Maloney. Jamitress also served as the Communications Director for the late Congressman A. Donald McEachin and supported communications for Congressman Bobby Scott in his capacity as the Ranking Member of the Committee on Education and the Workforce.
speakers.tagLine: Communications Director for the U.S. Committee on House Administration, Democratic Staff

speakers.bio: Jane Sasseen is the executive director of the McGraw Center for Business Journalism at CUNY’s Newmark Graduate School of Journalism, which provides grants and editorial support to journalists working on deeply-reported investigative and enterprise stories on business and economic topics. She is an award-winning former Washington bureau chief, senior news editor, and national correspondent for BusinessWeek magazine and editor-in-chief of Politics & Opinion for Yahoo News. Previously, Sasseen spent nine years in Europe, writing for Newsweek, the Christian Science Monitor, The Economist Group and others before joining London-based International Management magazine as Paris bureau chief. She is a recipient of the Gerald Loeb Award, the top prize for financial journalism in America.

Jarrett Ley is a visual forensics reporter at The Washington Post where he mobilizes open-source investigation methods alongside spatial analysis techniques honed through his prior training in architecture. He was among the team of reporters who received the 2024 Pulitzer Prize in Breaking News for coverage of the attempted assassination of Donald Trump in Butler County and the 2026 Emmy Award for Outstanding Investigative Coverage for the Frontline documentary Strike on Iran: The Nuclear Question.
speakers.tagLine: Visual Forensics Reporter, The Washington Post

speakers.bio: Jason Knowles is well known for warning viewers about the latest rip- offs and schemes. Since he began his work as the station's Consumer Investigative Reporter, he's exposed dozens of alleged con-artists on camera and recovered hundreds of thousands of dollars for consumers who say they were wronged by large companies and local businesses. He's also uncovered potential Red Light Camera Traps, DNA delays, Real Estate Scams, failures with the Cook County Property Tax System and problems with toxic Lead Paint and Lead Water in our community.

Jason Leopold is a senior investigative reporter at Bloomberg News and author of the award-winning FOIA Files newsletter. 

He is a recipient of the Gerald Loeb award for investigative reporting, a George Polk award for health reporting and he has twice been a finalist for the Pulitzer Prize in international reporting. He was honored as a finalist in 2021 as one of the lead reporters on the reporting project known as the FinCEN Files, which was based on a massive cache of internal Treasury Department documents Leopold obtained that revealed how banks knowingly profit from corruption and how authorities allow the dark economy to flourish. Leopold was also honored as a finalist in 2018 as a member of the team that investigated suspicious deaths linked back to the Kremlin.

Leopold's FOIA work has been profiled on the front-page of the New York Times and, in 2020, TRAC called Leopold "the most active individual FOIA litigator in the United States."

I make documentary podcast series for NHPR's Document team.

My work includes the Bear Brook podcast, which won praise from Stephen King and The New Yorker magazine. I was a producer on The 13th Step podcast (hosted by Lauren Chooljian) which was a finalist for the Pulitzer Prize in Audio Reporting.
speakers.tagLine: Senior Reporter/Producer, New Hampshire Public Radio

speakers.bio: Jatara McGee is an investigative reporter and anchor at WPXI in her hometown of Pittsburgh. Her work has been recognized with numerous honors, including two regional Edward R. Murrow awards and a Silver Telly Award. Jatara chairs the National Association of Black Journalist's Investigative Task Force and is a proud graduate of the University of Maryland.
Jazmine writes about the people and places involved in the nation’s battles over immigration.

Jeff Hermes currently serves as one of two Deputy Directors of the Media Law Resource Center, where he focuses on digital media law issues. Jeff previously served as the Director of the Digital Media Law Project at Harvard University's Berkman Center for Internet & Society, where he led multiple initiatives to provide free legal resources in response to the needs of independent journalists and online media ventures. Prior to joining the Berkman Center, Jeff assisted a wide array of clients in First Amendment, media, intellectual property and Internet law issues as a partner in the litigation practice of Brown Rudnick LLP and later as counsel to Hermes, Netburn, O'Connor & Spearing, P.C. in Boston. Jeff has represented an international media network and its subsidiaries, major metropolitan newspapers, broadcasters on television and radio, Internet-based publishers and social media networks. He has frequently been interviewed by national and international press outlets, written for numerous publications, spoken at a wide array of events, and been invited to international forums to discuss digital media law issues. Jeff received his J.D. degree from Harvard Law School, and received his undergraduate degree, summa cum laude, from Princeton University.
speakers.tagLine: Deputy Director, Media Law Resource Center

speakers.bio: Jeff Ostrowski covers the U.S. housing market and residential mortgages for Bankrate. His stories have won awards from the National Association of Real Estate Editors and the Society for Advancing Business Editing and Writing.

Jenifer McKim heads the GBH News Center for Investigative Reporting and the Equity and Justice Unit. 

As a multimedia reporter and editor, she specializes in topics, including human trafficking, child welfare, criminal justice, housing, and financial inequality. Her work has earned numerous awards, including two national Edward R. Murrow Awards, a Casey Medal for Meritorious Journalism, and the Freedom of Information Award from the New England First Amendment Coalition. She was a finalist for the 2005 Pulitzer Prize in Public Service.

McKim has worked as a staff reporter at the New England Center for Investigative Reporting, The Boston Globe, the Orange County Register in Southern California and the San Juan Star in Puerto Rico. She is a fellow of the Nieman Foundation for Journalism at Harvard University and serves on the board of the New England First Amendment Coalition. She also speaks French and Spanish. 

She's also running for the IRE board this year.
speakers.tagLine: Senior editor, Investigations and Equity and Justice

speakers.bio: Jennifer S. Forsyth is the Washington Investigations Editor for The New York Times, where she leads a team of seven reporters. The team’s work was recognized in 2026 with the Pulitzer Prize for Investigative Reporting for Trump’s self-enrichment and contributed to a package that won a Polk Award for coverage of Trump’s expansion of power. Previously, she was deputy chief of investigations for The Wall Street Journal. She edited packages that have won the Worth Bingham Award for Investigative Journalism, the New York Press Club’s Award for Investigative Reporting, a SABEW Best in Business Award for Investigative Reporting, and a Gerald Loeb Award for National Reporting. In addition, she edited stories that were part of the Journal’s Pulitzer Prize for National Reporting for Trump’s hush money payments. Jenn loves hard working reporters.
speakers.tagLine: The New York Times, Washington Investigations Editor

speakers.bio: Will provide later

Jennifer Nelson is the Reporters Committee’s director of pre-publication review & journalist support. She leads the organization’s pre-publication review practice, oversees the publication of its legal guides, supervises its legal hotline for journalists, and directs its newsroom legal training program.

Jen counsels journalists and newsrooms through pre-publication review for stories in print, on air, and in podcasts. Since joining the Reporters Committee in 2017, she has represented journalists in public records, court access, subpoena defense, and defamation defense matters in federal and state courts, and has drafted amicus briefs and letters advancing press-freedom interests before federal appellate courts.

Before law school, Jen covered breaking news as an editorial assistant and freelance reporter for The Boston Globe. Jen earned her J.D. from the University of Virginia School of Law in 2011 and her bachelor’s degree in journalism from Northeastern University in 2007.
speakers.tagLine: Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press

speakers.bio: Jennifer Smith Richards is a reporter for ProPublica. She began her journalism career writing obituaries in West Virginia, then covering small-town southern Ohio. She wrote about schools and education at newspapers in Huntington, West Virginia; Utica, New York; Savannah, Georgia, and Columbus, Ohio. She most recently worked for the Chicago Tribune, where her work exposed student ticketing at school, abusive educators, government misspending, sexual abuse in schools, lapses in police accountability and the mistreatment of students with disabilities. Her stories have prompted new state laws, the prosecution of school officials and the creation of child-protection units in school districts and state education departments. Jennifer is a graduate of Ohio University and lives in Chicago.

Jennifer Titus is an investigative reporter for 10TampaBayNews. Through her reporting, Jenn has helped drive policy changes, trigger federal oversight, and expose issues that led to lawsuits and public safety improvements.

eremy Chase is an experienced litigator with a passion for counseling content creators on complex media, intellectual property, and internet law. Jeremy has successfully litigated cases in both state and federal courts involving defamation, copyright, trademark, right-of-publicity, invasion of privacy, SLAPP, reporters' shield laws, prior restraint, open records statutes, access to courts, and all manner of news-gathering issues. Jeremy represents book, magazine, and newspaper publishers; television networks, radio stations, movie studios, and documentary film companies; and a wide array of Internet content and service providers, including the operators of blogs and other online publications; streaming platforms; and the publishers of news, health, technology, and travel websites. Jeremy also has extensive experience representing colleges and universities in libel and intellectual property related matters.
speakers.tagLine: Partner, Media and Intellectual Property at Davis Wright Tremaine LLP

speakers.bio: A 4-time IRE award winner, Jeremy Finley is the chief investigative reporter and evening anchor at WSMV-TV in Nashville, TN. His reporting has resulted in hearings before the U.S. Congress, new state laws, prison sentences  and the discovery of missing girls. His heroes are investigative reporters, so he looks forward to meeting you at the bar.

The stories of Jerry Mitchell have helped put four Klansmen and a serial killer behind bars as well as helping free two people from death row. He is a two-time Pulitzer Prize finalist and winner of 30+ national awards, including a $500,000 MacArthur “genius” grant. He is the author of “Race Against Time” about his pursuit of civil rights cold cases, including Medgar Evers' 1963 assassination, the Birmingham church bombing that killed four girls in 1963 and the KKK’s 1964 killings of three civil rights workers. He now leads Mississippi Today’s investigative team, which has exposed a 20-year reign of terror by deputies known as “the Goon Squad.” Six of those officers went to prison, and the series led to a major change in how the state investigates wrongdoing by officers. His team's work revealed how a Black mother of four had been imprisoned for life for a murder — a conviction contradicted by much of the evidence, including an apparent suicide note never presented to the jury.

Jill Blackman is Director of Data Journalism at Northwestern University's Medill School of Journalism, where she leads the development of data journalism curriculum across undergraduate and graduate programs. She built Medill's data journalism concentration and has trained hundreds of students and professionals to use data, technology, and storytelling to make sense of complex issues.

A journalist, educator, and digital media innovator, Blackman prepares the next generation of reporters through training in data reporting, visualization, and digital storytelling.

Blackman began her career at the Chicago Tribune, where she helped advance multimedia storytelling and earned a Jones Beck Award for Digital Editorial Excellence. A founding member of the Online News Association, she later helped launch local city coverage for Yahoo News and has spent her career exploring how technology can strengthen journalism and storytelling.

Jill has spent more than three decades at The Columbus Dispatch in Ohio before joining InvestigateTV in 2017. She investigated, among other things, naughty teachers, predatory mortgage brokers, slumlords, awful nursing homes, and defective baby products. and For kicks, she digs into sports and has written extensively about the dark side of youth, high school and college athletics.

Huo Jingnan is a reporter curious about how people navigate complex information landscapes and all the actors shaping that journey — people that produce and distribute content, people monitoring the content, and people affected by them.

Previously, she was an associate producer on NPR's Investigations team. She looked into flood-prone homes sold by the federal government, investigated why face mask guidelines differ between countries early in the COVID-19 outbreak, and helped gauge the federal government's role behind black lung disease's resurgence. The projects she was a part of have won awards including Edward Murrow Award, The National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine's Communications award, Silver Gavel Award, and have also been nominated for Emmy Awards and George Foster Peabody awards.

She can be reached via encrypted message at _J_H.07 on Signal.

Joaquin Palomino is an investigative reporter at the San Francisco Chronicle. Since joining the newsroom in 2015, he has covered a wide range of topics, including subpar conditions in aging supportive housing hotels, abuse and understaffing in for-profit psychiatric hospitals and increasingly empty and expensive juvenile detention centers. His work has prompted new state and local laws and other meaningful reforms. He has received several national journalism awards, and was a finalist for the Pulitzer Prize in investigative reporting in 2023 and 2026.

Joce Sterman is a National Murrow, National Headliner and Sigma Delta Chi award-winning investigative journalist who’s spent more than 20 years in TV/radio news. She is the senior national investigative reporter for InvestigativeTV, providing investigative content to Gray Media’s 113 TV stations, their websites and syndicated news magazine shows: InvestigateTV+ and InvestigateTV+ Weekend, which air in major cities nationwide. Her career has taken her through newsrooms across the mid-Atlantic including NBC25 in Hagerstown, FOX43 in Harrisburg, ABC2 in Baltimore and ABC7 in Washington, DC before moving on to national investigations. From accountability reporting on the failures of government agencies at every level to exposing product flaws that put you at risk, her work has resulted in Congressional investigations and federal legislation. She is a proud graduate of Penn State and also teaches Investigative Broadcast Journalism as an adjunct professor at American University in DC.
speakers.tagLine: Senior National Investigative Reporter - InvestigateTV/Gray Media

speakers.bio: Jodi Upton is the Knight Chair at Syracuse University, teaching investigative/data journalism classes. She is a consultant for the Knight Commission and the U.S. Olympic and Paralympic Committee and has been a libel case expert witness. Her research includes college athletics’ spending, and gender inequities in college sports; police misconduct in New York; and mass killings. A transparency advocate, Upton and her students have filed more than 1,000 open records requests in recent years.
speakers.tagLine: Jodi Upton, Knight Chair, Syracuse University

speakers.bio: As Managing Editor of Investigative Content and Collaborations for Cox Media Group, Jodie manages investigations and national projects for eight local television stations, and coordinates reporter training, coaching and development. She previously spent 20+ years as an on-air investigative reporter at NBC4 in Washington, WSB-TV in Atlanta, and WFTV in Orlando. She’s earned many of journalism’s highest honors including two duPont Awards, an IRE Award, a Loeb Award, a national Murrow, 20 Emmys, and led a team that was awarded a Goldsmith special citation and was a finalist for the Pulitzer Prize for Public Service. She has served on IRE’s Board of Directors since 2019. She currently leads the Academic committee which is spearheading student chapters on college campuses and focuses on improving IRE’s resources and programming for student members and professors.
speakers.tagLine: Cox Media Group, Managing Editor of Investigative Content and Collaborations

speakers.bio:
Joe Dana is a multi-media journalist for NBC-affiliate 12News in Phoenix. His investigative projects focus on public education and energy policy. 
Joe's reports on schools examine:
-false claims about funding routinely made by elected leaders
-how federal, state and local tax dollars support three systems of education in Arizona (school districts, charter schools, ESA private vouchers)
-how money flows through ESA's, now the subject of an ongoing public records lawsuit by 12News against the AZ Superintendent and State Treasurer 
Joe's energy coverage, Power & Influence, examines climate and social justice concerns in the energy sector. His reporting exposed faulty customer service at Arizona's largest utility, resulting in a $25 million class settlement. His investigation about utility shut-offs during extreme heat in 2025 was credited by the Arizona Attorney General for helping prompt a $7 million settlement for low-income customers in April 2026.

Joe Stephens is the newly named executive editor of the Investigative Reporting Workshop and a professor at American University.

He previously served as the founding director of the Program in Journalism at Princeton University, where he was Ferris Professor in Residence for more than a decade. A veteran investigative reporter, he is a three-time winner of the George Polk Award and a three-time finalist for the Pulitzer Prize.

Stephens joined the Washington Post in 1999, where he was a member of the investigative projects team for more than 16 years. He has written extensively on presidential politics, political corruption, the war against terrorism, Afghan reconstruction, the federal judiciary, and drug experiments conducted on children in the developing world.

He has reported and lectured in Europe, Africa, Asia, Latin America, and across the U.S. His stories have led to Congressional hearings, national legislative reforms, criminal convictions, and millions of dollars in fines.

Stephens has won more than a dozen other national honors, including top awards from the Overseas Press Club, the Gerald Loeb Foundation, the Society of Professional Journalists, the Scripps Howard Foundation, Investigative Reporters and Editors, and the Annie E. Casey Foundation. 

He has been a finalist on multiple occasions for Harvard’s Goldsmith Prize, the Gerald Loeb award for financial reporting, and the Investigative Reporters and Editors award. Stephens has judged many major contests, and served on Pulitzer Prize juries in 2019 (Criticism) and 2020 (Investigative Reporting). He served on the board of the Fund for Investigative Journalism.
speakers.tagLine: Executive Editor, Investigative Reporting Workshop & Professor at American University

speakers.bio:
John Cardinale is the National Investigative Correspondent for Hearst Television based in Washington, D.C. He is passionate about telling impactful stories and holding those in power accountable.
Cardinale previously worked at KOAT in Albuquerque, New Mexico, where he extensively investigated the Rust movie set shooting involving Alec Baldwin. His reporting uncovered flaws in the investigation, and his findings were referenced during trial proceedings.
Cardinale has received numerous state, regional, and national honors for his investigative journalism, including Regional Emmy and Edward R. Murrow Awards.
He is a proud graduate of Arizona State University's Walter Cronkite School of Journalism and Mass Communication.

John is a freelance investigative journalist focused on corruption, corporate misconduct, money-laundering and leaks. He has bylines on these subjects with the AJC, Post and Courier, OCCRP and others. John is also the founder of Investigative Simulations Lab, an organization that develops and delivers simulation games that show journalists how to follow the money.

John is an engineering manager at Freedom of the Press Foundation (FPF), leading the SecureDrop team. Prior to FPF, he worked as a software engineer at 18F within the U.S. federal government, at Cornell University Library, and as a startup co-founder. His career is dedicated to transparency, open knowledge, and open source software in support of democracy.
speakers.tagLine: Freedom of the Press Foundation -- SecureDrop Engineering Manager

speakers.bio:
Jordan Elder is an investigative reporter and fill-in anchor with News 4 and Fox SA.

During her time in San Antonio, Jordan has been a leader in reporting on dangerous dog issues following several brutal attacks. Her public records requests and data reporting led to the city releasing a map of where registered dangerous dogs live.

Jordan frequently covers immigration issues, the Southern border, and was one of the first reporters on scene during the tragedy at Robb Elementary. She spent several weeks reporting on the deadly flooding in Kerrville, Texas, providing in-depth coverage of the disaster and its aftermath. She spends her free time investigating celebrity gossip and reality TV.
speakers.tagLine: Investigative Reporter, News 4 and Fox San Antonio

speakers.bio: Joseph Darius Jaafari is the founder and editor in chief of LOOKOUT, the Southwest's only nonprofit newsroom focused on accountability journalism for and about LGBTQ+ people and the issues they care about.

Josh Dawsey is a political investigations and enterprise reporter for The Wall Street Journal. He most recently worked as a political enterprise and investigations reporter for the Washington Post. He joined the Post in 2017 and previously covered the White House. Josh was part of a team of journalists who won the Pulitzer Prize for Public Service in 2022 for the newspaper’s coverage of Jan. 6 and won the 2024 Pulitzer Prize for Public Service for the newspaper's coverage of the role of the AR-15 in American life. He also won the 2026 Aldo Beckman Award for Excellence in White House Coverage and is a two-time recipient of the White House Correspondents Association award for news reporting. He is also a lecturer at the Allbritton Journalism Institute.

Josh is a proud graduate of the University of South Carolina and the enthusiastic owner of a rambunctious rescue dog named Pepper.
Senior Bloomberg Businessweek reporter covering the workplace

Josh Hinkle is KXAN’s director of investigations and innovation, leading the Austin, Texas, station’s 13-person investigative team on multiple platforms. He also leads KXAN’s political coverage as the executive producer and host of “State of Texas,” a weekly program focused on the Texas Legislature and elections, seen in 14 markets statewide. Josh also serves as IRE Board President.

Josh helps to lead the network’s coverage of crime and courts. His reporting appears on Good Morning America, World News Tonight, Nightline, 20/20, ABC Radio and ABC documentaries. Josh was a coordinator of the network's coverage of the coronavirus pandemic and the 2021 insurrection at the US Capitol. He has chronicled the rise of the modern hate movement in America and has led the network’s reporting on the explosion of political violence and the case of Luigi Mangione. In 2022-23, Josh was a senior producer on ABC’s yearlong examination of the schoolhouse massacre in Uvalde, Texas. 

Before joining ABC News in 2013, Josh held reporting positions at newspapers across the country, most recently with The New York Post and The Newark Star-Ledger. 

Josh is the co-author of the bestselling 2011 investigative book, The Jersey Sting. And he is the recipient of a number of honors, including eight Emmy Awards and the Pulitzer Prize.

Juan Lorenzo Holmann Chamorro is the Publisher of LA PRENSA, Nicaragua’s oldest newspaper. For its pro-democracy stance, his family has endured decades of persecution, including the 1978 assassination of his uncle, then-publisher Pedro J Chamorro.
Earn a degree from LSU. Alongside his business career, he led several social development and micro-finance organizations.
In 2019, he took the helm of LP during a brutal crackdown by the Ortega-Murillo regime. Despite systemic harassment, newsprint embargoes, and a violent police raid, he successfully moved operations to the cloud to keep the paper running digitally. For his resistance, he was unjustly imprisoned for 545 days under inhumane conditions.
In 2023, he was stripped of his citizenship and exiled to the U.S. He serves as regional VP of the Inter American Press Association (IAPA) and president of RELPEX. LP celebrated its centenary in 2026. UNESCO World Press Freedom Prize and King of Spain International Journalism Award.

Juan Pablo Garnham is a Chilean journalist interested in cities, public policy, and immigrant communities. In his previous work as the Urban Affairs reporter for The Texas Tribune, Juan Pablo reported on the main challenges of Texas’ largest metro areas — Houston, Austin, San Antonio, El Paso and Dallas-Fort Worth. Juan Pablo has also worked as senior producer for the podcast In The Thick, editor of CityLab Latino, and City Hall reporter for El Diario in New York. In Chile, he wrote for some of the country’s most important magazines and newspapers. Juan Pablo brings his expertise as a bilingual and multimedia journalist to his position as the Communication & Policy Engagement Manager for the Eviction Lab.

Julie Watts is a national-award-winning investigative correspondent for CBS News California. 

Based in California’s State Capitol, Julie's reporting combines watchdog journalism with solutions-focused storytelling to expose systemic failures, hold the powerful accountable, spur legislative reforms, increase transparency and drive change.

Julie K. Brown has won some of the top investigative prizes in journalism for exposing corruption and human rights abuses in the nation’s criminal justice system. Her 2018 investigation “Perversion of Justice,” led to the arrests of sex traffickers Jeffrey Epstein and Ghislaine Maxwell; the resignation of the U.S. secretary of labor; the first arrest of a British Royal in 400 years – and the downfall of countless other captains of industry, politics, and academia around the world. 
The ensuing scandal led to the release of millions of Justice Department files about Epstein’s sex trafficking and money laundering empire -- and ignited a global reckoning about the delicate balance between power, wealth and morality. 
Her effort to persuade a reluctant police chief and traumatized survivors to go on the record for the first time stands as a distinguished example of the importance of investigative reporting. That work was recently awarded a special citation by the Pulitzer Prize Board.
Juliet Linderman is an investigative reporter with The Associated Press based in Baltimore. Her reporting focuses on government and corporate accountability, supply chains and inequality.

Justin Gray is the Consumer Investigator for WSB-TV Channel 2 Action News in Atlanta.
His investigations have brought changes to state laws and federal rules and have won some of the highest awards in journalism.
He’s also worked as the Washington Correspondent for the Cox Media Group and as a reporter at WAGA-TV in Atlanta as well as at stations in Philadelphia, California and West Virginia.
Justin has an MS in Journalism from Columbia University and a BA in History and Political Science from Emory University.

Justin Lum is an Emmy and Murrow-award-winning investigative reporter for FOX10 Phoenix (KSAZ-TV). Since joining the station in 2019, Justin has been committed to become one of Arizona's most trusted voices in crime, courts, and watchdog reporting. He is nationally recognized for his longtime coverage of the Lori Vallow/Chad Daybell case, breaking several details in the investigation. He also reported extensively on a massive Medicaid fraud scheme targeting vulnerable Native Americans, which ultimately cost Arizona $2.8 billion. Over the past four years, FOX10 Investigates has been entrenched in longform journalism, in-depth reporting and documentaries. Justin is also the former President of the Asian American Journalists Association (AAJA) Arizona chapter.

Justin Nobel writes for US magazines and investigative sites, his 2024 book, Petroleum-238: Big Oil’s Dangerous Secret and the Grassroots Fight to Stop It, was inspired by an award-winning Rolling Stone magazine story on oil and gas radioactivity. His work has been published in Best American Science and Nature Writing and Best American Travel Writing as well as law and scientific journals.
speakers.tagLine: Investigative science journalist, Rolling Stone, DeSmog

speakers.bio: Justin is a data reporter at the AJC.

Kaley Johnson is an independent journalist in North Texas. She previously worked at the Belleville News Democrat and the Fort Worth Star-Telegram. She has written more than 50 stories on federal prisons in North Texas since 2020.  Her coverage has focused on systemic sexual abuse, medical neglect, a fatal lack of air conditioning in a men's prison, COVID policy failures, the rights of incarcerated trans people and other issues in the Bureau of Prisons. Her investigation into sexual abuse at a women's prison in Fort Worth prompted a congressman to call for a judiciary hearing and federal inquiry into the prison.  She has been a freelance reporter since October 2023.

Kara Kenney is an Emmy and Murrow award winning investigative reporter with WRTV in Indianapolis. She has 25 years of experience in the television news industry. Her investigations focus on government fraud and waste, as well as consumer frauds and ripoffs.

Kara McGuirk-Allison is a senior radio editor for the Center for Investigative Reporting, where she works on podcast strategy and audio production. In her three decades of audio work, she has produced for a number of NPR news programs, including the award-winning Justice Talking, and was the founding producer of NPR’s Hidden Brain. Before joining CIR, Kara was a podcast producer for Marvel/Disney.
speakers.tagLine: Center for investigative Reporting/Reveal/More To The Story

speakers.bio: Karen Wei is a sophomore at the University of Houston and serves as the news editor at The Cougar. Her high school journalism work was recognized with multiple Best of School Newspapers Online awards, and she was one of six high school seniors in Texas to receive a scholarship from the Texas Association of Journalism Educators. She focuses on spotlighting policy issues through the voices of so-called ordinary people. Karen is also involved in ongoing research on political communication and the effects of media coverage on federal policymaking.
speakers.tagLine: News Editor, The Cougar (University of Houston)

speakers.bio:
Karen Ye is an award-winning journalist who has spent the past decade producing documentaries for VICE News, CBS News, and ABC News. As a senior producer on ABC News Studios' Emmy-winning documentary series IMPACT x Nightline, she covered the crypto industry, the TikTok ban, Saudi "sportswashing", and the rise of ultra-fast fashion giant Shein. For 60 Minutes+, she produced the first on-camera interview with Jacob Chansley, the "QAnon Shaman", who spoke from jail about his role in the January 6 Capitol riots. 

A fluent Mandarin speaker who grew up in China, she also covered Asia for VICE News Tonight on HBO, securing rare interviews with Chinese dissidents, billionaires, government officials, as well as a high-ranking executive at the secretive telecom giant Huawei. Her work has earned numerous awards, including an Overseas Press Club award for her VICE documentary "Year of the Dog".

Kat Stafford is the global race and justice editor for Reuters, leading coverage worldwide. An award‑winning journalist, Stafford was previously a national investigative race writer and global investigations correspondent at the Associated Press. Her honors include the National Press Club Journalism Institute’s 2023 Neil and Susan Sheehan Award for Investigative Journalism and Wayne State University’s 2024 Spirit of Diversity Award. A 2022 University of Michigan Knight‑Wallace Fellow, Stafford produced an award‑winning investigative series on generational health inequities affecting Black Americans. She is a mentor, newsroom diversity advocate and board member with NABJ’s Investigative Task Force and USC’s Center for Health Journalism. She is a past vice president of the Investigative Reporters and Editors' advisory board of directors.
speakers.tagLine: Reuters, Global Race and Justice Editor

speakers.bio: Kate Grumke is the environment and data reporter at St. Louis Public Radio, her hometown NPR station. Before joining STLPR, she was an award-winning TV and documentary producer based in DC. Kate is a graduate of the University of Missouri School of Journalism and Columbia University’s Lede Program in data journalism.
speakers.tagLine: Environment and Data Reporter, St. Louis Public Radio

speakers.bio: Kate Howard is vice president of the IRE board and an editorial director at The Center for Investigative Reporting, where she works for the Reveal radio show and podcast and Mother Jones. Previously, she spent four years as managing editor at the Kentucky Center for Investigative Reporting, a nonprofit newsroom by Louisville Public Media. There, Howard oversaw a team of five reporters and edited the investigative podcast Dig, which won an IRE Award and was nominated for a Peabody Award. Previously, Kate spent nearly 14 years as a newspaper reporter, including stints at The Tennessean, The Florida Times-Union and the Omaha World-Herald. She has served on the IRE board since 2022, and is also on the board of directors at Louisville Public Media. Howard is based in Louisville, Kentucky.
speakers.tagLine: Reveal from The Center for Investigative Reporting

speakers.bio: Kate Martin is a correspondent at APM Reports. Over two decades, her investigative reporting has driven policy reforms, forced resignations, led to criminal indictments and spurred changes to at least five state laws or legal precedents. Most recently, she uncovered widespread violations of Illinois laws by hospitals meant to protect sexual assault survivors.

Katherine Dailey is a reporter at the Michigan Advance, covering state politics and policy around the Great Lakes State with a focus on law enforcement, courts and civil rights issues. She's previously covered housing, healthcare and government spending in Washington D.C., Chicago and New Jersey. She's a graduate of Northwestern University's MSJ program, as well as an alum of Princeton University.

Katherine Jacobsen is the U.S., Canada and Caribbean Program Coordinator at the Committee to Protect Journalists where she focuses on documenting press freedom violations and supporting journalists at risk. She has authored two special reports, one on the second Trump administration's impact on media freedom, and the other on the state of local press freedom in the United States. Prior to working at CPJ, Katherine was a foreign correspondent in Russia with the AP, and in Ukraine as a freelancer.

Katherine Reynolds Lewis is the founder of the Institute for Independent Journalists, which supports freelancers of color; special projects editor on the science of love for Greater Good Magazine; and a columnist for Nieman Reports on media careers and equity in journalism. An award-winning science journalist and author, her work has appeared in the Atlantic, Elemental, Fortune, New York Times, Parents, Slate, Undark, and Washington Post. Her book on children’s behavioral and mental health, The Good News About Bad Behavior, grew out of the most-read story Mother Jones has ever published. Fellowships include O’Brien Public Service Journalism, MIT Knight Science Journalism, Medill and RJI innovation. She has taught courses or lectured at the journalism schools of American, Marquette, Northwestern, the University of Southern California, and the University of Texas, Austin. A Harvard physics graduate, Katherine previously worked as a national correspondent for Newhouse and Bloomberg News.
speakers.tagLine: Founder and CEO, The Institute for Independent Journalists
Arnolt Center for Investigative Journalism at Indiana University

speakers.bio: Kathryn Hurd is a senior reporter at UC Berkeley's nonprofit newsroom and teaching institute, the Investigative Reporting Program. Her reporting has appeared in ProPublica, the Los Angeles Times, PBS FRONTLINE, KQED, and the San Francisco Examiner. In 2022, she was part of the George Polk Award-winning team behind the FRONTLINE documentary American Insurrection. In 2023, her investigation into Los Angeles County's foster care housing shortage contributed to policy changes that ended the county's practice of housing foster youth in hotels. She is currently at work on her next investigation focused on the child welfare system.
speakers.tagLine: Senior reporter, UC Berkeley's Investigative Reporting Program

speakers.bio: Kathy Jones is CPJ’s director of research & analysis, and was formerly deputy editorial director for CPJ. She works on CPJ's global reports on killed, and imprisoned journalists, and assists the regional, advocacy, and emergencies teams with research projects. For more than two decades, she has helped shape and tell stories with data, reporting, and visuals for news organizations including Reuters, Bloomberg, and Newsweek.

Katie Campbell is a national Emmy award-winning filmmaker and journalist at ProPublica where she produces investigative documentary films. She has focused on in-depth science reporting throughout her career and has been recognized by National Academies of Sciences and the American Association for the Advancement of Science. 

Campbell recently helped produce ProPublica's multi-year, multi-award-winning "Sick in a Hospital Town," which explores how a hospital system became the most powerful economic and political force in Albany, Georgia. As the hospital expanded, health outcomes worsened, costs rose and the city suffered severe, long-standing health disparities. The driving question of the project is—Why were residents so sick, when the most powerful institution in town was a hospital?

Katie Colaneri is the senior editor of podcasts at NHPR, where she leads the Document team, the station's award-winning longform narrative audio unit. Prior to joining NHPR, Katie was the assistant news director at WHYY in Philadelphia, where she also worked as a reporter covering energy and politics. Katie is still based in the Philadelphia area.

Katie Licari is an investigative reporter for AfroLA. She is a former LA Times data fellow and a graduate of UC Berkeley Graduate School of Journalism. When she isn't reporting she can be found in her community garden tending to her tomatoes.

Katie McQue is an award-winning freelance investigative journalist based in Brooklyn, NY. Her coverage focuses on human rights, big tech, the defense sector, policy and regulation. Her work has been published by the Guardian, Washington Post, International Consortium of Investigative Journalists, Thomson Reuters Foundation, New Scientist, Rappler, and others. She is also a US correspondent for the UK national newspaper The Observer.

Katie Wilcox is an investigative reporter, editor, data journalist and educator with more than a decade of experience exposing waste, fraud and abuse. She leads teams in holding the powerful accountable and empowering communities to enact change. Her newsroom leadership and collaborative reporting has prompted legislation, criminal and civil investigations, terminations and helped put money back in the hands of those who were victimized.
speakers.tagLine: Investigative Reporter & Editor | The Beam at ASU

speakers.bio: Katrina Manson is a Bloomberg reporter covering the intersection of technology, national security and defense-tech. She is author of the new book Project Maven: A Marine Colonel, His Team, and the Dawn of AI Warfare. She was previously US foreign policy and defense reporter for the Financial Times, and before that spent more than a decade reporting in several African countries, including as the FT's East Africa correspondent.
speakers.tagLine: Author of Project Maven: A Marine Colonel, His Team, and the Dawn of AI Warfare

speakers.bio:
Kayla Solomon is the Investigative Reporter at FOX13 in Memphis. In eight years as a professional journalist, she’s covered everything from protests and COVID-19 mandates in Macon, Georgia, to the trials of former Memphis Police officers charged in the death of Tyre Nichols and the ongoing Memphis Safe Task Force operations.

She’s a proud Temple University alum, previously serving as the President of the Temple Association of Black Journalists. She is the current Co-Chair of the National Association of Black Journalists Young Black Journalists Task Force.
Kayla Zhu is a member of Visualping's marketing team and is a former data journalist.
Kelly Dietz is a passionate journalist and national award-winning investigative producer for KARE11.

Kelly Glass is an independent journalist and solo publisher whose interests focus on culture, family, and policy. Her writing has appeared in The New York Times, Washington Post, National Geographic, Health, and many more national publications. She was the founding executive editor of Parents magazine's Kindred, their first brand for Black families. Currently, she's the project manager for IIJ's freelance marketplace project and the founder and EIC of The VP Chicago, a news initiative centering the information needs of Chicago's most disinvested neighborhoods.
speakers.tagLine: Founder and EIC of The VP Chicago

speakers.bio: Kendall Taggart recently joined ProPublica's new business investigations team. Previously, she was a reporter on the global investigations team at Bloomberg News and BuzzFeed News.  She has written about everything from private equity to the NYPD to nonprofits. Her work has received a George Polk award for health reporting and Gerald Loeb awards for investigative business journalism.

Keri Blakinger is an investigative journalist at ProPublica and author of the critically acclaimed 2022 memoir, "Corrections in Ink." She is a Pulitzer finalist and producer of the Oscar-nominated short documentary, "I Am Ready Warden."  She has spent more than a decade covering prisons and capital punishment, working as a staff writing for the New York Daily News, the Houston Chronicle, The Marshall Project and the LA Times. Her work has appeared everywhere from the BBC to VICE to the Washington Post Magazine, where her 2019 reporting on women in jail helped earn a National Magazine Award for best single-topic issue.

A self-described “recovering” news director, Kevin Benz is well-known as an inspiring, engaging speaker. He coaches thousands of journalists and hundreds of newsrooms around the country with his own style of pragmatic, ethical craft. His 30 years in newsrooms and reporting on the streets provide a unique understanding of what journalists need.
He began his career as an award-winning videographer and became a nationally recognized journalism leader — serving 14 years as news director in Austin.
His teams received national recognition from organizations including the Sigma Delta Chi awards, the National Edward R. Murrow awards, three National Walter Cronkite awards for excellence in Political Journalism and the DuPont-Columbia awards. 
Kevin is former Chairman of RTDNA and the Texas AP Broadcasters. He has served as visiting faculty for the Poynter Institute. He currently serves as leadership faculty with the Carole Kneeland Project for Responsible Journalism.

Kevin Ozebek is an investigative reporter for ABC7 Eyewitness News. He comes to us from KTRK-TV in Houston, where he reported on the environmental impact of orphan wells along the Texas coast, brought attention to chronically flooding homes, as well as the aftermath of Hurricane Harvey's destruction.

Before joining KTRK, Kevin worked as an investigative reporter at WSVN-TV in Miami. During his time there, he exposed how South Floridians were wrongly billed by local utilities and elder care facilities that were failing to meet state standards. He also investigated environmental hazards and how climate change impacted the South Florida landscape.

Shortly after the tragic deadly condo collapse in Surfside, Florida, Kevin uncovered that city's mayor owned an apartment building with crumbling balconies and multiple violations.

Khadeeja Safdar is an enterprise reporter at The Wall Street Journal, where she has worked for more than a decade specializing in investigations and high-impact scoops. Her reporting on Jeffrey Epstein’s network and his 50th birthday book was a finalist for the 2026 Pulitzer Prize for Public Service and helped provoke the release of millions of Justice Department documents. She was also part of the team that won the 2025 Pulitzer Prize for National Reporting for a series on Elon Musk’s political and personal evolution, and a 2020 Pulitzer finalist for Investigative Reporting for a series on unsafe products on Amazon’s marketplace. She is writing a book about her reporting on Epstein.
speakers.tagLine: Enterprise Reporter, The Wall Street Journal

speakers.bio: Kim Kleman is the executive director of Report for America. The nonprofit organization has recruited, placed and mentored more than 750 reporters in 450 local newsrooms throughout the country—and just announced, with the Investigative Editing Corps, plans to expand their ability to help these newsrooms produce local investigations. Previously, Kleman was editor-in-chief of Consumer Reports, editor-in-chief at The American Lawyer, and an editor and reporter at the St. Petersburg Times (now the Tampa Bay Times). Her passion for local news and local investigations was sparked at her first job, covering six small towns in Polk County, Florida, for the Tampa Tribune. She lives in Marlborough, MA.

Kirstin Garriss is an independent journalist and the PR & Communications Director for URL Collective, a nonprofit committed to empowering independent newsrooms through education and training initiatives, grant funding and operational support. Garriss is an award-winning political journalist turned public relations strategist with more than 14 years of newsroom experience. She has worked in local and national TV newsrooms in Washington, DC, Maryland, North Carolina, and Tennessee, covering city halls, state houses, Capitol Hill and the White House. She has won regional and national awards and her freelance work has appeared in The Guardian and EBONY Magazine. Garriss provides public relations and marketing for URL Collective itself, elevating the program’s impact and increasing awareness of its services. Additionally, she supports URL Media partners by amplifying their stories through strategic media placements.
speakers.tagLine: independent journalist and PR & Communications Director, URL Collective

speakers.bio: Kristen Hare is the director of craft and local news at the Poynter Institute. Before joining Poynter's teaching team, Kristen spent eight years covering the local news industry. Her career includes five years writing obits about regular people for the Tampa Bay Times, and 10 years as a features and projects writer in newsrooms in St. Louis and St. Joseph, Missouri. Kristen served in the Peace Corps in Guyana and graduated from Mizzou. She's also a travel writer who writes books about the Sunshine State.
speakers.tagLine: Director of craft and local news, Poynter Institute

speakers.bio:
Kyle Iboshi is an award-winning investigative reporter at KGW, Portland's NBC affiliate. Since joining the station in 2000, he has covered a wide range of local, national, and international stories — from the 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami to the Winter Olympics in Vancouver.
Kyle has participated in numerous journalism fellowships, including the RIAS Germany-U.S. Journalists Exchange, the East-West Center's Korea-U.S. Journalists Exchange, an international reporting fellowship from the International Center for Journalists, the Journalists Law School at Loyola Law School, and the Kiplinger Fellowship at Ohio State University.
He has earned recognition for investigative reporting projects, including coverage of Japanese tsunami debris and for the series "The Cost of Collections," which exposed aggressive government debt-collection tactics.

Kyle Spencer, an award-winning journalist and former New York Times contributor, and the author of Raising Them Right.  She is the founding editor of Reporting Right, a weekly guide for journalists who cover the truth; and the CEO of The Pro-Democracy Information Lab. 

She has an expertise in narrative storytelling. She is a frequent contributor on MSNBC, podcasts and NPR, and often conducts journalism workshops and seminars. 

Spencer spent five years reporting on her critically acclaimed book Raising Them Right (Ecco/Harper Collins 2022) which explores how radical extremists message anti-democratic ideals via social media, online celebrity culture and false facts.

Laird Townsend is an award-winning reporter and editor and the director of Freelance Investigative Reporters and Editors (FIRE). As the only organization that exclusively supports freelancers doing investigative stories, FIRE has facilitated investigations for outlets like the Boston Globe, Washington Post, Esquire, and the BBC, via a range of reporting grants and services, from contract-related legal assistance to the $25,000 Greenlight Grants. As a freelancer, Townsend himself reported for publications from the Dallas Morning News to Mother Jones. In 2018, his coverage of migrant labor won the SPJ Peter Lisagor Award for online investigative/public service reporting.
speakers.tagLine: Director, Freelance Investigative Reporters and Editors (FIRE)

speakers.bio: Lam Thuy Vo is a journalist who marries data analysis with on-the-ground reporting to examine how systems and policies affect individuals. She is currently an investigative reporter working with Documented, an independent, non-profit newsroom dedicated to reporting with and for immigrant communities, and an associate professor of data journalism at the Craig Newmark Graduate School of Journalism. Previously, she was a journalist at The Markup, BuzzFeed News, The Wall Street Journal, Al Jazeera America and NPR's Planet Money.
speakers.tagLine: Associate Professor & Investigative Reporter, CUNY & Documented

speakers.bio:
LaMonica Peters is a reporter for The LA Local, covering Inglewood and South LA since November 2025. 

She spent 14 years in broadcast news. Her work has been seen on ABC News Live, CNN, CBS News, Spectrum News Network and LiveNOW from FOX.

LaMonica has taught an online, broadcast news writing course at Palomar College since 2021. She's also taught at De Anza College, Mississippi State University and Mississippi Valley State University.

LaMonica earned a B.A. in Communication Studies from UCLA and an M.A. from Cal State LA.

Lance Washington is currently the Production Manager for InvestigateTV+. He has worked in newsrooms at stations in New Orleans, LA and Atlanta, GA. In addition, he does freelance work with various organizations, most notably as a senior member of the production staff at the Caesars Superdome and Smoothie King Center in New Orleans.
Lance is an award winning photojournalist, earning multiple recognitions from the Murrow awards, NATAS (Emmys), the Press Club of New Orleans, Atlanta Association of Black Journalists, Louisiana Associated Press, and others. He serves as the chair of the Visual Task Force for NABJ.

Laura Bennett is the founder and director of The Center for Just Journalism, an organization dedicated to fostering a media environment that equips people with the information they need to build safer communities. The Center connects journalists with reliable information on critical public safety and criminal legal issues and helps reporters, editors, and educators identify and implement practice changes that foster rigor, curiosity, and nuance, rather than sensationalism and anecdotes. Since its founding in 2022, the Center's work has directly resulted in impactful journalism and enduring practice change at news outlets across the country. Prior to founding the Center, she worked at FWD.us and The Pew Charitable Trusts where she led major policy and research projects in states across the country.
speakers.tagLine: Director, The Center for Just Journalism

speakers.bio:
Laura Geller is an Emmy, Gracie, Murrow, Associated Press and Telly winning journalist for CBS News. Laura's work as a national investigative producer has impacted lives across the country. 

Over the course of 20 years in journalism, her stories have prompted changes and new legislation on a local, state and national level.

Before joining the CBS Innovation Lab, Laura worked on Capitol Hill, reporting and producing stories about the impact of the political process on people's every day lives.

Laura moved to Washington to join the investigative team at WUSA9. There, her award-winning work changed multiple laws and led to the creation of new ones. 

Prior to joining WUSA9, Laura investigated issues at WVEC-TV in Norfolk, VA, and NBC12 in Richmond, VA. She began her career in Tallahassee, FL.

Laura is the proud wife of a Navy retiree and mom to a spunky little girl. She was born and raised in New York and graduated from the Medill School of Journalism at Northwestern University.

Laura Jael Kurtzberg is a data visualization specialist, cartographer, and a news applications developer with a particular interest in environmental stories. Laura has worked at the intersection of data journalism and design with organizations like InfoAmazonia, Ambiental Media, WLRN Public Media, and Mongabay.

She earned her MFA in Interactive Media from the University of Miami and a BA in Information Science from the University of Arizona. She remembers her first forays into data and communication with groups at Biosphere2 (the space colony training ground featured in 'Spaceship Earth') and The Santa Fe Institute (a complexity science research center).

In her free time, Laura enjoys reading fiction, trying to learn how to play the lever harp, and crocheting!

Laura Meckler is senior national education writer for the Washington Post, where she covers the news, politics and people shaping American schools. She previously reported on the White House, presidential politics, immigration, and health care for the Wall Street Journal, as well as on health and social policy for the Associated Press. Her honors include a Nieman Fellowship and a Livingston Award for National Reporting for her coverage of organ transplantation, and she was part of a team that won the George Polk Award for Justice Reporting for a series on the life of George Floyd. She is the author of the award-winning book DREAM TOWN: Shaker Heights and the Quest for Racial Equity, about her hometown. She lives in Washington, D.C., with her husband and two sons.
speakers.tagLine: National Education Writer, The Washington Post

speakers.bio: Laura Moscoso is a journalist and educator with 15 years of experience, currently living in Manatí, Puerto Rico. She has worked in small nonprofits, national publications, and both public and private universities. Laura joined the IRE training team in 2022, where she leads custom training, the Spanish training program, and collaboration efforts with sister organizations. When she's not working, you might find her at the beach, enjoying live music, or at the movies, eating lots of popcorn. She is a foodie and a reader.

Lauren Chooljian is a senior reporter/producer on NHPR’s DOCUMENT team, a narrative-driven, long form and often investigative reporting project. She is perhaps best known as the host and reporter behind The 13th Step, a podcast about sexual misconduct in the addiction treatment industry. The 13th Step won a duPont-Columbia award, was named one of the best podcasts of 2023 by The New Yorker, The Atlantic and Vogue, and was a finalist for the 2024 Pulitzer Prize in Audio reporting. Before joining NHPR in 2017, Lauren spent nearly six years as a reporter, producer and fill-in host for WBEZ in Chicago.
IRE staff

Lauren is Freedom of the Press Foundation’s first Daniel Ellsberg Chair on Government Secrecy, a position established to honor and continue the legendary whistleblower’s fight for secrecy reform. Her work highlights how excessive government secrecy prevents the public from meaningfully participating in self-government in every area that secrecy permeates, from public health to foreign policy. She advocates for commonsense improvements to the classification system, reforms to the Freedom of Information Act, and increased government accountability. Harper previously served as the public policy director for the National Security Archive in Washington, D.C., helping researchers get historically significant government documents declassified, and served as a member of the federal FOIA Advisory Committee.
speakers.tagLine: Ellsberg chair on government secrecy, Freedom of the Press Foundation

speakers.bio:
Lauren Mucciolo has produced and directed more than a dozen documentaries for PBS Frontline, including international co-productions with the BBC’s Storyville, Panorama and Current Affairs series, and Channel 4. Her most recent documentaries are the Emmy-nominated "Born Poor" and "Alaska's Vanishing Native Villages," both released on Frontline in 2025.

A seven-time Emmy nominee and two-time Grierson nominee, Lauren has won two RFK Journalism Awards, an Online Journalism Award, a Best Director Award from the Royal Television Society, a SXSW Film Festival Jury Prize, among other honors. She came to the Cronkite School in 2019 from Brooklyn, NY to help launch the Howard Center for Investigative Journalism as its inaugural executive producer and overseen stories published in top news outlets and recognized with 70+ awards. She was an editor on the HC collaboration with The AP, Frontline and U. Maryland on "Lethal Restraint," a finalist for a Pulitzer Prize and a Murrow Award winner.
speakers.tagLine: Executive Producer, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism ASU

speakers.bio: Lautaro Grinspan writes about immigration policy & enforcement, labor, and daily-life issues within Georgia's diverse immigrant communities for The Atlanta Journal-Constitution.
speakers.tagLine: Immigration Reporter at The Atlanta Journal-Constitution

speakers.bio: Leah Dunn is the Executive Producer of special projects and investigations at WSB-TV in Atlanta. Leah started her career as a photographer and worked her way up to become a photographer/producer for nationally known consumer advocate Clark Howard and the WSB investigative team. She’s passionate about creating consumer-focused content that helps people make informed decisions and connects with audiences through meaningful storytelling.
Leah Rosenbaum is an investigative reporter at The War Horse, a nonprofit newsroom that investigates military and veteran issues  and exposes the true cost of military service.

Lena Inthisenesouk is an Inbound Sales Development Representative at Everlaw, where she helps journalists, nonprofits, and pro bono teams access technology that supports investigative work and document review. Through Everlaw for Good, Lena works closely with mission-driven organizations to understand their needs, guide them through the application process, and help determine whether Everlaw is the right fit for their investigations. Her work is rooted in supporting teams that pursue accountability and public-interest reporting, with a focus on being thoughtful, responsive, and accessible throughout the process. Lena is passionate about helping organizations level the playing field so they can do impactful work in their communities.

Letitia Walker is the News Director at WBRZ in Baton Rouge, LA.  She's previously worked in all of the other Louisiana markets along I-10, including New Orleans, Lafayette, and Lake Charles.  From an editor to a reporter, to a producer, she's worked in multiple roles in the newsroom, but has served as a News Director for the past 18 years.   Letitia has been back at WBRZ for the last two years, which is where she first started her career as a production assistant in college at LSU.

Levi Ismail is a multi-Emmy award-winning journalist with NewsChannel 5’s prestigious investigative unit and has built a reputation as one of the industry’s leading digital storytellers. Known for using short and long-form video to break down complex stories, Levi has become a trusted voice for audiences across Nashville and beyond.

His innovative approach to journalism earned him the inaugural award of Nashville’s Best TikTok Reporter and Best TikTok Page (Nashville News), while his work across television and digital platforms has helped strengthen NewsChannel 5’s connection with viewers. 

As an investigative reporter, Levi recently earned an Emmy award for exposing failures in Tennessee’s bond conditions for domestic violence cases. His reporting contributed to new laws that expanded the use of GPS monitoring as a bond requirement in more domestic violence cases.
