Born in Cairo, Egypt, to a family with roots in South Sudan, Levi immigrated to Minnesota.

Liam Adams is the religion reporter for The Tennessean - USA Today Network, where he covers a mix of national, state and local faith-related news. At The Tennessean, he routinely covers major Nashville-based denominations, including the Southern Baptist Convention, United Methodist Church and National Baptist Convention, USA. He's interested in the intersection of religion and state and federal politics on issues like immigration or education.
In 2025, he and photojournalist Andrew Nelles did a reporting project in Zimbabwe about the United Methodist Church, which was supported in partnership with the Pulitzer Center. More recently, he's covered the Christian media scene, history about Civil Rights leaders and how ICE activity has impacted local congregations.

Lisa Armstrong is an award-winning journalist with credits in The Guardian, The New Yorker, Rolling Stone, The Texas Tribune and other outlets. She is currently reporting on detention and deportations, and has also been reporting on incarceration for the past decade. She has written about people who were sentenced to life without parole as minors, solitary confinement and other issues. She also produced a documentary for CBS News about how subpar mental health care provided by for-profit companies led to in an increase in suicides in state prisons. 

Lisa has used her audio reporting to partner with musicians, poets and other artists for live performances at Carnegie Hall, SFJAZZ and other venues.

She is a professor at the UC Berkeley Journalism School and was previously an associate professor at the Craig Newmark Graduate School of Journalism, where she taught for 12 years. She is a trainer for the Global Center for Journalism and Trauma.

Liz Lucas is the Houston Harte Chair in Journalism at Mizzou, where she teaches courses in data journalism and emerging technologies. She previously worked as a data reporter and editor for various newsrooms and as a trainer for Investigative Reporters and Editors (IRE).

I've worked in broadcast television as a bilingual journalist for 3 decades. For the past 12 years, I've been doing my favorite job as a consumer investigative reporter at Telemundo 47 in New York. You can watch my stories here: telemundo47.com/responde. I'm a multiple Emmy award winner, but what is more rewarding is knowing that our team has recovered more than $5 million for our viewers.

Lomi Kriel is a statewide investigative reporter for The Texas Tribune. Previously, she was a founding member of the Tribune’s investigative unit with ProPublica, joining the initiative in 2020 before leaving for the Tribune in November 2025. Before joining ProPublica, Kriel reported on immigration for the Houston Chronicle, often focused on the Texas border. Months before Trump’s first administration announced its family separation policy, Kriel uncovered it in 2017. Kriel is a two-time Pulitzer finalist, in 2018 as part of the Houston Chronicle’s Hurricane Harvey coverage, and in 2024 for the Tribune’s reporting with ProPublica and FRONTLINE PBS on the Uvalde school shooting. She is a George Polk Award winner, National Magazine Award winner, Edward R. Murrow winner, and Emmy nominee, among other accolades. Kriel, who was born and raised in South Africa, immigrated to the United States in 1998. She has worked as a Central American correspondent for Thomson Reuters and a criminal justice reporter for the San Antonio Express-News, among other publications. Kriel is a graduate of the University of Texas at Austin and Columbia University and speaks Afrikaans and Spanish.

Lori Jane Gliha is the Senior National Investigative Correspondent for Scripps News. With more than 20 years of experience working at national, local, and non-profit news agencies, she has reported on a huge range of important issues including fentanyl poisonings among babies and young children, mass shootings and red flag laws, the use of ketamine, by paramedics, to sedate agitated people, drownings at vacation rental homes, and political violence. She and her team won a duPont-Columbia Award for their investigation into multiple missed warnings ahead of a mass murder in Maine.
speakers.tagLine: Senior National Investigative Correspondent, Scripps News

speakers.bio: Lulu Ramadan is an investigative reporter at The Seattle Times and co-curator of the newsletter Local Matters, a weekly compilation of the best investigations by local media across the county.

Lynn Walsh is the Assistant director or Trusting News and an Emmy award-winning journalist who has worked in investigative journalism at the national level and locally in California, Ohio, Texas and Florida. She is the former Ethics Chair for the Society of Professional Journalists and a past national president for the organization. Based in San Diego, Lynn is also an adjunct professor and freelance journalist.

Mackenzie Mays is an investigative journalist at Bloomberg Industry Group. Her 2025 project on women forced to give birth in jail is an IRE award finalist and was honored with the Carolyn C. Mattingly Award for Mental Health Reporting and the Al Nakkula Award for Police Reporting. She has worked at the Los Angeles Times, POLITICO, the Fresno Bee and her hometown newspaper, the Charleston Gazette-Mail.

Madi Alexander is the deputy editor of POLITICO’s Data and Graphics team, specializing in graphics-driven reporting on public policy, health care, education and elections.

With more than a decade of journalism experience, she analyzes complex policy issues and large datasets, transforming them into clear, compelling visual stories that help readers understand the real-world impact of state and federal policy decisions.

Prior to POLITICO, Madi was a data journalist at Bloomberg Government and The Dallas Morning News. Her work has been published by CNN, The New York Times, ProPublica, Reuters and more.

Originally from Oklahoma, Madi has a bachelor’s degree in political science from Oklahoma City University and a master’s degree in journalism from the University of Missouri.

Madisyn Parisi is a queer tech reporter from Maryland.  They're passionate about data journalism, feminist accountability reporting, and media criticism.  Their work has appeared in Assigned Media, Mothership, and others.

Maggie is an award-winning data journalist working with ABC Owned Television Stations and ABC News. In this role, she provides context and original analysis for daily news coverage, creates data visualizations, and builds interactive web applications like the ABC Price Tracker. Most recently, Maggie provided data analysis for the Edward R. Murrow Award-winning series Killer High at ABC30 in Fresno--an in depth look at the opioid epidemic in central California. Maggie is an alumna of Yale University and Columbia University Graduate School of Journalism. San Diego-born but Memphis-raised, Maggie currently lives in Chicago with her rescue dog, Simon.

Maggie Mulvihill is a veteran reporter, data journalism trainer, news entrepreneur, First Amendment advocate and attorney. Mulvihill is an associate professor of the practice in computational journalism at Boston University and a member of IRE’s Academic Task Force.
speakers.tagLine: Investigative Journalist ,Associate Professor of the Practice in Computational Journalism, Boston University

speakers.bio: Mago Torres is an investigative data journalist and editor with experience working at the intersection of research and data. She has worked on award-winning long-form investigations and developed programs that support collaborations, data-driven stories and investigative journalism in the U.S. and internationally. She is currently a trainer for North America for the Google News Initiative.

Mara Gassmann is the Legal Director at PEN America. She oversees the organization’s litigation docket and provides legal support to U.S. Free Expression program teams. She brings years of experience in First Amendment litigation and legal counseling in law firm and non-profit settings. Before joining PEN, she led the amicus curiae practice at the Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press, overseeing the development and filing of state and federal “friend-of-the-court” briefs on issues affecting the right to gather and report the news and publish commentary and opinion. 

Gassmann has taught media law as an adjunct professor at The George Washington University and at American University in Washington, D.C. She regularly speaks to U.S. and non-U.S.-based audiences on free expression topics and has co-authored congressional testimony on related matters. She serves on the board of directors of the National Press Club Journalism Institute.

Mara graduated magna cum laude from the Georgetown University Law Center. Before beginning her legal career, she was a spokesperson and later a freelance legal writer for CNN in its Washington, D.C., bureau.

Having been a news librarian, researcher, database editor, research editor and fact-checker for over four decades n New York and Washington DC, Margot Williams continues her lifelong passion for investigative journalism at The Intercept. Having worked for Time Inc, Poughkeepsie Journal, Washington Post, New York Times, NPR, and the International Consortium of Investigative Journalists (ICIJ), Williams has covered a wide range of investigations — both international and domestic — including the Guantánamo database on the Times website. She was a member of two Washington Post Pulitzer Prize-winning teams during her 14 years at the Washington Post.

Mariam is a research reporter at ProPublica, where she has worked on collaborative investigations on maternal health, immigration, criminal justice, and more. She works with public records and primary source information to advance investigative stories at the local and national level.

Marina Walker Guevara is a Pulitzer Prize-winning journalist who has led and transformed newsrooms and philanthropic organizations globally. 

Her work explores how investigative journalism and collaborative models interrogate power, shape public narratives and build trust. 

She is executive editor at the Pulitzer Center, a global non-profit organization that facilitates collaboration and innovation among journalists, technologists and civil society leaders on some of the most complex challenges facing society, including AI governance, environmental harms and the erosion of democracy. 

Her work leading cross-border collaborations dramatically changed how journalists approach their work. Through projects like the Panama Papers, she was instrumental in developing the model of large-scale media collaboration, persuading reporters who used to compete with one another instead to work together, share resources and amplify their reach and impact.

Mark Feldstein spent 20 years as an award-winning on-air investigative correspondent at CNN, ABC News and various local television stations.
     On assignment, he was beaten up, subpoenaed and sued; detained and censored by authorities in Egypt; and escorted out of Haiti under armed guard.
     His exposés led to resignations, firings, multimillion dollar fines and prison terms — and more than 50 journalism awards, including two George Foster Peabody medallions and the Columbia-DuPont baton.
     As a scholar, Feldstein has been published in numerous peer-reviewed journals. His book "Poisoning the Press" earned top academic awards for research and was selected by the Washington Post and other newspapers as a best book of the year.
     Feldstein is regularly quoted as a media analyst by leading outlets in the U.S. and abroad, and has testified as an expert witness on 1st Amendment issues before Congress and in court, including on behalf of WikiLeaks founder Julian Assange.
speakers.tagLine: Professor & Chair of Broadcast Journalism, University of Maryland

speakers.bio: Mark is a multiple-term board member and the elected Treasurer for  IRE. He is also the executive editor and a professor of practice at the Howard Center, ASU, where he teaches investigative students how to lead work that will attract major publication professional partners like NPR, NewsHour and CBS News.
speakers.tagLine: Howard Center for Investigative Journalism ASU

speakers.bio:
Mark Horvit is a professor at the University of Missouri School of Journalism, where he teaches investigative reporting and a project capstone course. He is also chair of the Journalism Professions faculty.
Horvit previously served as executive director of IRE and the NICAR, where he conducted training in investigative reporting and data journalism throughout the world. Horvit worked as a reporter and editor for 20 years before joining IRE.

Mark Lieberman is a reporter for Education Week covering K-12 school finance. He's spent the last year and a half tracking federal policy changes, documenting grant changes, and breaking stories about federal disruptions in school districts nationwide. 

Mark previously covered technology and online learning for Education Week and, before that, for Inside Higher Ed. He's also covered DC-area local news for the Current Newspapers and the Washington Post Express. His work has also appeared in Poynter, Vulture, Vox, IndieWire, The Week, USA Today, and Vice. He graduated in 2015 from the American University School of Communication with a bachelor’s degree in journalism.

Mark Scheffler is the co-founder and head of the visual investigations team at the New York Times. In his 9-plus years in that role he has overseen investigations into war crimes in Ukraine and Sudan, police violence throughout the U.S., and conflicts across the Middle East. His team's work draws heavily on visual and open-source evidence. Prior to the Times he worked at the Wall Street Journal. For years he was a producer/shooter/editor in the 'one-man-band' mode of video journalism. His written work has been published by The Times, the Chicago Tribune and Slate.com.
speakers.tagLine: Head of Visual Investigations | New York Times

speakers.bio: A Pulitzer Prize-winning reporter for The New York Times, who previously worked as the paper's Freedom of Information Act coordinator.

Marta Jewson is an independent journalist based in New Orleans. Marta has covered New Orleans schools for 15 years through the nation's largest education reform experiment and was instrumental in holding schools accountable to sunshine laws during the rapid expansion of charter schools in the city. She focuses on education, health and climate. She launched Lamplighter.news in 2026.

A two-time Pulitzer Prize winner, Martha Mendoza’s reports have prompted Congressional hearings and new legislation, Pentagon investigations and White House responses. She was part of a team whose investigations into slavery in the Thai seafood business led to the freedom of more than 2,000 men. And she was part of a team that uncovered a US Army massacre of hundreds of civilians during the Korean War.

Currently a FRONTLINE correspondent, Mendoza won a 2020 Emmy for “Kids Caught In The Crackdown,” about the detention of migrant children. A year later she won an Investigative Editors and Reporters award for “America’s Medical Supply Crisis,” a documentary holding those responsible for deadly shortages during the pandemic.

During her Associated Press career, Mendoza covered the war on drugs while based in Mexico City, and focused on human trafficking while working in Bangkok. She spent years covering tech inequality, booms and busts in the Silicon Valley.

Mendoza studied journalism and obtained a California teaching credential the University of California, Santa Cruz where she has continued to teach for more than a decade in the Master’s Science Communication program. She was a Knight Fellow at Stanford University and a Ferris Professor at Princeton University.

Martha Pskowski covers the oil and gas industry and the border region from her base in El Paso. She was previously an environmental reporter at the El Paso Times. She began her career as a freelance journalist in Mexico, reporting for outlets including The Guardian and Yale E360. Martha is a former Fulbright research fellow in Mexico.

Maryam Saleh is a senior editor at Type Investigations, where she works with local and regional newsrooms serving historically marginalized communities as part of Type’s Springboard Project. She previously held editing roles at The Intercept, Reveal from The Center for Investigative Reporting, and Rest of World. 

Maryam’s award-winning work as a reporter and editor spans immigration, criminal justice, politics, and global affairs. Her experience ranges from co-leading quick-turn politics coverage to editing narrative features and managing multiplatform investigative projects. She has worked extensively with freelance reporters from around the world.
Matt Apuzzo is the international investigations editor at the New York Times. A three-time Pulitzer winner, Matt leads a team of seven reporters.

Matt is the Vice President and News Director at CBS Stations in Los Angeles.  In this role, Matt oversees the production of seven straight hours of local news at CBS LA each weekday, including CBS LA Mornings (weekdays, 5:00am-12pm), as well as weekend morning newscasts. Matt also oversees CBS News California Investigates, a team of investigative reporters focused on accountability in the state.  

Matt has more than 25 years of experience in TV news, previously working as the head of LX News, Assistant News Director at KNBC in Los Angeles and at NBC Bay Area.  Prior to moving into management, Matt was an Investigative Producer in Los Angeles where he won numerous local and national awards for his work.  Matt served as an elected board member of Investigative Reporters & Editors (IRE) and was the Board President from 2016-18.  He started his career in TV news while attending Arizona State University and has also worked in Houston, Texas.

Matt's reporting has directly sparked new local, state and federal laws. His work, over more than 20 years, has been honored with 15 regional Emmy's, a National Edward R. Murrow Award, a National Headliners Award, a National Press Club Award and recently the Fourth Estate Award from the American Legion. He holds a M.S. in Journalism from Columbia University and is a proud IRE member since 2012. Matt works at KXAN-TV as an investigative reporter in Austin, Texas.

Matt Topic is a partner at Loevy + Loevy in Chicago, where he runs the firm's media, freedom of information, and intellectual property practices.  Matt and his team have litigated hundreds of state and federal open records cases, including release of video exposing the murder of Laquan McDonald by Chicago police, and war-crime photographs used in a Pulitzer-winning podcast.  Matt also represents news publishers in copyright cases against OpenAI and Microsoft challenging the unlicensed use of copyrighted news articles to train AI models, for which he was named to the Time Magazine 100 Most Influential People in AI in 2024.  Matt is the author of Long Live the Meat Humans: Why AI Has No Copyright Value, and is the co-host (with Jason Leopold) of the Bloomberg podcast Disclosure.
Matthew oversees investigations for CBS News. He has worked in broadcast television, print, audio and digital news as a reporter, producer, storyteller, editor and manager.

Mc Nelly Torres is an award-winning independent investigative journalist and former editor at the Center for Public Integrity where she led an award-winning team investigating inequality. She was part of a team that produced 40 Acres and a Lie, a groundbreaking investigation about the government’s failed promise of land to formerly enslaved people that received a Pulitzer Prize finalist citation for explanatory reporting and honors, including 2025 Alfred I. duPont-Columbia Award, the Investigative Reporters and Editors Awards, Global Investigative Journalism Network Sigma Award and a national Edward R. Murrow award. She previously worked as an investigative producer for NBC6 and as consumer watchdog reporter for the SunSentinel. Torres has earned over two dozen awards, including an Emmy, Edward R. Murrow Awards, a Gracie award, the SABEW, NAHJ, EWA and SPJ. Torres was the first Latina to be elected to the IRE board of directors. She was a recipient of the Gwen Ifill Award  in 2022.
speakers.tagLine: Independent Journalist, editor & forthcoming book author

speakers.bio: Meg Kelly is an investigative reporter with the Visual Forensics team at the Washington Post, which combines traditional and open source reporting. She has covered a half dozen conflicts including the war in Gaza, for which she shared in a Pulitzer Prize finalist and an Overseas Press Club award.

Megan Fan Munce is a reporter on the San Francisco Chronicle's climate team covering the way climate change is affecting housing in the state, from wildfires to sea level rise. In 2026, she won the Pulitzer Prize for Explanatory Reporting for a series investigating how insurance industry business practices left survivors of California's largest wildfires underpaid and unable to rebuild. The reporting also received top honors from IRE, SABEW and the Barlett and Steele awards. Prior to joining the San Francisco Chronicle, Munce covered retail for the Houston Chronicle.

Megha Rajagopalan is a reporter working on international investigations at The New York Times. She won a Pulitzer Prize in 2021 for a series on China's mass internment of Muslim minorities. She is a former foreign correspondent with professional stints in Beijing, Bangkok, Tel Aviv and London. Her work focuses on human rights, labor and conflict.

Mel Zahnd is a Supervising Attorney in the Housing Law Unit at Legal Aid DC. He represents low-income tenants in eviction and housing conditions cases before the D.C. Superior Court. He also litigates housing law claims before administrative bodies, such as the D.C. Housing Authority and the Office of Administrative Hearings.

Prior to joining Legal Aid DC in September 2018, Mel clerked for the Honorable Ronna Lee Beck and for the Honorable William M. Jackson of the D.C. Superior Court. He also worked as a fellow for the Jail and Prison Advocacy Project of Disability Rights D.C. at University Legal Services.

Meribah Knight is a reporter and producer based in Nashville where she has produced, hosted and reported a number of longform narrative podcasts including the Peabody-award winning podcast The Promise, Serial’s podcast, The Kids of Rutherford County, and National Public Radio’s Peabody-nominated podcast Supermajority.
 
Knight has received numerous national awards for her reporting, including the George Foster Peabody Award, the George Polk Award and the inaugural National Press Foundation’s 2024 Reporting on Women in Politics Award. Knight was also a finalist for the Pulitzer Prize, the Goldsmith Award, and twice a finalist for a National Magazine Award. She has produced four podcasts over her career, and each has been featured on The New Yorker’s top ten podcasts of the year—2018, 2020, 2023 and 2024.

Michael Biesecker is a global investigative reporter for The Associated Press. Biesecker’s work tracking potential war crimes in Ukraine was recognized with the 2022 IRE Gold Medal, as well as the top award for investigative reporting from the Overseas Press Club of America. He is a two-time Pulitzer finalist and was a contributor to the AP team that won the 2023 Pulitzer Prize for Public Service.
speakers.tagLine: Global Investigative Reporter, The Associated Press

speakers.bio:
Michael Corey is the Geospatial, Technical and Data Lead / Associate Director for Mapping Prejudice. Michael manage's Mapping Prejudice's relationships with local records custodians, manages the project's Deed Machine software, and researches the racialization of space in the U.S. Midwest.

Before transitioning to public history, Michael spent 20 years as a journalist and data journalist at the Star Tribune, Reveal from the Center for Investigative Reporting and the Des Moines Register.

Michael Nolan is an investigative journalist and researcher with MuckRock's Sunlight research desk. He previously worked as a data journalist with MinnPost and has written as a freelance contributor to STAT News and IEEE Spectrum. In a previous career he was a biomedical engineer and computational neuroscientist, but that doesn't come up much nowadays.

Michael is a data journalist with Project Salt Box, an all-volunteer nonprofit research team based in Baltimore covering the Department of Homeland Security's budget expansion under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. He built and maintains the ICE Warehouse Tracker, which combines land records, tax filings, commercial sales comparables, and first-hand accounts to follow the money behind migrant detention procurement — documenting how the agency acquires industrial properties and converts them into detention facilities.

In January, Project Salt Box was first to report one such purchase, a warehouse in Hagerstown, Maryland; the tracker has since established that the government paid just over $1 billion for eleven of these buildings.

Before turning to journalism, Michael spent more than two decades as a military intelligence analyst, with assignments at Fort Meade, Maryland; the Presidio of Monterey, California; and the Pentagon.

Michael Hudson is a Pulitzer-winning reporter and editor. He is currently head of US investigations at the Guardian. He previously worked as a senior editor at the International Consortium of Investigative Journalists, global investigations editor at the Associated Press and a reporter at the Wall Street Journal.
speakers.tagLine: Head of US Investigations, the Guardian

speakers.bio:
Mike Balsamo is the U.S. news director at The Associated Press, leading AP’s all-formats national news report across all 50 states and overseeing coverage of breaking news, criminal justice and accountability journalism. Previously, he served as AP’s national law enforcement editor, leading coverage of the Justice Department, federal executions and major law enforcement investigations.

Alongside a colleague, Balsamo led a landmark investigation into abuses and failures within the federal prison system that prompted the resignation of the Bureau of Prisons director and spurred significant legislative reforms. His reporting on prisons, executions and conditions in federal custody helped bring greater national attention to the federal prison system and broader accountability issues within the Justice Department.

He is a two-time winner of the White House Correspondents’ Association Award for Deadline Reporting and a finalist for the Goldsmith Prize for Investigative Reporting.
speakers.tagLine: US News Director, The Associated Press

speakers.bio: Mike Beaudet is a journalism professor at Northeastern University and an investigative reporter at WCVB-TV, Boston’s ABC affiliate. He’s worked in local television news for more than 30 years. Before joining WCVB-TV he was an investigative reporter and anchor at WFXT-TV in Boston. Beaudet has won numerous awards, including 21 New England Emmys and multiple Edward R. Murrow Awards. Beaudet’s research at Northeastern focuses on the future of local television news and finding new ways to grow the audience and engage younger viewers where they’re consuming content.

Mike Hixenbaugh is a senior investigative reporter for NBC News and the author of "They Came for the Schools: One Town's Fight Over Race and Identity, and the New War for America's Classrooms." His reporting examines the forces shaping American institutions, from public schools and child welfare systems to immigration enforcement and the military. Across print, broadcast, podcasts and documentaries, his work has exposed deadly failures in the U.S. military, the misuse of unclaimed bodies, abuses by religious leaders and threats to public education, prompting reforms and policy changes. His reporting has earned a George Polk Award, Peabody Award, duPont-Columbia Award and two Edward R. Murrow Awards, among many other honors. He was also a Pulitzer Prize finalist in audio reporting for the "Southlake" podcast, which he co-reported, wrote and hosted. Before joining NBC News, Hixenbaugh worked at newspapers in Ohio, North Carolina, Virginia and Texas.

Mike Morris is an award-winning investigative reporter for the Houston Chronicle who has spent nearly 15 years exploring the roots of Houston's complex problems and exposing public corruption and failing programs. He has revealed serious flaws in Texas tax incentive programs, twice helping to spur legislative action in response. He has been a finalist for several national journalism awards, including the Gerald Loeb Awards (in 2022, for coverage of a Texas tax break program), the Investigative Reporters & Editors awards (in 2020, for coverage of the COVID-19 pandemic) and the Livingston Award (in 2012, for coverage of mismanagement at the Harris County Housing Authority).

Mike has worked as an investigative reporter with The Associated Press and The Boston Globe Spotlight Team. At the Globe, he shared two Pulitzer Prizes, one for revealing the cover-up of child sex abuse in the Catholic Church, and one for covering the bombing of the Boston Marathon. Mike was running the marathon when the bombs exploded and worked into the night covering the tragedy. In addition, Mike was a Pulitzer finalist twice, once for an investigation of the debt collection industry, and again for an exposé of the mental health care system in Massachusetts. In 2015, Mike was played by Mark Ruffalo in the Academy Award-winning movie, "Spotlight." He is currently at work on a biography of the late newspaper columnist, Jimmy Breslin, to be published by Simon & Schuster in 2027.

37 years of front-line experience as a general assignment reporter in television prepared Mike Schuh for his current assignment: The Owner & Creative Director of MS StoryWorks.
Clients include: The Washington Post, CNN, PBS, The Discovery Channel, VELUX, Andersen Windows, CARFAX, Hard Rock Cafe and The Baltimore Symphony Orchestra.
His reporting in Louisville, Indianapolis, and Baltimore led to multiple Emmy Awards for stories that have brought him to Africa, Asia, Europe, the Middle-East, and South America. 
MS StoryWorks creates turn-key video stories, and offers a la carte pre-production, videography, production, lighting/gaff, video editing, writing, hosting, voice-tracking, podcast hosting, and segment writing for 	non-fiction web, local, and network television shows.  
For over two decades Mike has been on the Faculty of the National Press Photographers Association’s NewsVideo Workshop, the oldest television storytelling conference in the world. 

www.msstoryworks.com

Miranda Green is an investigative journalist based in Los Angeles who focuses on politics and climate change for national audiences.

She is a correspondent and author of Atmos Magazine’s weekly climate focused newsletter, The Understory.
speakers.tagLine: Investigative reporter, Correspondent at Atmos Magazine

speakers.bio: Mitch Ryals is the editor at The Banner in Prince George’s County. He previously worked at Washington City Paper, first as the Loose Lips reporter and then as managing editor. He began his career as a staff writer for the Pacific Northwest Inlander in Spokane, Washington, covering cops and courts. Ryals holds a master's degree in journalism from the University of Missouri School of Journalism.
speakers.tagLine: Editor of The Baltimore Banner, Prince George's County

speakers.bio: Mitchell Kotler is the co-founder and CTO of MuckRock, a non-profit organization dedicated to making government transparency accessible through technology. He leads the technical development of MuckRock's ecosystem of tools, including DocumentCloud and MuckRock Requests, which collectively serve thousands of journalists, researchers, and citizens seeking accountability through public information. Currently, Mitch is building AI-powered tools to help journalists navigate the complex landscape of public records requests, including a custom agent that uses retrieval-augmented generation over records retention schedules to surface what documents can be requested. His work focuses on removing technical barriers between people and the information they have a right to access.

Molly Blake is a social sciences librarian at the University of Minnesota-Twin Cities, where she serves as the liaison to the Economics, Educational Psychology, and Political Science departments and the Hubbard School of Journalism & Mass Communication. She has additional professional experience in teaching and writing. She is one of the co-founders of Save of Signs.

Mukta writes about law enforcement and the justice system in Mississippi as part of the fellowship. She holds a master’s degree from Columbia Journalism School and a law degree from the National Law School of India University, Bengaluru. Her prior work in India has led to high-level political debate, a parliamentary question, and recognition by the Global Investigative Journalism Network.
speakers.tagLine: Local Investigations Fellow, The New York Times

speakers.bio:
Nadia is a reporter and producer for Reveal, from the Center for Investigative Reporting. She has worked on a wide range of investigative stories touching on all sorts of issues.

Most notably, she co-reported and produced the historical investigation Forty Acres and a Lie, exploring a reparation that wasn’t — and the wealth gap that remains. The project was a finalist for the 2025 Pulitzer Prize, and the winner of an Edward R. Murrow Award, a duPont-Columbia Award, and a National Magazine Award. Nadia is also a 2026 Livingston Award finalist for her reporting on Gaza activism. 

Before she landed at Reveal, Nadia was a reporter, producer and host with the NPR station in Austin, Texas (where she also won some awards and stuff). Even more impressive, she once conducted an entire interview while riding a mule.

Nadia Sussman is an award-winning filmmaker and journalist at ProPublica. She directed the feature documentary “Before a Breath: America’s Stillbirth Crisis” which follows three mothers striving to make pregnancy safer after losing children to stillbirth. In 2026, the film received the Scripps Howard Award for Excellence in Visual Human-Interest Storytelling. She co-directed the documentary "Unprotected," which revealed the shocking secrets behind an American charity that claimed it was saving vulnerable girls from sexual exploitation. The film received an Edward R. Murrow Award in News Documentary. Her work to amplify the voices of sexual assault survivors in Alaska in the "Unheard" project was honored with a Dart Award for excellence in the coverage of trauma. In 2022, she contributed to an investigation revealing the dangerous air quality during Florida’s sugar cane harvest that was a finalist for a Pulitzer Prize.

Najib is a reporter and producer at Reveal. His work has earned him accolades like the George Polk Award, two Alfred E. duPont Awards, and an Edward R. Murrow Award. Before Reveal, he was Flipboard’s first news editor and has reported for Newsday and The Indianapolis Star. He’s a Stony Brook alum and a proud New York Knicks fan, and he's mainly working to feed his cat.

Nancy Youssef is a staff writer at The Atlantic. Before that, she was a longtime national security correspondent for The Wall Street Journal. 

She also worked at BuzzFeed News and The Daily Beast. She spent much of her career at McClatchy Newspapers where she served as National Security Correspondent and Middle East Bureau Chief based in Cairo covering the Middle East and the Islamic world.  Prior to that she was McClatchy's chief Pentagon correspondent, focusing on the wars in Iraq and Afghanistan. She traveled frequently to those two nations to see how the policies crafted in Washington reached Afghans, Iraqis and the troops alike. She is also founder of the Pentagon Press Association.

Before covering the Pentagon, she spent four years covering the Iraq war, including a stint as Baghdad bureau chief. Her pieces focused on the everyday Iraqi experience, civilian casualties and how the U.S.'s military strategy was reshaping Iraq's social and political dynamics.

She began her career at the Detroit Free Press, covering legal issues. While at the Free Press, she traveled throughout Jordan and Iraq for Knight Ridder, covering the Iraq war from the time leading up to it through the post-war period. She began her journalism career at the Baltimore Sun.

A Washington, D.C.-area native, she earned a bachelor's degree in Economics from University of Virginia and master’s degree in Security Studies from Georgetown University’s School of Foreign Service. Her parents are from Egypt, and she has been visiting the region all of her life. She currently lives in Washington D.C. and is an advocate for supporting neurodiverse children. She speaks Arabic.

I am a freelance investigative journalist and Alicia Patterson Fellow based in Washington, DC. I report on the
corporate and political forces undermining people’s health, the environment and sustainable food systems. 
My work has been published by Public Health Watch, Type Investigations, the Guardian, Nature, among others.
I like telling people's stories and getting at the truth. I am a good listener, a keen observer, and a bit of a cynic.

Nate Rosenfield is an investigative reporter and the co-founder of The Bell Project, a nonprofit newsroom covering torture in the American criminal justice system. His work has been featured in The New York Times, The Guardian, Mississippi Today, The Tampa Bay Times and other publications. He has been a finalist for a Pulitzer Prize and two Goldsmith Awards among other honors. He graduated from Columbia Journalism School.

Niala Boodhoo is an award-winning Washington, DC-based journalist, podcast and events host. You can hear her many Fridays on NPR stations hosting the Friday News Roundup on 1A, and she also currently works for Religion News Service as its Events Host/EP. This summer, she’ll be launching Oh God Now What,  a weekly podcast and Substack, where she talks to religious and spiritual leaders about how to live with the news. It is distributed by PRX, one of the world’s top podcast publishers.
speakers.tagLine: Award-winning Washington, DC-based journalist, podcast and events host

speakers.bio:
Nicholas Whitaker is an executive capacity coach, mindfulness in nature facilitator, leadership guide and writer who works at the intersection of culture, well-being, and technological change. After more than a decade working inside Google and the tech industry on education, learning, and culture initiatives, Nicholas now focuses on how individuals and organizations navigate disruption, and the hidden costs of always-on work culture. 

He is the founder of the Rebellion Collective, where he helps individuals and teams reclaim the capacity that modern work systems extract from them. His framework, the C.H.E.A.T. Code™, makes visible how our habits shape the energy, attention, and time we have left to give... and what happens when any one of those hits zero. He has co-authored three Amazon bestselling books and writes on Substack, where his newsletter has ranked in the top 100 in Education.
speakers.tagLine: Chief Rebellion Officer for Rebellion Collective

speakers.bio:
I cover oil and gas in Oklahoma for ProPublica and The Frontier, as part of ProPublica’s Local Reporting Network. 

From 2019 to 2025, I was a contributing writer and editor for High Country News, based in a small town on Colorado’s Western Slope. I often wrote about oil, gas and mining industries; public lands; and economics and labor in the Mountain West

My freelance work has appeared in Harper’s, The Guardian, ProPublica, The Drift, The Nation, Vox, Politico Magazine, Civil Eats, Montana Free Press and Outside Magazine, among others. My story about the battery metals mining boom for The Drift was selected for the 2024 The Best American Science and Nature Writing anthology. That story was also a finalist for a 2024 Livingston Award and was named one of the best stories of the year by Longreads. An ongoing ProPublica project that investigates oil and gas decommissioning costs was named a 2025 Livingston Award finalist.
speakers.tagLine: Reporter with The Frontier and ProPublica

speakers.bio: Nicole Dungca is a reporter in The Washington Post's investigative unit. She has been part of reporting teams that have been recognized in the Audio Reporting, Illustrated Reporting and Commentary and Local Reporting categories of The Pulitzer Prizes. Before The Post, she was part of the Boston Globe's Spotlight Team, covered education at the Oregonian, and has written for The Times-Picayune and The Providence Journal. She is currently the president of the Asian American Journalists Association.

Nicole Foy covers immigration at ProPublica, with a particular focus on how immigration enforcement changes communities around the country. Before joining ProPublica in 2024, she was an enterprise and investigative reporter across the West. She previously worked for CalMatters, the Austin American-Statesman, the Idaho Statesman, the Idaho Press and the Orange County Register.

Nicole Vap is a duPont-Columbia and IRE Award-winning investigative journalist with decades of experience in local and network television. Driven by a commitment to accountability journalism and the sustainability of local news, she has built a career connecting communities with the reporting that matters most.
Most recently, Nicole served as Executive Producer of Content Development for CBS News and Stations. She now consults and advises, including with the Colorado News Collaborative (COLab) on its MATCH Lab program — pairing resource-challenged newsrooms with academics, business mentors, and students to solve real-world journalism problems.
A respected voice in press freedom and journalism ethics, Nicole served three terms on the board of IRE (Investigative Reporters and Editors) and currently serves as Vice President of the Colorado Freedom of Information Coalition, championing transparency and public access to government information.

Senator Nikil Saval is a father, husband, writer, and organizer representing the First Senatorial District in the Pennsylvania legislature. Serving as Democratic Chair of the Senate’s Urban Affairs & Housing Committee and Chair of the Senate’s Philadelphia Delegation, Saval’s legislative work is focused on critical response to Pennsylvania’s ongoing housing, mass incarceration, wage, and climate crises, while simultaneously pushing for deep structural change. His office led the creation of the Whole-Home Repairs Program, which establishes a one-stop shop for home repairs and weatherization. 

As a writer, Saval has written extensively for The New York Times and The New Yorker and served as co-editor of literary journal n+1 (he remains on its board). He authored Cubed: A Secret History of the Workplace and Rage in Harlem: June Jordan and Architecture. 

He is fighting for a Philadelphia and Pennsylvania that work for everyone.
Nina Zweig is the research editor at Type Investigations, where she also runs the Inside/Out Journalism Project for incarcerated reporters.

Noam N. Levey, senior correspondent, joined KFF Health News in 2021 after 17 years at the Los Angeles Times, the last 12 as the paper’s national health care reporter based in Washington, D.C. Noam has reported on health care issues from more than three dozen states and four continents and won numerous honors, including two prestigious NIHCM awards, one for the 2019 series “Inside America’s High-Deductible Revolution,” and one for the 2022 series “Diagnosis: Debt.” He has also been published in Health Affairs, JAMA, and The Milbank Quarterly. Noam started his career at newspapers in Duluth, Minnesota; Montgomery, Alabama; and the United Arab Emirates. Before joining the L.A. Times, he was an investigative reporter for the San Jose Mercury News. Noam has a degree in history and Near Eastern studies from Princeton University.

Olivia Reed is a May 2026 graduate from the University of Nebraska-Lincoln, where she studied broadcasting on the news track and history. She spent her college years gaining experience in video, audio, print, data, photo and multimedia journalism, with a focus on investigative journalism. Olivia won four awards during her three years at UNL: two first-place Eric Sevareid awards, one Mark of Excellence award from the Society of Professional Journalists and one Award of Excellence from the Broadcast Education Association. She was the news intern for KOLN (Lincoln, NE) in the summer of 2024 and the news/investigative intern for CBS Sacramento/CBS California Investigates in the summer of 2025. Her stories range from unsolved cases involving Missing and Murdered Indigenous women in rural America, to youth incarceration and mentorship.  Olivia is an early-career journalist looking for her first market.

Owen Hornstein is an award winning journalist with more than 15 years of experience in media production, cinematography, visual effects, animation, and post-production. As Director of Photography and VFX at InvestigateTV, he helps shape the visual identity of national investigative stories through documentary-style production, computer-generated animations, and innovative digital storytelling. His work focuses on creating compelling, emotional narratives that inform audiences while pushing the boundaries of visual journalism.

Paige Pfleger is a senior criminal justice reporter at WPLN News, Nashville Public Radio, and a fellow with ProPublica's Local Reporting Network. She has reported investigative series on gun violence and juvenile justice, and her investigation into domestic violence and firearms dispossession won an Edward R. Murrow Award and was a finalist for the Livingston Award and an Investigative Reporters and Editors Award. Her story about a rural domestic violence court helped close a dangerous loophole in courtrooms across Tennessee. Paige is also the southern chapter leader for the Association of Gun Violence Reporters.
speakers.tagLine: Senior Criminal Justice Reporter, WPLN News

speakers.bio:
Paris Lewbel serves as the Public Relations Manager for Flock Safety, where he leads media strategy across the country, highlights customer success stories, and builds strong relationships with reporters and public sector partners across the country.

Previously, he was Deputy Director of Communications for the Metropolitan Police Department of Washington, D.C., one of the nation’s largest police departments. In that role, he oversaw media relations, public affairs, and digital media, and directed crisis communications during major incidents, including serving as Unified Command PIO for the 2025 midair collision between a Blackhawk helicopter and an American Airlines jet. Earlier in his career, Paris was the PIO for the Aurora (IL) Police Department, where he co-led the communications response to the 2022 Highland Park Parade Shooting.
speakers.tagLine: Former Deputy Director of Communications - DC Metropolitan Police, Public Relations Manager - Flock Safety

speakers.bio: Paroma Soni is a data and graphics reporter at POLITICO covering trade, agriculture, politics and immigration. She produces data-driven visual analyses for POLITICO Pro on topics such as the Trump administration’s tariffs, federal funding, visa policy and shifts in domestic manufacturing. Previously, she was an associate visual journalist at FiveThirtyEight covering elections and politics, and a Delacorte Fellow at Columbia Journalism Review. She is based in New York.

Paul Cobler is the Tribune's economy and industry reporter, covering the socioeconomic and political forces that impact Texans’ pocketbooks and upward mobility. Before joining the Tribune, he was a politics reporter for the Houston Landing and covered Baton Rouge City Hall for The Advocate in Louisiana. Paul grew up in Victoria, Texas, and graduated from the University of Texas at Austin. His reporting has appeared in news publications across the state, including the Dallas Morning News, Austin American-Statesman and the Houston Chronicle. He is based in Austin.
speakers.tagLine: Economy and Industry reporter at The Texas Tribune

speakers.bio:
Paul Haven is Vice President and head of Global Newsgathering for The Associated Press, based in New York. He is in charge of about 1,200 reporters, photographers and video journalists across the globe. In this position, Haven sets the daily news agenda for the cooperative and is responsible for its newsgathering budget. Under his tenure, the AP has won four Pulitzer Prizes, an Oscar for its collaboration with Frontline on the documentary “20 Days in Mariupol,” and numerous other awards.
 
Haven has spent most of his 30-year career at AP based internationally, serving at various times as bureau chief for Afghanistan and Pakistan, Spain, and Cuba before becoming news director for Latin America. He’s covered the wars in Afghanistan and Iraq, the devastating 2010 earthquake in Haiti, the 9/11 attacks, multiple Olympics and many other stories of historical importance. 
 
A New York City native and graduate of Cornell University, Haven has also written two novels for children.

I’m a reporter with experience covering transportation, new American communities, housing, the environment and more — both domestically and internationally.
Before joining Newsday as a news transportation reporter on Long Island, I covered immigration for The Columbus Dispatch in Ohio. Before that, I was based in Kathmandu and covered Nepali politics, the environment, human rights issues, and more for the Nepali and international press.
I speak fluent Nepali and English and intermediate Spanish and Wolof.

Peyton Barish is the assistant project manager for the Knight-Newhouse College Athletics Database and a former Division I athlete at NC State University. He has a background in college athletics policy with a particular interest in labor policy and innovative sport solutions.

Phillip Martin, former senior investigative reporter for GBH News, was inducted into the Massachusetts Broadcast Hall of Fame in February 2024.
He has been awarded numerous fellowships and grants over his career for in-depth and thought-provoking reports — particularly when it comes to inequities and social justice at a hyperlocal and global scale. Awards include the 2023 Online Journalism Award for social justice reporting on labor trafficking, a 2022 National Edward R. Murrow Award for the multi-part GBH News series “Unseen,” and National Edward R. Murrow awards for investigative reporting in 2019 and 2014.
Martin was honored with national Sigma Delta Chi Awards for radio feature reporting in 2023 and investigative reporting in 2017.
He was a Shorenstein and Nieman Fellow at Harvard University, a Pulitzer Center grantee, an International Center for Journalist grantee, and a Japan-US Fellow. He was NPR'S first Race Relations correspondent (1998-2001) and a former NPR senior editor.

Rachel Jones is the Director of Journalism Initiatives with the National Press Foundation in Washington, DC, She spent two decades as a journalist with outlets including the St. Petersburg Times, the Detroit Free Press, and National Public Radio before heading to East Africa as a journalism trainer and consultant.

In 2007, Jones began an eight-month assignment in Northern Uganda as a journalism adviser for radio reporters covering the aftermath of civil war. In 2008, she moved to Nairobi, Kenya as a journalism trainer and mentor for the International Center for Journalists. When that program ended in 2011, Jones remained in Nairobi for five more years as a freelance communications consultant and media trainer for organizations including Voice of America.

Since returning to the U.S. in 2016, Jones has freelanced for National Geographic and served as a writing mentor for The Aspen Institute’s Healthy Communities fellows. In 2021, Jones began her tenure at National Press Foundation, where she directs the Widening the Pipeline Fellowship program for early career journalists of color, the Covering Rare Diseases Fellowship for international journalists, and the upcoming U.S. Healthcare System fellowship.
speakers.tagLine: Director of Journalism Initiatives, National Press Foundation

speakers.bio:
Raphael Romero Ruiz is a graduate student and journalist from Tucson, Arizona. He recently covered the border beat for The Arizona Republic, reporting on the 2024 presidential election, the first 100 days and beyond of President Donald Trump’s second administration, and how U.S.-Mexico border communities have been impacted by American immigration policy.

Prior to that, Ruiz began his journalism career shortly before graduating from Arizona State University’s Walter Cronkite School of Journalism and Mass Communication, earning a B.A. in Journalism with minors in Transborder Chicana/o and Latina/o Studies and Digital Audiences.

For nearly five years, he focused his reporting on working-class Latine communities in metro Phoenix, spotlighting community activists, artists and everyday people shaping local culture. His journalism emphasizes the power of storytelling to uplift underrepresented communities like his own — the Rose neighborhood on Tucson’s southside.

He is excited to study data journalism to strengthen his reporting on immigration at a time when harmful narratives and disinformation heavily influence federal immigration policies. He aims to make complex and obscure data accessible to communities like his own, especially people like his parents — proud Mexican immigrants.
speakers.tagLine: Howard Fellow, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism

speakers.bio:
Rebecca Woolington is a senior enterprise editor at NBC News Digital. She previously worked in investigations at The Washington Post, the Tampa Bay Times and The Oregonian.

At the Times, her work chronicled the rise and fall of a troubled small-town mayor, discrepancies in the state’s counting of coronavirus deaths and dangerous working conditions inside a Tampa lead factory. The latter earned her and her reporting partners the 2022 Pulitzer Prize in investigative reporting, a George Polk Award, an Investigative Reporters & Editors medal and several other national honors.

As investigative editor at the Times, she led award-winning projects that examined Florida’s kratom industry and the state’s ailing waterways. 

Rebecca grew up in Portland and attended Portland Community College before graduating from the University of Oregon’s School of Journalism and Communication. She lives in Washington, D.C. with her two cats, Caroline and Claudette.
speakers.tagLine: Senior Enterprise Editor, NBC News Digital

speakers.bio:
Rhaina Cohen is an award-winning producer and editor for NPR's Embedded podcast, where she's worked on acclaimed series about a wide range of issues, from abortion access to the aftermath of gun violence. She previously produced and edited the podcasts Hidden Brain, Invisibilia, Rough Translation, and Louder Than A Riot. 

Rhaina is also the bestselling author of "The Other Significant Others: Reimagining Life with Friendship at the Center." Her writing about social connection has been supported by the National Endowment for the Humanities and published by The Atlantic, The New York Times, The Washington Post and is forthcoming in the anthology "Living, Together."

Ricardo Sandoval-Palos is the Public Editor of the Public Broadcasting Service -- the audience representative before PBS and its community of producers and journalists. He is an award-winning investigative reporter and multimedia editor who helped shape reporting teams of nonprofit newsrooms and recently co-founded palabra, the digital magazine of the National Association of Hispanic Journalists.

Ricardo is a former supervising editor of NPR’s Morning Edition broadcast, an editor with the International Consortium of Investigative Journalists and The Sacramento Bee, and a Latin America correspondent for the Dallas Morning News and the San Jose Mercury News. Before that, he was an investigative business reporter in California and co-author of the award-winning biography, "The Fight in the Fields: Cesar Chavez and the Farmworkers Movement."

Rick Yarborough is Senior Investigative Platform Manager at NBC Washington where he launched the NEWS4 I-Team in 2011.  His work has been recognized with multiple national awards, including a Peabody,  duPont-Columbia University, Gerald Loeb Award, Scripps Howard Foundation, National Headliners, National Press Club, National Gracie Award, Fourth Estate Award, NLGJA Excellence in Journalism Award, NABJ Award for Investigative, along with many regional Emmy and Murrow Awards.  Rick was recently inducted into the Capital Emmy Silver Circle for his 25 years of service to the industry. He also launched the OUT DC Employee Resource Group for LGBTQ+ and Ally employees in 2016 for NBC4 & Telemundo44.

Rob Antle is senior producer with the Canadian Broadcasting Corporation's Atlantic investigative unit, working with journalists in four provinces. Over a career spanning three decades, he's had a number of roles, from reporter to editor to producer, focusing strongly on enterprise and investigative storytelling.

Recent stories led to a review of how Newfoundland and Labrador’s child protection system handles reports of sexual violence, and the rollback of a secret severance benefit for retiring provincial cabinet ministers.
speakers.tagLine: CBC News, senior producer, Atlantic investigative unit

speakers.bio: Benincasa has been reporting data-driven investigations for NPR since 2008. His recent work includes a look at how credit scores drive auto insurance premiums, including ZIP Code-level variations in this effect around the country. He has also conducted numerous award-winning investigations on consumer issues and occupational safety and health. Benincasa taught data journalism for Georgetown University's master's program from 2008 to 2016.

Robert Cribb is founder and director of the Investigative Journalism Bureau. He has received national and international reporting awards for investigations into offshore tax evasion, child exploitation, human trafficking, dangerous doctors, environmental threats and public safety. He was part of the international reporting team that produced the Pulitzer Prize-winning Panama Papers investigation in 2017. Cribb is past president of the Canadian Association of Journalists, the first international board member of Investigative Reporters and Editors and co-author of Digging Deeper: A Canadian Reporter’s Research Guide (Oxford University Press). He teaches investigative reporting at the University of Toronto.

Robert is the vice president for research and data at Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington (CREW), where he leads efforts to investigate dark money and corruption. Prior to joining CREW, he founded the dark money tracking program at OpenSecrets, where he won SPJ’s Sigma Delta Chi award for his reporting. He has collaborated with and appeared in major outlets nationwide like NPR, The Washington Post, the New York Times and CNN.
speakers.tagLine: Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington (CREW)

speakers.bio: Rose Ciotta is the executive director of Investigative Editing Corps, a project she founded in 2017 to help local news organizations do investigative reporting by linking them to experienced investigative editors. Rose is a Pulitzer Prize-winning investigative editor. She co-edited "Assault on Learning," an investigation into violence in Philadelphia schools that won The Philadelphia Inquirer the 2012 Public Service Gold Medal. Most recently, Rose was  a senior investigations editor at The Atlanta Journal-Constitution. She was also an award-winning investigations editor at EdSource, an education-focused news organization based in California, and an investigative producer for the CBS affiliate, WIVB-TV in Buffalo, N.Y. where she was an award-winning investigative reporter and editor for The Buffalo News. She attended Stanford University as a JSK Fellow and served as an IRE board member.
Rose White is an investigative journalist for MLive.com, a Michigan newspaper group, covering immigration, housing, labor and the economy.

Rowan Philp is GIJN’s global reporter and impact editor. He was formerly chief reporter for South Africa’s Sunday Times. As a foreign correspondent, he has reported on news, politics, corruption, and conflict from more than two dozen countries around the world.

Roxanna Asgarian is a Texas-based journalist and the author of We Were Once a Family: A Story of Love, Death, and Child Removal in America, which won the 2023 National Book Critics Circle Award for Nonfiction, the Andrew Carnegie Medal for Excellence in Nonfiction, and the L.A. Times Book Prize. Her work has appeared in The New York Times, The Washington Post, New York Magazine, Texas Monthly, and more.

Sam Kessler is a reporter for The Wall Street Journal based in New York City. He focuses on the intersection of power, business and technology, using data analysis and programming alongside traditional reporting to uncover investigative stories.

Previously, Sam covered the crypto industry as deputy managing editor at CoinDesk, where he was part of the team that won a Gerald Loeb Award for its coverage of Sam Bankman-Fried and FTX. He has also earned a SABEW award for his work covering AI at the Journal. 

Sam holds a bachelor's degree in computer science from Harvard University, where he led the Harvard Political Review.

Hi! My name is Sam. I'm a proud Minnesotan covering the 2026 midterm elections in Orlando, Florida this year. Before this, I covered the 89th Texas Legislature in Austin, Texas and reported on local government, schools, state agencies, campaign finance and more for four years in Ketchikan, Alaska. I also love bringing coding and data visualization into the newsroom and helping share my enthusiasm with the rest of the world!
speakers.tagLine: Elections & Investigations Reporter, Central Florida Public Media

speakers.bio: Samantha Storey is the managing editor for The Trace where she oversees news and data. Prior to The Trace, she worked as an executive producer and podcast host at Bloomberg, where she won a Gerald Loeb Award in audio for “In Trust” and received a Webby award nomination for “Bedrock, USA.” She was a senior enterprise editor at HuffPost, leading a team of reporters who covered social inequality, and produced investigations on hate crimes, domestic violence, and school policing. She spent more than half of her career at The New York Times as an assignment editor across metro, national and sports, and she served as an audio reporter on “An Imam In America,” which won a Pulitzer Prize for feature writing. Samantha’s first job in journalism was at the Seattle Weekly; the first editor hired to put the paper on the Internet. Samantha holds a B.A. in English and Philosophy from Boston College.

Sammy Sussman is an investigative reporter based in New York City. In 2025, Sammy reported about police misconduct throughout New York State as part of The New York Times' Local Investigations Fellowship in partnership with New York Focus. Over the past few years, Sammy has also written about book bans in New York State prisons, mass detentions in the West Bank as evidenced in press statements on a little-known Israeli military webpage and allegations of sexual misconduct in the New York Philharmonic.

Before Sara DiNatale joined the staff of the San Francisco Chronicle, she was an energy reporter at the San Antonio Express-News, where she exposed the unregulated underbelly of rooftop solar industry, its poorly trained installers and smarmy salespeople, who made careers from victimizing homeowners. Her reporting prompted new state laws in Texas and was recognized with a 2024 George Polk Award. 

After a decade reporting across the southern U.S., she joined the Chronicle in 2025 to cover Trump policy impacts in California. She was a recipient of the 2026 Pulitzer Prize for Explanatory Reporting for her role in the Chronicle's “Burned” series, which showed how California’s largest insurers shortchange wildfire survivors, threatening the recovery and health of whole communities. 

She spent several years covering business, retail, the economy and labor at the Tampa Bay Times and Mississippi Today. She’s a graduate of the University at Buffalo and a native of Western New York.
speakers.tagLine: the San Francisco Chronicle, Policy and politics reporter

speakers.bio: Sarah Blustain is an assistant managing editor with ProPublica. In this role, she helps oversee ProPublica’s Local Reporting Network, its partnership program with local newsrooms. She came to ProPublica from Type Investigations, where for seven years she spearheaded longform investigative projects with independent journalists. All those stories were published in partnership with publications around the country. Blustain previously served as senior editor for Newsweek/Daily Beast and The New Republic among other publications.

Sarah Cohen is a Pulitzer Prize-winning reporter and editor currently working with The New York Times' local investigations fellowship through Big Local News. She spent most of her career as a data editor and investigative data reporter for The Washington Post and The New York Times. She has served as president of Investigative Reporters and Editors, Knight Chair in Journalism at both Duke University and Arizona State University and a board member of the Fund For Investigative Journalism.
speakers.tagLine: Sr Data Reporter, Big Local News

speakers.bio: Sarah Glover is the Vice President of News & Civic Dialogue at WHYY in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. She has worked in local news for three decades and is the former managing editor at MPR News, Minnesota Public Radio. She also previously worked as a social media manager at NBC Owned Television Stations and NBC10 Philadelphia. She started her career as a staff photojournalist at The Philadelphia Inquirer and Philadelphia Daily News. Over the course of her career as a photojournalist, editor and executive producer, she has championed telling community-focused, untold stories representing the tapestry of people where she has lived and worked.
speakers.tagLine: Vice President of News & Civic Dialogue, WHYY

speakers.bio:
As the senior investigative producer for CNBC, Scott set up the current investigative unit which produces long and short form pieces for broadcast and online. His team teams up with correspondents and field producers depending on the subject area. 

Scott joined CNBC in June 2017 after spending nine years at CNN also as a senior investigative producer. During his career, he has won more than 100 journalism awards, including four first place National Headliner Awards, eight second place and two third place, two Investigative Reporters and Editors (IRE) awards while at NBC 6 Miami and CNN, five national Emmy nominations, and 23 regional Emmys and 44 Emmy nominations while he was an investigative producer in south Florida at NBC 6 and WPLG-TV.

In 2026, he won two first place awards from SABEW and his latest first place National Headliner Award.

His stories range from financial fraud to illegal business related to health care. He began his career as a reporter at the Las Vegas Sun.

Scotty Smith is an award-winning National Investigative Photojournalist for InvestigateTV in Washington D.C. Originally from Alaska, he has over 20 years of experience in newsrooms across the country covering some of the biggest events of the past two decades.

Seamus Hughes has a career that spans journalism, academia, and government.  Seamus Hughes is a senior research faculty member at University of Nebraska Omaha’s National Counterterrorism Innovation, Technology, and Education Center (NCITE). In addition to his academic pursuits, he is the editor of Court Watch, an online news site that covers the federal court system that has broken hundreds of national stories. Hughes also works as a research journalist at the New York Times and Bloomberg Law News. In 2022, Hughes was part of a New York Times team that won a Pulitzer Prize for National Reporting for stories on law enforcement in America. In 2026, he was a finalist for the Goldsmith Award for Investigative Reporting and was awarded a Pulitzer Prize for Investigative Reporting. Washingtonian Magazine has repeatedly named him one of the country’s most influential voices on national security.

Sean Mussenden is Director and Executive Editor of the Howard Center for Investigative Journalism at the University of Maryland Philip Merrill College of Journalism. The Howard Center for Investigative Journalism is an investigative reporting unit and journalism training program based at the University of Maryland Philip Merrill College of Journalism that partners with leading news organizations like the Associated Press, NPR and Frontline. It specializes in producing deeply reported investigations that require complex data analysis and the application of computational methods using artificial intelligence. The center is funded by the Scripps Howard Foundation. Mussenden is also a principal lecturer in data and computational journalism at Merrill College and a doctoral candidate at the University of Maryland College of Information, studying the design and development of AI-powered reporting tools.
speakers.tagLine: Director and Executive Editor, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism, University of Maryland

speakers.bio: Sef Kloninger recently left Google to work on public service projects. He is interested in how to use AI to bend the cost curve for those working in the civic interest. He's a volunteer engineer on Big Local News, based at Stanford University, which helps newsrooms do accountability journalism without need for data specialists or extensive programming knowledge -- generating leads, not writing stories. He's also built tools for the Data Rescue Project.
speakers.tagLine: Engineer, Big Local News, Stanford University

speakers.bio:
Shernay Williams is a 3x entrepreneur and an award-winning independent journalist with 15 years of cross-platform experience. She began her journalism career at a newspaper before transitioning to local TV as a general assignment reporter, anchor and producer. Among her many ventures, she's run a content strategy firm, group coaching program for new entrepreneurs, and a national directory and resources platform for Black mom entrepreneurs. As an educator, she's partnered with Poynter, the Institute for Independent Journalists, and Loyola University. She chairs the National Association of Black Journalists' Entrepreneurship Task Force.

Her areas of expertise as an entrepreneurship coach: identifying multiple revenue streams, freelancing, marketing, time management, solopreneur life, small business planning and client management.

Sona Patel is the Program and Editorial Director of the Local Investigations Fellowship at The New York Times, a year-long program aimed at developing the next generation of local investigative reporters. She started her career as a beat reporter for The (San Luis Obispo) Tribune then pivoted to working in audience development in newsrooms. She was the first Social Media editor for The Seattle Times and was part of the team that won the Pulitzer Prize for Breaking News Reporting in 2010. She joined The New York Times in 2012 to develop audience strategies for the newsroom and oversaw the newsroom’s efforts to connect with readers via crowdsourcing and reader comments.
speakers.tagLine: Program and Editorial Director, Local Investigations Fellowship, The New York Times

speakers.bio: Sonner Kehrt is an investigative reporter at The War Horse, where she often covers the intersection of the military and democracy. Her work has also been featured in The New York Times, WIRED magazine, Mother Jones, and other publications. She studied government at the U.S. Coast Guard Academy and served for five years as a Coast Guard officer before earning a masters degree in democracy and governance from Georgetown University and a masters of journalism from UC Berkeley.

Soorin Kim is a White House producer with MS NOW. Before joining MS NOW, she was at ABC News, covering President Donald Trump as a campaign embed reporter during the 2024 election and covering the influence of money in politics for the Investigative Unit.
speakers.tagLine: White House producer at MS NOW

speakers.bio: Spencer Woodman is a reporter for the International Consortium of Investigative Journalists. His work has appeared in The New York Times, The Washington Post, NBC News, Associated Press, The Guardian, Rolling Stone and The Intercept.
speakers.tagLine: The International Consortium of Investigative Journalists

speakers.bio:
Stacey Cameron is an Investigative Reporter at WSMV4.

In a career spanning two decades, Stacey has been honored with the Sigma Delta Chi Bronze Medallion, seven Regional Edward R. Murrow awards, six Regional EMMY awards, and three Associated Press Reporter of the Year awards. 

Before joining WSMV4, Stacey was the Chief Investigative Reporter for KSLA News 12 in Shreveport, LA. He also covered the news and conducted investigations in San Antonio, TX; Kansas City, Mo.; Raleigh, N.C.; Oklahoma City, OK; and Charleston, WV.

Prior to his work in journalism, Stacey practiced law in Cincinnati, St. Louis, and New York City, litigating constitutional, trademark, and copyright matters before federal and state bars.

Stacey holds a Bachelor of Arts from Indiana University, a Juris Doctor from Syracuse University, and a Master of Laws in intellectual property from the Benjamin N. Cardozo School of Law in New York City. Stacey also studied broadcast journalism at Ohio University.

Starlight Williams is a health, wellness, and night sky editor at National Geographic Digital, where she commissions science-driven stories on everything from brain chemistry and aging to exercise physiology and epic cosmic events.

Before her current role, Starlight was a National Geographic Travel editor, a staff reporter covering education, business, and city government for Community Impact Newspaper in Texas, a columnist for New Orleans Weddings Magazine, and a culture and festivals contributor for The Times-Picayune and NOLA.com.

When she’s not hunting for great pitches (send yours to starlight.williams@natgeo.com), you can find her training herself and others to meet their full physical potential.

Stephen Stock is Assistant News Director and Investigative Journalist at WBRZ Baton Rouge. He specializes in public corruption, safety and transportation. Stock was a founding member of CBS News and Stations Innovation Lab and helped build investigative teams in San Francisco, Miami and Orlando. He teaches at IRE, newsrooms and universities around the US. He’s won a Peabody, a du-Pont, a national SPJ, 5 Murrows, 6 AP awards and 23 Emmys.

Steve Berkowitz worked for USA Today for 25 years, first as the sports projects editor and then as a reporter with the department's enterprise and investigations group. Most of his efforts over the past decade-plus centered around helping to construct, maintain and analyze annually updated databases of college athletics compensation and finances. He also covered various NCAA, federal and state legal and legislative issues, including Title IX. He began his career at The Washington Post, he worked as a news aide, copy editor, reporter, night editor and assistant sports editor.

Steve Eder is an investigative reporter for The New York Times. He recently reported on policing in America, sharing in the 2022 Pulitzer Prize for National Reporting for an investigative series on deadly traffic stops. He also was part of the team of Times journalists honored with the the 2018 Pulitzer Prize for Public Service for reporting on workplace sexual harassment issues. At The Times, he has also served as an investigative sports reporter, covered the 2016 presidential campaign, and written extensively about the presidency of Donald J. Trump, among other subjects. Before joining The Times in 2012, he reported for the Wall Street Journal, Reuters and The Toledo (Ohio) Blade.
speakers.tagLine: Investigative Reporter, The New York Times

speakers.bio: Steve Sapienza is Senior Editor for U.S. News Partnerships at the Pulitzer Center. An award-winning journalist and documentary producer, he has reported across dozens of countries on issues including HIV/AIDS, climate change, conflict, and human rights. His work has appeared on PBS NewsHour, The New York Times, NPR, Al Jazeera, and The Financial Times. A National News & Documentary Emmy winner, he leads the Pulitzer Center’s StoryReach U.S. fellowship, helping journalists combine investigative reporting with innovative audience engagement and collaborative storytelling.

Steve Staeger is a consumer investigative reporter based in Denver, Colorado, with 18 years of experience in television journalism. Known for rigorous, impactful storytelling that holds powerful institutions accountable, his work spans high-stakes deep dives into police misconduct, exorbitant medical billing practices, and systemic consumer issues. To date, Steve’s consumer investigations have successfully returned more than $4 million to Colorado residents. Over the course of his career, his reporting has been recognized with 19 Emmy Awards and an Edward R. Murrow Award. He frequently speaks on the intersections of media verification, public records, and consumer advocacy.

Steve Warren is a retired Army Colonel who last served as the senior uniformed Pentagon spokesman. As a civilian, he served as a Senior Executive in the Department of the Army and as a communications executive in the aerospace & defense industry. Formerly with CNN, he is currently an NBC News military analyst. 

During his 25-year Army career, Steve rose to the rank of Colonel and served in some of the most senior public affairs and media leadership positions in the Department of Defense. As Director of Defense Press Operations, he led the U.S. government’s largest press office, managing comms across the Pentagon and interagency while conducting hundreds of press conferences/media engagements. From 2015-2016, he deployed to Baghdad as Coalition Spokesperson for Operation Inherent Resolve, overseeing communications for the 67-nation coalition to defeat ISIS.

A combat veteran, Steve served as an Infantry Officer in Iraq, Afghanistan, Bosnia, Kosovo, at NATO Headquarters, and in Korea.
speakers.tagLine: Former Pentagon spokesman/Principal Deputy Chief of Army Public Affairs

speakers.bio: Sukey Lewis is an investigative reporter covering policing, prisons and government accountability. She created and hosted the investigative podcast On Our Watch, that examines misconduct and secrecy inside law enforcement agencies and California prisons. Her work has received multiple honors including last year’s IRE award for Best Longform Journalism in Audio.
