Susan Batt is an investigative and special projects producer at WTHR (NBC Indianapolis), where she collaborates with the award-winning 13 Investigates team to produce in-depth reporting across the station’s broadcast, streaming, and digital platforms.  That work has helped change laws, hold the powerful accountable, and protect the community. She is part of teams honored with a number of awards including two Peabody Awards in the same year as well as a duPont-Columbia University silver baton. Susan is a graduate of Wheaton College (IL) and holds a master’s degree from the University of Missouri.
speakers.tagLine: Investigative & Special Projects Producer - WTHR Indianapolis

speakers.bio: Susan Ferriss is a senior editor at the Pulitzer Center, where she supports journalists worldwide as they pursue projects, including stories on global health. As a staff reporter at the Center for Public Integrity, Susan produced multiple prize-winning investigations One project exposed wage theft affecting immigrants and guest workers. Another exposed hidden COVID-19 deaths of immigrant food-production workers, Another exposed unjust suspensions and criminalization of young students at schools. Susan's stories on excessive use of police at schools led to new state laws in Virginia and multiple local reforms in California and other states. As a foreign correspondent, Susan’s reporting on failed promises of economic reforms in Mexico won an Overseas Press Club award. She also produced a documentary, The Golden Cage, about farmworkers and co-wrote a book, The Fight in the Fields, about the impact of U.S. farmworker leader Cesar Chavez.
speakers.tagLine: Pulitzer Center on Crisis Reporting, Senior Editor

speakers.bio: Emmy Award Winning Consumer Investigative Reporter, NBC WASHINGTON and host of "Susan Makes Sense" a consumer-focused multi-platform show airing on YouTube and the NBC4 streaming channel.

Susan Merriam is a journalist specializing in graphics, data reporting, and interactive storytelling. At McClatchy, she contributed to investigative projects across the Miami Herald, The Kansas City Star, and The Charlotte Observer. She was recently part of a Miami Herald/WLRN investigation named a Pulitzer Prize finalist in Local Reporting. Her work has also been recognized by the National Press Foundation, Investigative Reporters & Editors, and the Society for News Design.

Susanne Reber is the founder and executive producer of Piz Gloria Productions specializing in serialized narrative storytelling and multilingual productions. She is also working with a kick ass team developing a new incubator and rolling fund to help ambitious narrative stories get off the ground + find global audiences. Susanne is an experienced editorial leader with a proven track record as an ambitious creator, executive editor and show runner. She’s created legacy audio platforms such as Reveal, now in its tenth year as a weekly radio show and podcast reaching millions every week and Verified a binge worthy and award winning serialized show for character driven narratives.

Susie Neilson is a investigative reporter at the San Francisco Chronicle. Her work has triggered changes to police pursuit policies; a five-state probe into insurance issues affecting wildfire victims; and a California-wide ban on a debunked lie detector technology used to interrogate prisoners.

In 2026, she won the Pulitzer Prize for Explanatory Reporting for “Burned,” a series examining how insurance company practices have failed California fire survivors. The previous year, she was a finalist for the Pulitzer Prize in National Reporting for “Fast and Fatal,” an investigation into deadly police chases. Other honors include the Hillman Prize, the Barlett and Steele Gold Award and two Investigative Reporters and Editors (IRE) Awards.
speakers.tagLine: Investigative Reporter, the San Francisco Chronicle

speakers.bio: I’m a national enterprise reporter for NBC News based in Washington, DC, focusing on federal agencies and their impact on ordinary people.

Tanya Eiserer is a senior investigative reporter at WFAA-TV in Dallas, where she covers accountability, government, criminal justice and public safety. Her reporting focuses on uncovering systemic failures, exposing wrongdoing and examining how public institutions affect people's lives.
Eiserer began her journalism career in newspapers and spent more than a decade as a criminal justice reporter for The Dallas Morning News before joining WFAA in 2014. She moved to the station's investigative team in 2018 and has led award-winning investigations into vulnerable adult abuse, transportation safety, government oversight and regulatory failures.
A graduate of the University of Houston, Eiserer's work has prompted legislative reforms, criminal investigations and policy changes across Texas. Among her honors are multiple Lone Star Emmy Awards, three Regional Edward R. Murrow Awards, an Investigative Reporters and Editors (IRE) Award, the Stephen Philbin Award and the Charles E. Green Award.

Tara Puckey, MBA, FASAE, CAE, CMP leads strategic initiatives that advance the future of journalism and strengthen the impact of news organizations across the country in her work with RTDNA. A champion of mission-driven leadership, Tara brings more than a decade of senior association management experience with a focus on leadership development, press freedom advocacy and organizational sustainability.

In 2018, she was named to Association Forum’s Forty Under 40 for her work with a variety of associations and involvement in state and national organizations. 

Tara is a frequent speaker on leadership wellbeing, values-based decision-making, and the evolving local news landscape. She has trained more than 3,000 journalists and nonprofit professionals in effective leadership practice.

She’s a proud graduate of Indiana University, aside from associations and journalism, she loves craft beer, great pens and true crime podcasts.
speakers.tagLine: President & CEO of Radio Television Digital News Association (RTDNA)

speakers.bio: Taylor Giorno covers lobbying and influence for NOTUS. She previously covered business and lobbying for The Hill and money in politics for OpenSecrets. An Investigative Reporters and Editors (IRE) member, Taylor has helped train fellow journalists to track dark money in elections and foreign influence operations.
speakers.tagLine: Reporter at NOTUS covering all things money in politics.

speakers.bio:
Tazbia Fatima is a Newsroom AI and Automation Engineer with Hearst Newspaper's DevHub, where she applies emerging AI technologies to local journalism, from newsroom automation and reporting tools to audience-facing products that make complex information more accessible.
At Hearst, Tazbia is part of the team behind Assembly, the award-winning AI-powered platform that monitors, transcribes, and summarizes public meetings for reporters, helping local newsrooms expand coverage of government and community affairs. She also contributes to Meeting Monitor, the reader-facing version of the platform, and has helped develop AI-powered service journalism products such as Tx Tax tool that empower audiences to navigate important civic and financial decisions. She has a Dual Master's in Journalism and Computer Science from Columbia University.
Her work has contributed to projects recognized by the Online Journalism Awards, Education Writers Association, and International News Media Association.

Ted is an Investigative Reporter at WRC in Washington, DC where he focuses on adding context and detail to what is a dizzying news pace here.
Before DC, Ted spent time in Houston, Norfolk, Roanoke & Iron Mountain, MI.
His three daughters frequently tell him how awesome it is to have a dad active on social media!
speakers.tagLine: Investigative Reporter, WRC TV NBC Washington

speakers.bio: Tenzin Pema is a journalist of Tibetan origin with over two decades of experience as a reporter, editor, and newsroom leader in global media firms. She was most recently a 2025-2026 Knight-Wallace Fellow at the University of Michigan, where she studied new tools and frameworks to show the full scale of authoritarian attempts to erase Tibetan identity. Prior to that, Tenzin was the Director of Radio Free Asia’s Tibetan Service, where she led critical and sensitive reporting on Tibet across multiple formats and platforms, including groundbreaking coverage of the Dege dam protests in 2024 that led to mass arrests. Previously, Tenzin was Editor of Strategic Initiatives at Economic Times and Editorial Head at YourStory Media. She spent a decade at Reuters, where she was an Editor-in-Charge of teams covering different regions. She holds a postgraduate diploma in Broadcast Journalism from the Asian College of Journalism in India.

Theo Francis has covered business for The Wall Street Journal for most of the last quarter-century. He mines securities filings and other public disclosures for insights into executive pay, corporate taxes, accounting strategies, corporate jets, pensions and employee benefits, business law, diversity practices, workforce trends, corporate governance, how public policy affects business strategy, how companies affect the economy — and other topics that won't help you much at social gatherings. He contributed to coverage that has been recognized with a Pulitzer Prize, Loeb awards and other prizes. Francis has also worked at or written for footnoted.com, Bloomberg News, BusinessWeek, DealBook, Planet Money's blog, the Arkansas Democrat-Gazette and other publications. He has taught reporting at the University of Maryland and data journalism at American University.
speakers.tagLine: Special writer, The Wall Street Journal

speakers.bio: Tibisay Zea is a journalist and public radio reporter with more than a decade of experience covering immigration, politics, human rights, climate and underrepresented communities. She is the Mexico correspondent for The World, a public radio program heard on NPR stations across the United States, and has reported from across Latin America, the Caribbean and beyond. Her work combines international and local reporting with rigorous, deeply sourced, human-centered storytelling. She is the host and co-producer of Salud, a bilingual podcast about immigrant health, and has collaborated on community-based journalism projects with Harvard and MIT. She reports in English and Spanish and is fluent in Portuguese.
speakers.tagLine: Latin America correspondent for The World radio program

speakers.bio: Tiffany Stanley is a reporter and editor on The Associated Press’ global religion team. She has written on religion, politics and policy for The Washington Post, The Atlantic, The New Republic, Politico and more. Her reporting on the long-term care industry was a finalist for a National Magazine Award for Public Interest and featured in The Best American Magazine Writing. A graduate of Harvard Divinity School and the University of Georgia, she lives in Washington, D.C., with her family.

Tim Spears is an Emmy Award-winning journalist for WISH-TV in Indianapolis, anchoring the weekend evening news and serving as an investigative reporter for I-Team 8.

He previously served as evening anchor and investigative reporter at ABC57 News in South Bend, Indiana. 

Spears has covered corruption in law enforcement, produced a one-hour special LGBTQ+ issues in Indiana, and regularly interviewed state and national lawmakers. Before moving to Indiana, he worked at Spectrum News 1 in Charlotte, winning an Emmy for “Carolina in Crisis,” a documentary about the opioid epidemic in North Carolina.

Starting in television at KOAM-TV in Pittsburg, KS, Spears won awards in Kansas and Missouri for his coverage of PTSD effects from the 2011 Joplin tornado.

Tina Rosenberg co-founded the Solutions Journalism Network.  Before that, she was a longtime New York Times writer. She's also written hundreds of magazine articles and three books.  She has won the Pulitzer Prize and National Book Award.

Tisha Thompson is an investigative reporter and senior writer at ESPN. She specializes in complex stories exploring the intersection of sports and power, including league and government investigations, high-profile civil and criminal cases, sports betting, fan and stadium security, sexual assault, athlete health and safety, ticketing and consumer issues, and the evolving field of sports banking.

Tracee Wilkins is an investigative reporter with more than 25 years in broadcast, 20 years at NBC Washington. Her investigations focus heavily on areas of law enforcement, incarceration, racial justice and government accountability. She’s an Edward R. Murrow, multiple Emmy, National NABJ, Dateline and AP award winner. Wilkins was the Journalist of the Year for the Washington Association of Black Journalists in 2022.

Trenton Daniel is a Senior Reporter in New York for Healthbeat, a nonprofit newsroom covering public health in the U.S. Previously, he spent almost 14 years at The Associated Press, most recently as a reporter on the global investigations team using public records to break news and bolster the AP report. Trenton started at AP as its Haiti correspondent, in 2011, writing about reconstruction efforts following the 2010 earthquake and a subsequent cholera outbreak. Before AP, he spent almost eight years as a staff writer at The Miami Herald and reported extensively from Haiti, Iraq and throughout the Caribbean. Trenton also covered his share of hurricanes, homicides and zoning meetings. He lives in Brooklyn.

Walter Smith Randolph is an Emmy and national Murrow award-winning investigative journalist and newsroom leader. He is currently the Executive Producer of Investigations at CBS News New York. He also serves as Vice President-Broadcast of the National Association of Black Journalists and teaches the next generation of journalists at CUNY’s Newmark Graduate School of Journalism and UC Berkeley’s Graduate School of Journalism.

Before returning home to New York City, Walter spent more than a decade working as a reporter and anchor around the country at stations in Elmira, NY (WENY); Flint, MI (WEYI); Kalamazoo, MI (WWMT), and Cincinnati, OH (WKRC). Walter also launched and led The Accountability Project, an award-winning investigative unit at Connecticut Public Broadcasting (CPTV& WNPR-FM).

Walter is a proud graduate of Villanova University and CUNY’s Newmark Graduate School of Journalism.
speakers.tagLine: Executive Producer of Investigations, CBS News New York

speakers.bio:
Wendy Fry is an Emmy-winning multimedia investigative journalist who reports on poverty and inequality for the California Divide team. Based in San Diego and Mexico, Wendy has been covering the California border region for more than 15 years and covers immigration, reparations and issues affecting San Diego-area families. 

She’s a board member of the San Diego chapter of the Society of Professional Journalists and has reported for the Watchdog team at the San Diego Union-Tribune from 2009 to 2012. 

For television, she worked as an on-air reporter, investigative producer and assignment editor at NBC San Diego from 2013 to 2018 — where she helped launch an investigative team and Telemundo20, the Spanish language news station — before returning to print journalism, covering Mexico and Baja California for the Union-Tribune and the Los Angeles Times from 2018 to 2022.

Wendy won SPJ’s Sol Price Award for Responsible Journalism in 2012 for uncovering corruption among construction contractors and elected officials at the Sweetwater Union High School District, resulting in indictments of about a dozen public officials. 

She also won the Grand Golden Watchdog Award from the San Diego County Taxpayers Association in 2017, for her coverage of dangerous levels of lead in water in the San Diego Unified and San Ysidro school districts. Her short documentary “Missing in Mexico,” about family members searching for the remains of missing loved ones, won a regional Emmy. 

A graduate of San Diego State, Wendy speaks English and Spanish.

Will Fitzgibbon is a senior reporter and the global partnership coordinator for The Examination.

Will previously worked as a reporter with the International Consortium of Investigative Journalists (ICIJ), where he played key roles in award-winning investigations, including the Panama Papers, Pandora Papers, FinCEN Files and Shadow Diplomats. As ICIJ’s Africa and Middle East partnerships coordinator, Will trained and led the largest pan-African investigations in recent history.

Ximena Bustillo is a multi-platform correspondent at NPR covering politics and policy in Washington related to DHS and immigration.

On air and in print, Bustillo has covered the full gamut of American politics at NPR: From award-winning stories on farm labor and agriculture politics and crisscrossing the country speaking to voters deciding the 2024 election, to leading the network's coverage of President Trump's criminal trial in New York City. Her stories, which can be centered in places like the echoey halls of Congress or the sandy sidewalks of Arizona alike, aim to show readers the connection between the policies in the Beltway and their daily lives. 

She is a graduate of Boise State University.

Ximena Natera is an award-winning photojournalist, educator and strategist based in Oakland, CA. Formerly a Report for America corps member and CatchLight fellow at Berkeleyside, she championed visual reporting and worked with editors to integrate a visual strategy. Her photographic work is guided by themes of resilience and collective memory. Her focus on expansive safety practices for journalists stems from her experience reporting in Mexico during a time of extreme violence against the press, and she was part of the first IWMF Next Gen Safety Trainers Fellowship in 2021. She is a graduate of the International Center of Photography and a member of NAHJ and Women Photograph.

Yamiche Alcindor is a White House Correspondent for NBC News, covering all aspects of the Trump administration. Her reporting appears across all NBC News platforms including Today Show, NBC Nightly News and Meet the Press as well as on NBC News NOW.

She previously served as a Washington correspondent for NBC News, covering former President Biden’s administration and the presidential campaign of former Vice President Kamala Harris as well as the impact of federal policies on communities across the country and issues at the intersection of race, culture and politics. 

She was previously the moderator for Washington Week, the Peabody Award-winning weekly news analysis series on PBS, and the White House Correspondent for PBS NewsHour. While at PBS, she co-moderated a presidential primary debate in 2019 featuring several candidates running to be the Democratic nominee. 

She also covered the impact of the coronavirus pandemic including the virus' disproportionate impact on Black people and communities of color. In addition, she has covered a range of issues including the effects of federal policies and rhetoric on vulnerable populations domestically and internationally, protests and police killings such as the murder of George Floyd, as well as the consequences of federal immigration policies.

Previously, Yamiche also worked as a national political reporter for The New York Times and a national breaking news reporter for USA Today.

Yamiche is the recipient of numerous awards including a Peabody Award for her coverage of the January 6th attack on the U.S. Capitol, the White House Correspondents' Association's Aldo Beckman Award for Overall Excellence in White House Coverage, the Radio Television Digital News Association's John F. Hogan Distinguished Service Award, and the International Women's Media Foundation's Gwen Ifill Award. She is also a member of the National Association of Black Journalists (NABJ) and was named the organization's 2020 Journalist of the Year.

Yamiche earned a master's degree in broadcast news and documentary filmmaking from New York University and a bachelor's in English, government and African American studies from Georgetown University. 

A native of Miami, Yamiche is married to a fellow journalist and is the daughter of Haitian immigrants who met while attending Boston College.

Follow Yamiche at @Yamiche.

Yami Virgin is an Emmy Award-winning investigative reporter and anchor for KABB 29 San Antonio with more than 25 years of experience covering immigration, border security, criminal justice, public corruption, and government accountability. Her investigations have exposed failures in public systems, uncovered hidden records, and sparked community and policy discussions across Texas.
A bilingual journalist, Yami hosts the Edward R. Murrow Award-winning Podcast Immigration Crisis: The Fight for the Southern Border.  She focuses on immigration, law enforcement accountability, mass casualty events including Robb Elementary, and years of coverage along the U.S.-Mexico border.
Yami holds a BA in Political Science, a Master's degree in Digital Audience Strategy and Analytics, and is a Doctoral Candidate in Organizational Leadership and Higher Education at St. Edward's University. She works with journalism fellows through the RTDNA and the RIAS Berlin Commission.
speakers.tagLine: KABB WOAI San Antonio, Investigative Reporter/Anchor

speakers.bio:
Yesica Prado is an investigative reporter and researcher for El Tecolote in San Francisco, focusing on issues of access to land and belonging, such as homelessness, immigration and Indigenous status. She is a first-generation Mexican immigrant from Nezahualcóyotl, Mexico. Prado holds a BFA in photography from the University of Illinois at Chicago and a master’s in journalism from UC Berkeley Graduate School of Journalism.

As a photojournalist, Prado turns her lens to the silent moments of everyday life, capturing our shared humanity. She invites viewers to look past “a situation,” and discover a new way of living: coexisting together. Prado hopes her work can help change prejudices about poor communities. Erase borders. Unite People.

I've covered government and politics for more than a dozen years. I was part of the team that was a Pulitzer Prize finalist for reporting on the flawed police response to the mass shooting in Uvalde, Texas. That series, which was made possible by a trove of police records I obtained from a confidential source, also won the Collier Prize.
My reporting at the Houston Chronicle revealed that deaths from the 2021 Texas blackouts were far higher than the government initially reported and how the failure of concert staff and police to heed warning signs led to the deaths of 10 fans in the Astroworld Festival disaster.
My investigation into how Texas diverted Hurricane Harvey aid away from high risk-areas for storms led to a federal investigation that concluded the state discriminated against minority residents. I also reported on firefighters being denied workers’ compensation benefits for cancer likely caused by on-the-job exposure to chemicals, which spurred lawmakers to expand coverage.

Zack Newman is a freelance investigative data journalist based in Denver. Newman's award-winning piece have been published by publications of all sizes in the States and New Zealand. They include the Colorado Sun, BusinessDesk, Denver Gazette, Colorado Public Radio, 9NEWS, MSNBC and more.

His work spurred changes in laws and policies, exposed a human trafficking ring and inspired the American Civil Liberties Union to sue Denver Police. Newman's accolades include a Murrow, Sigma Delta Chi and an Emmy.

Zhe Wu is a reporter at San Francisco Public Press covering the Asian American community, with a focus on health equity, housing, transportation, and politics. She reports in Cantonese, Mandarin, and English. Zhe Wu joined the Public Press in 2023 as part of the inaugural cohort of the California Local News Fellowship, a state-funded initiative to strengthen local journalism in underserved communities across California. In 2025, she was named Outstanding Emerging Journalist by the Society of Professional Journalists, Northern California Chapter.
speakers.tagLine: Reporter, The San Francisco Public Press
